generation 3

inputs
✦ claude designer · scheduled round · 3 design(s) injected, 2 rejected pre-flight
elites carried over unchanged2
claude-designed3
crossovers (tournament parents)9
mutants (single parent)2
mutation σ 0.164 of gene range
crossover 90% · immigrants 10%
tournament k=3 · elitism 2 · patience 3 gen
✦ claude designer · generation 3 · scheduled round · claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

what claude was asked

You are the designer in an automated evolutionary study of 3D-printable quadcopter frames. The geometry is the real TBS Source One V6 7-inch DeadCat, morphed by a genome; the mission is 2 km north + 2 km south at 12 m/s through six adverse-weather scenarios; fitness = mean Wh/km across scenarios + 0.5 x worst scenario (LOWER IS BETTER). The platform (battery, 2806 motors, 7x4 props, electronics) is fixed; only the frame genome varies.

GENES (name: min..max — meaning):
- arm_length_scale: 0.75..1.35 — arm shaft stretch (wheelbase / disk loading)
- arm_width_scale: 0.75..1.4 — arm width at shaft ends (stiffness vs drag)
- arm_waist_scale: 0.55..1.3 — extra mid-shaft narrowing (drag vs stiffness)
- arm_thickness: 0.004..0.009 — arm plate thickness in meters
- front_sweep_deg: 30.0..52.0 — front arm azimuth from nose
- rear_sweep_deg: 34.0..62.0 — rear arm azimuth from tail
- plate_length_scale: 0.85..1.3 — deck plate stretch along flight axis
- plate_width_scale: 0.85..1.25 — deck plate stretch across
- deck_gap: 0.02..0.045 — standoff length in meters (stack space, prop-deck gap)
- battery_wedge_deg: 0.0..15.0 — battery tilt on the top plate (frontal area)
- plate_thickness_scale: 0.7..1.6 — x2 mm deck plates (mass vs stiffness)
- material: 0.0..0.999 — 0-0.17 carbon plate, 0.17-0.33 PA12-CF, 0.33-0.5 PET-CF, 0.5-0.67 PLA+, 0.67-0.83 PETG, 0.83-1 ASA

BEST-PER-GENERATION TRAJECTORY (Wh/km-agg): g0 6.886, g1 6.807, g2 6.552

CURRENT ELITES (best first):
fitness 6.552 Wh/km-agg, frame 116 g, cf_plate: genes={"arm_length_scale": 1.1874, "arm_width_scale": 0.8442, "arm_waist_scale": 0.55, "arm_thickness": 0.0045, "front_sweep_deg": 31.4716, "rear_sweep_deg": 35.6322, "plate_length_scale": 1.067, "plate_width_scale": 1.0617, "deck_gap": 0.0302, "battery_wedge_deg": 0.0, "plate_thickness_scale": 1.0643, "material": 0.0716} scenarios={"calm_warm": 3.44, "cold_headwind": 4.18, "crosswind": 4.03, "gusty_light": 3.86, "hot_thin": 3.68, "storm": 5.03}
fitness 6.719 Wh/km-agg, frame 114 g, pa12_cf: genes={"arm_length_scale": 1.0948, "arm_width_scale": 0.7946, "arm_waist_scale": 0.8092, "arm_thickness": 0.0069, "front_sweep_deg": 34.3743, "rear_sweep_deg": 39.6449, "plate_length_scale": 1.066, "plate_width_scale": 1.0, "deck_gap": 0.0312, "battery_wedge_deg": 10.4674, "plate_thickness_scale": 1.0, "material": 0.288} scenarios={"calm_warm": 3.5, "cold_headwind": 4.35, "crosswind": 4.15, "gusty_light": 3.95, "hot_thin": 3.73, "storm": 5.16}
fitness 6.790 Wh/km-agg, frame 130 g, cf_plate: genes={"arm_length_scale": 1.2802, "arm_width_scale": 0.8799, "arm_waist_scale": 0.6032, "arm_thickness": 0.006, "front_sweep_deg": 31.6322, "rear_sweep_deg": 36.431, "plate_length_scale": 0.9426, "plate_width_scale": 0.9959, "deck_gap": 0.0299, "battery_wedge_deg": 0.0, "plate_thickness_scale": 0.7085, "material": 0.0} scenarios={"calm_warm": 3.55, "cold_headwind": 4.36, "crosswind": 4.18, "gusty_light": 3.99, "hot_thin": 3.79, "storm": 5.21}
fitness 6.807 Wh/km-agg, frame 132 g, cf_plate: genes={"arm_length_scale": 1.35, "arm_width_scale": 0.8577, "arm_waist_scale": 0.5917, "arm_thickness": 0.006, "front_sweep_deg": 31.5277, "rear_sweep_deg": 36.3405, "plate_length_scale": 0.9431, "plate_width_scale": 0.996, "deck_gap": 0.0299, "battery_wedge_deg": 0.0368, "plate_thickness_scale": 0.7, "material": 0.0069} scenarios={"calm_warm": 3.56, "cold_headwind": 4.37, "crosswind": 4.2, "gusty_light": 4.0, "hot_thin": 3.8, "storm": 5.23}
fitness 6.828 Wh/km-agg, frame 137 g, cf_plate: genes={"arm_length_scale": 1.1874, "arm_width_scale": 0.8442, "arm_waist_scale": 0.55, "arm_thickness": 0.006, "front_sweep_deg": 31.4716, "rear_sweep_deg": 35.6322, "plate_length_scale": 1.0498, "plate_width_scale": 0.9971, "deck_gap": 0.0302, "battery_wedge_deg": 0.0, "plate_thickness_scale": 1.0643, "material": 0.0716} scenarios={"calm_warm": 3.59, "cold_headwind": 4.36, "crosswind": 4.21, "gusty_light": 4.02, "hot_thin": 3.83, "storm": 5.24}

YOUR EARLIER PROPOSALS AND HOW THEY FARED:
- g0 `59b9a52c` fitness 7.127 Wh/km-agg — was: Compact, wide, stiff-armed storm-hardened design with a modest sweep shift and generous deck_gap/plate scale margin to clear the prior rotor-to-deck failure.
- g0 `7792e425` fitness 6.886 Wh/km-agg — was: Long low-disk-loading arms at exact stock sweep with a safely-oversized plate footprint and carbon construction, targeting hot_thin efficiency without touching the plate-web failure zone.
- g0 `89ffb906` fitness 8.479 Wh/km-agg — was: Storm-hardened stiff bruiser: wide thick arms, long wheelbase and thick carbon plates trade mass for structural robustness, targeting the storm scenario that dominates worst-case fitness.
- g0 `9be55419` fitness 7.598 Wh/km-agg — was: Heavy-duty ASA build with thick plates and wide arms near stock sweep for maximum stiffness/durability robustness in storm gusts, accepting a mass penalty.
- g0 `9f8cb660` fitness 6.908 Wh/km-agg — was: Narrow low-drag arms with a tilted battery to cut frontal area for cold_headwind/crosswind, keeping sweep change modest and deck_gap raised for safety margin.

RECENT INVALID-DESIGN REASONS (histogram): {"plate web too thin (plate_main 0.32 mm, stock 1.90 mm)": 1, "deck gap too small for FC/ESC stack": 3, "plate web too thin (plate_main 0.66 mm, stock 1.90 mm)": 1, "plate web too thin (plate_main 1.06 mm, stock 1.90 mm)": 1, "arm tongue bolts miss the main plate": 5, "plate web too thin (plate_main 1.48 mm, stock 1.90 mm)": 1, "plate web too thin (plate_main 0.59 mm, stock 1.90 mm)": 1, "rotor too close to deck/battery": 1, "arm resonance with rotor 1P": 1, "plate web too thin (plate_top 1.25 mm, stock 1.75 mm)": 1, "rotor disks overlap (tip clearance)": 3, "plate web too thin (plate_mid 1.11 mm, stock 1.47 mm)": 1, "plate web too thin (plate_main 0.84 mm, stock 1.90 mm)": 1, "arm root tongues collide on the main plate": 1, "plate web too thin (plate_main 0.78 mm, stock 1.90 mm)": 1, "plates too thin for pet_cf (1.5 < 1.6 mm)": 1, "plate web too thin (plate_main 0.85 mm, stock 1.90 mm)": 1}


SCENARIO NOTES: storm (8 m/s wind, severe gusts, rain) dominates worst-case; cold_headwind rewards low frontal drag; hot_thin (thin air) rewards low mass; crosswind rewards a small side profile.

Propose exactly 3 NEW genome vectors that are meaningfully DIFFERENT from the elites and from each other — design hypotheses, not small perturbations. Build on what the trajectory and your earlier proposals' outcomes show worked; avoid repeating what failed. Respect the hard constraints implied by the failure histogram. Known couplings: the deck gap must exceed 0.023 m for the FC stack; arm tongues collide when both sweeps sit at their minimums with wide arms; the tongue BOLTS must stay on the main plate -- sweeps far from stock (front 31.4, rear 36.0) need plate_length_scale/plate_width_scale to grow with them (rule of thumb: keep sweeps within ~6 deg of stock unless you also raise the plate scales by ~0.1 per extra 5 deg); the FC-stack holes stay PINNED while the plates scale, so plate scales below ~0.95 crush the material webs between holes and cutouts (min 80% of the stock web is enforced); and printed materials (anything but cf_plate) need plate_thickness_scale >= 0.8 (>= 1.6 mm plates).

Respond with a JSON object of the shape:
{"proposals": [{"rationale": "<one sentence>", "genes": {"arm_length_scale": <float>, "arm_width_scale": <float>, "arm_waist_scale": <float>, "arm_thickness": <float>, "front_sweep_deg": <float>, "rear_sweep_deg": <float>, "plate_length_scale": <float>, "plate_width_scale": <float>, "deck_gap": <float>, "battery_wedge_deg": <float>, "plate_thickness_scale": <float>, "material": <float>}}, ...]}

what it proposed

  • candidate edbeb491 — Moderate-stiffness storm specialist: wider/thicker arms with less waist-narrowing and tough PA12-CF material plus generous deck_gap, betting that structural robustness (not raw mass-adding bulk like earlier failed 'storm-hardened' attempts) trims the worst-case storm penalty.flew at 6.977 Wh/km
  • candidate a76a6566 — Wider front/rear sweep for a broader stance to help crosswind/storm stability, paired with generously enlarged (not merely stock) plate scales and deck_gap to safely clear the tongue-bolt and web-crush failures seen at extreme sweep.flew at 6.705 Wh/km
  • candidate 4ac62300 — Near-stock sweep (small deltas, clear of the tongue-collision minimum) with plate scales pushed well above the 0.95 web-crush threshold and a thicker printed PLA+ build to test a tougher, mass-tolerant alternative to carbon.flew at 7.026 Wh/km
  • candidate 738d04e5 — rejected pre-flight (arm tongue bolts miss the main plate) — Sweep-diverged crosswind hypothesis: push rotor azimuth well past stock (front 40, rear 48) with plate scales raised ~0.15 per the coupling rule to protect the FC-stack webs, testing an unexplored rotor-placement regime for crosswind/storm gains.
  • candidate 03f8842c — rejected pre-flight (plate web too thin (plate_main 1.43 mm, stock 1.90 mm)) — Ultra-light short-wheelbase design: shrink arm length and deck_gap to their safe minimums with stock sweeps (avoiding the plate-web/tongue failure zone) and thinner plates, betting mass reduction beats the elites' 114-116 g on hot_thin.

generation 0

inputs
✦ claude designer · opening round — gen-0 design hypotheses · 5 design(s) injected, 4 rejected pre-flight
seeds (baseline + random probes)11
claude-designed5
mutation σ 0.180 of gene range
crossover 90% · immigrants 10%
tournament k=3 · elitism 2 · patience 3 gen
✦ claude designer · generation 0 · opening round — gen-0 design hypotheses · claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

what claude was asked

You are the designer in an automated evolutionary study of 3D-printable quadcopter frames. The geometry is the real TBS Source One V6 7-inch DeadCat, morphed by a genome; the mission is 2 km north + 2 km south at 12 m/s through six adverse-weather scenarios; fitness = mean Wh/km across scenarios + 0.5 x worst scenario (LOWER IS BETTER). The platform (battery, 2806 motors, 7x4 props, electronics) is fixed; only the frame genome varies.

GENES (name: min..max — meaning):
- arm_length_scale: 0.75..1.35 — arm shaft stretch (wheelbase / disk loading)
- arm_width_scale: 0.75..1.4 — arm width at shaft ends (stiffness vs drag)
- arm_waist_scale: 0.55..1.3 — extra mid-shaft narrowing (drag vs stiffness)
- arm_thickness: 0.004..0.009 — arm plate thickness in meters
- front_sweep_deg: 30.0..52.0 — front arm azimuth from nose
- rear_sweep_deg: 34.0..62.0 — rear arm azimuth from tail
- plate_length_scale: 0.85..1.3 — deck plate stretch along flight axis
- plate_width_scale: 0.85..1.25 — deck plate stretch across
- deck_gap: 0.02..0.045 — standoff length in meters (stack space, prop-deck gap)
- battery_wedge_deg: 0.0..15.0 — battery tilt on the top plate (frontal area)
- plate_thickness_scale: 0.7..1.6 — x2 mm deck plates (mass vs stiffness)
- material: 0.0..0.999 — 0-0.17 carbon plate, 0.17-0.33 PA12-CF, 0.33-0.5 PET-CF, 0.5-0.67 PLA+, 0.67-0.83 PETG, 0.83-1 ASA

BEST-PER-GENERATION TRAJECTORY (Wh/km-agg): (no generations evaluated yet)

CURRENT ELITES (best first):
(none yet -- this is generation 0: propose diverse OPENING design hypotheses that stake out different regions of the genome space)

YOUR EARLIER PROPOSALS AND HOW THEY FARED:
(none yet)

RECENT INVALID-DESIGN REASONS (histogram): {}


SCENARIO NOTES: storm (8 m/s wind, severe gusts, rain) dominates worst-case; cold_headwind rewards low frontal drag; hot_thin (thin air) rewards low mass; crosswind rewards a small side profile.

Propose exactly 5 NEW genome vectors that are meaningfully DIFFERENT from the elites and from each other — design hypotheses, not small perturbations. Build on what the trajectory and your earlier proposals' outcomes show worked; avoid repeating what failed. Respect the hard constraints implied by the failure histogram. Known couplings: the deck gap must exceed 0.023 m for the FC stack; arm tongues collide when both sweeps sit at their minimums with wide arms; the tongue BOLTS must stay on the main plate -- sweeps far from stock (front 31.4, rear 36.0) need plate_length_scale/plate_width_scale to grow with them (rule of thumb: keep sweeps within ~6 deg of stock unless you also raise the plate scales by ~0.1 per extra 5 deg); the FC-stack holes stay PINNED while the plates scale, so plate scales below ~0.95 crush the material webs between holes and cutouts (min 80% of the stock web is enforced); and printed materials (anything but cf_plate) need plate_thickness_scale >= 0.8 (>= 1.6 mm plates).

Respond with a JSON object of the shape:
{"proposals": [{"rationale": "<one sentence>", "genes": {"arm_length_scale": <float>, "arm_width_scale": <float>, "arm_waist_scale": <float>, "arm_thickness": <float>, "front_sweep_deg": <float>, "rear_sweep_deg": <float>, "plate_length_scale": <float>, "plate_width_scale": <float>, "deck_gap": <float>, "battery_wedge_deg": <float>, "plate_thickness_scale": <float>, "material": <float>}}, ...]}

what it proposed

  • candidate 89ffb906 — Storm-hardened stiff bruiser: wide thick arms, long wheelbase and thick carbon plates trade mass for structural robustness, targeting the storm scenario that dominates worst-case fitness.flew at 8.479 Wh/km
  • candidate 7792e425 — Long low-disk-loading arms at exact stock sweep with a safely-oversized plate footprint and carbon construction, targeting hot_thin efficiency without touching the plate-web failure zone.flew at 6.886 Wh/km
  • candidate 59b9a52c — Compact, wide, stiff-armed storm-hardened design with a modest sweep shift and generous deck_gap/plate scale margin to clear the prior rotor-to-deck failure.flew at 7.127 Wh/km
  • candidate 9f8cb660 — Narrow low-drag arms with a tilted battery to cut frontal area for cold_headwind/crosswind, keeping sweep change modest and deck_gap raised for safety margin.flew at 6.908 Wh/km
  • candidate 9be55419 — Heavy-duty ASA build with thick plates and wide arms near stock sweep for maximum stiffness/durability robustness in storm gusts, accepting a mass penalty.flew at 7.598 Wh/km
  • candidate 0769309e — rejected pre-flight (plate web too thin (plate_main 1.25 mm, stock 1.90 mm)) — Low-drag speed racer: long slender waisted arms and minimal-frontal-area carbon-plate build at stock sweeps to hold bolt registration while chasing the lowest steady-cruise Wh/km.
  • candidate cc82d367 — rejected pre-flight (plate web too thin (plate_main 1.07 mm, stock 1.90 mm)) — Ultralight hot/thin-air specialist: short thin arms and PLA+ at the minimum viable plate thickness to cut absolute mass for low-density-air scenarios.
  • candidate afda73b2 — rejected pre-flight (rotor too close to deck/battery) — Wide-sweep compact X hypothesis: pushes both sweeps ~6-7 deg past stock for a tighter crosswind silhouette, growing the deck plates to protect the tongue bolts, printed in tough PA12-CF.
  • candidate a642e36d — rejected pre-flight (plate web too thin (plate_main 1.07 mm, stock 1.90 mm)) — Cold-headwind glider: near-minimum sweeps (kept just off the exact minimum to dodge tongue collision) with narrow waisted arms and a steep battery wedge to minimize frontal area into a headwind, printed PETG.

metricvalue
energy score6.257 Wh/km
scenario mean3.857 Wh/km
worst scenario4.801 Wh/km
frame mass90.7 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 15 via pivot
hypothesisParent was already essentially at the front (6.286), so this pivot makes a small, careful bet: trim the arm waist and length just a bit further to shed the last bit of unneeded mass.
methodTightened arm waist slightly, shortened arm length a touch, nudged arm width up marginally, and made a tiny material blend adjustment.
result6.257 — a NEW WINNER, edging past the previous best-so-far of 6.274. Every scenario improved a little, including storm dropping to 4.80, suggesting this lands right at a mass/stiffness sweet spot rather than winning on any single scenario. Worth exploring rear sweep near 36° next to see if it compounds with this gain.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.274 Wh/km
scenario mean3.867 Wh/km
worst scenario4.815 Wh/km
frame mass91.6 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 11 via mutation
hypothesisSame champion parent again, but instead of touching front sweep (already tuned), the bet was that rear-arm sweep was the next untapped lever, paired with the same plate-thinning trick that had been working.
methodIncreased rear sweep by about 3 degrees, thinned the plate stock (0.75x to 0.70x), left the material blend essentially at the parent's value, and stretched arm length marginally.
resultFitness hit 6.274, the best result of the generation and a NEW WINNER, beating not just its parent (6.291) but also sibling ba61fc4b63fb's 6.286. Storm score matched ba61fc4b63fb almost exactly, but small gains stacked across calm, cold, crosswind, gusty, and hot scenarios pushed it ahead, suggesting the rear-sweep tweak has broader benefit than the front-sweep one. Worth testing next: combine this rear-sweep change with ba61fc4b63fb's arm-waist change to see if the two mass-saving moves stack further.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.289 Wh/km
scenario mean3.875 Wh/km
worst scenario4.827 Wh/km
frame mass92.5 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 20 via crossover
hypothesisThis crossover paired the near-best 6.274 genome with the weaker 6.588 one, betting that only picking up a tiny width/sweep nudge from the weaker parent would keep performance close to the stronger one.
methodVery slightly widened the plate and increased front sweep a touch; arm thickness was left unchanged between parents.
resultFitness came in at 6.289, very close to the 6.274 parent and just short of the 6.257 best-so-far — not a new winner, but it confirms this region of the genome is a strong, stable local optimum. The tiny width/sweep tweaks were nearly neutral. Worth testing: nudge front sweep back down toward the champion's own value to see if it closes the remaining gap.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.313 Wh/km
scenario mean3.890 Wh/km
worst scenario4.846 Wh/km
frame mass93.8 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 20 via pivot
hypothesisThis pivot came directly off the reigning champion (54a73703249e, 6.257), betting that small refinements — slightly wider arms and a purer carbon-fiber blend — could squeeze out an incremental improvement.
methodWidened the arms a little, nudged rear sweep up marginally, shifted the material blend closer to pure carbon fiber, and thickened the arm waist slightly.
resultFitness landed at 6.313, slightly worse than the 6.257 champion — did not dethrone it. The extra arm width and waist mass likely outweighed any benefit from the purer material, nudging the storm scenario up to 4.85. Worth testing: apply the material shift alone, without widening the arms, to isolate whether purer carbon fiber helps on its own.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.323 Wh/km
scenario mean3.896 Wh/km
worst scenario4.854 Wh/km
frame mass94.3 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 20 via crossover
hypothesisThis crossover blended the same 6.298 performer with the 6.274 near-best genome, betting that a wider deck gap and reduced rear sweep together would improve rotor clearance and efficiency.
methodWidened the deck gap by several millimeters, reduced rear sweep, lengthened the arms slightly, and nudged front sweep up marginally.
resultFitness landed at 6.323, worse than both parents and off the 6.257 best-so-far — the blend underperformed either input alone. The wider deck gap likely added structural mass without a proportional efficiency return. Worth testing: apply the deck-gap widening alone against the 6.274 parent to confirm it's the culprit before combining it with other changes.

metricvalue
energy score6.339 Wh/km
scenario mean3.905 Wh/km
worst scenario4.866 Wh/km
frame mass94.4 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 20 via crossover
hypothesisThis crossover combined two near-best performers (6.298 and the 6.257 champion), betting that blending their plate-length and arm-length traits could produce something better than either parent.
methodGrew the plate length toward full stock scale, lengthened the arms slightly, and nudged rear sweep up marginally.
resultFitness came in at 6.339, worse than both parents and off the 6.257 best-so-far — the cross did not combine favorably. The slightly longer arms and plate appear to have added drag or mass without a matching benefit, reflected in a higher storm figure (4.87). Worth testing: repeat the cross but hold arm length fixed at the better parent's value, varying only plate length.

materialpa12_cf
borngeneration 20 via designer
hypothesisA designer-proposed clean-slate design testing the pa12_cf material family — instead of the cf_plate dominating the population — with arm geometry close to the population's converged sweep angles, to see if a different material could open a new efficiency path.
methodBuilt from scratch using pa12_cf material with moderate arm thickness and waist, sweep angles near the population's converged values (33°/37°), standard deck gap, and stock-scale plate thickness.
resultFitness came in at 6.514 — better than the other two designer entries this generation but still well off the 6.257 best-so-far. The material swap alone didn't overcome the extra mass pa12_cf's thicker-wall requirement imposes. Worth testing: graft the population's converged sweep and plate-length values onto a pa12_cf frame to give the material a fairer, more competitive genetic context.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 20 via pivot
hypothesisFrom the same 6.431 parent, this sibling pivot bet on the opposite lever — more front sweep instead of rear — paired with a longer plate, to see if front-loaded sweep would handle crosswind and gust scenarios better.
methodIncreased front sweep substantially, lengthened the main plate, lengthened the arms slightly, and trimmed rear sweep a little.
resultFitness came in at 6.589, worse than the 6.431 parent and far off the 6.257 best-so-far — not a winner. The extra front sweep and longer plate likely added drag and mass that outweighed any stability gain, with storm and cold-headwind scenarios both elevated. Worth testing: isolate the front-sweep increase without the plate lengthening to see which change is actually costing efficiency.

borngeneration 20 via designer
hypothesisA designer-proposed clean-slate design testing whether repositioning the battery mass via a steep wedge tilt, combined with slim long arms and a wide plate, could improve weight distribution independent of the arm-geometry lineage dominating the population.
methodBuilt from scratch with long, slim arms, a pronounced 46° rear sweep, a 20%-wider plate, and a steep 15° battery wedge tilt not seen elsewhere in this generation.
resultFitness came in at 6.619, well off the 6.257 best-so-far and middling among the designer entries this generation. With no direct lineage to compare against, the result sits closer to the weaker cluster of frames, suggesting the steep battery tilt didn't pay off. Worth testing: graft the battery wedge tilt alone onto the current champion's other genes to isolate its effect cleanly.

materialcf_plate
borngeneration 20 via pivot
hypothesisThe parent was already trailing the leading cluster at 6.588, so this pivot bet that pushing sweep angles further and thickening the arm waist would trade a little mass for better aerodynamic and structural balance.
methodIncreased front sweep further, thickened the arm waist, lengthened the arms slightly, and trimmed rear sweep marginally.
resultFitness landed at 6.678, worse than the 6.588 parent and well off the 6.257 best-so-far — not a new winner. The added waist mass and extra sweep likely cost more in drag and weight than they returned in stiffness, especially visible in the storm scenario (5.13). Worth testing: vary front sweep and arm waist independently rather than together, to see which one is actually hurting.

energy score8.018 Wh/km
scenario mean4.932 Wh/km
worst scenario6.171 Wh/km
frame mass221.1 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 20 via designer
hypothesisA designer-proposed clean-slate design testing an intentionally over-built, heavy-duty frame — thick arms, thick waist, wide deck gap — betting that extra structural margin might unlock better storm/high-wind performance even at a weight cost.
methodBuilt from scratch with much thicker and wider arms (7.5mm thick, 30% wider, 20% thicker waist), a 40mm deck gap, and plates 35% thicker than stock — no lineage, everything pushed toward heavy and stiff.
resultFitness came in at 8.018, far worse than the 6.257 best-so-far and the worst result in this generation — the mass penalty of over-building dominated every scenario, including storm (6.17). This confirms the population is well past the point where added structural margin helps; efficiency gains now come from trimming mass, not adding it. Worth testing: if stiffness is still worth exploring, target one dimension at a time (e.g. waist only) rather than compounding thickness across arm, waist, and plate simultaneously.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
statusinvalid — rotor too close to deck/battery
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass86.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 20 via pivot
hypothesisThe parent sat right at the current best-so-far (6.257), so this pivot bet that shrinking the arms and pushing the material blend fully to carbon fiber could shave more weight without giving up stiffness.
methodShortened the arms substantially (from 27% over stock to just 7% over), shifted the material blend entirely to pure carbon fiber, trimmed rear sweep slightly, and thickened the plates a touch.
resultInvalid: shortening the arms brought the rotor disc too close to the deck/battery stack, a hard clearance violation. The lesson is that arm length can't be cut in isolation once deck gap and battery placement are tuned to a longer arm. Worth testing: repeat the same material/arm-length bet but widen the deck gap or drop the battery wedge to preserve rotor clearance.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

statusinvalid — plates too thin for pa12_cf (1.4 < 1.6 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass83.5 g
materialpa12_cf
borngeneration 20 via pivot
hypothesisThe parent's fitness hadn't resolved (null), so this pivot explored a very different structural region — much wider arms and a tighter deck gap — to see if a stiffer, more compact frame in the pa12_cf material family could carve out a new niche.
methodWidened the arms substantially, reduced front sweep, tightened the deck gap by several millimeters, and thinned the plates slightly.
resultInvalid: the thinner plates fell below the minimum wall gauge pa12_cf requires (1.4mm vs a 1.6mm floor). The lesson is that pa12_cf's thickness floor is stricter than cf_plate's, so thickness cuts that are safe in carbon fiber can break the mold-strength constraint here. Worth testing: keep plate thickness at or above the pa12_cf floor while still exploring the wider-arm, tighter-deck-gap idea.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass103.3 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 20 via pivot
hypothesisWith the parent stuck at 6.431, this pivot bet that a much sharper rear sweep (more V-tail-like) plus a slight battery tilt could cut rear-facing drag and improve weight balance.
methodPushed rear sweep up sharply, added a small battery wedge tilt, and trimmed front sweep slightly.
resultInvalid: the sharper rear sweep caused the arm root tongues to collide on the main plate, a geometric packing failure. This shows large sweep swings need matching changes to arm root width or position to keep the tongues clear. Worth testing: pair a big rear-sweep increase with a narrower arm root or repositioned tongue to avoid the collision.

frame mass93.6 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 20 via pivot
hypothesisThe parent was near the best-so-far at 6.274, so this pivot bet that shortening the main plate while lengthening the arms could trim mass without compromising stiffness.
methodShortened the plate length noticeably, increased plate thickness slightly, reduced front sweep, and lengthened the arms further.
resultInvalid: shortening the plate thinned the mid-plate web below the required minimum (1.11mm vs 1.47mm needed), even with the higher thickness multiplier — the length cut dominated the web geometry. This shows plate-length and plate-thickness interact in a way that a simple thickness bump can't fully compensate for. Worth testing: check mid-web thickness directly when shrinking plate length, or apply a larger thickness increase to offset it.

borngeneration 20 via crossover
hypothesisThis self-crossover of the 6.298 performer bet that thicker plates plus a purer carbon-fiber blend could add stiffness margin and push past the current leader.
methodSubstantially thickened the plates, trimmed front sweep slightly, and shifted the material blend to pure carbon fiber.
resultInvalid: the thicker plate geometry caused the arm root tongues to collide on the main plate — the same failure mode seen in c90d90d7c88c. This repeated failure shows plate thickness above roughly 0.85x needs matching arm-root spacing to avoid tongue collisions. Worth testing: cap plate thickness increases below that threshold, or widen arm root spacing whenever thickness is pushed higher.

borngeneration 19 via crossover
hypothesisCrossover between two near-best performers, 1704aea48de9 (6.274) and 52ab8e14cb63 (6.31); bet was that blending their deck gap and material traits could land between or beat both.
methodDeck gap opened (26.0→29.5mm), material nudged marginally toward cf_plate blend (0.00→0.01), arm width effectively unchanged.
result6.298 — lands between the two parents but doesn't beat best-so-far (6.257), coming within about 0.04 of it. This suggests a deck gap near 29–30mm is a mild lever worth isolating on the champion lineage itself. Worth testing: apply just the deck-gap increase directly to reigning champion 54a73703249e and see if it pushes past 6.257.

borngeneration 19 via crossover
hypothesisSelf-crossover of strong performer 1704aea48de9 (6.274), exploring nearby mutations to see if minor thickening/widening could round out its edges.
methodArm thickness increased (4.0→4.6mm), plate slightly lengthened (×0.97→×1.01), arm width widened (×0.76→×0.81), deck gap opened (26.0→27.2mm).
result6.431 — worse than the parent's 6.274 and off best-so-far. The added arm and plate mass outweighed any structural benefit, consistent with this generation's pattern that thickening past the champion lineage's lean profile costs efficiency. Worth testing: the deck-gap increase in isolation, since deck-gap changes haven't clearly hurt elsewhere this generation.

metricvalue
energy score6.496 Wh/km
scenario mean4.002 Wh/km
worst scenario4.989 Wh/km
frame mass107.2 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 19 via pivot
hypothesisParent f6397fd6043c (6.487) had plateaued with a front-heavy sweep and a fairly thin plate; the bet was that rebalancing sweep front-to-rear while nudging plate thickness up a touch could trim drag without tripping the web-thickness constraint.
methodRear sweep opened slightly (47.1°→48.4°), front sweep trimmed a hair (44.0°→43.8°), plate thickness nudged up slightly (×0.71→×0.75), plate width essentially unchanged.
result6.496 — essentially flat with, and marginally worse than, the parent's 6.487; not a new winner against the 6.257 best-so-far. The small thickness increase likely added just enough mass to cancel out any drag benefit from the sweep rebalance. Worth testing: the same sweep tweak but with thickness held flat or reduced, to see if the sweep benefit shows through without the added mass.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

scenario mean4.060 Wh/km
worst scenario5.057 Wh/km
frame mass108.4 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 19 via designer
hypothesisClaude-designer candidate testing a lean-arm, high-sweep, large-plate hypothesis with a large forward battery wedge, betting that shifting CG forward would help headwind/crosswind efficiency.
methodHand-designed genome: moderate arm length (×1.15) with a lean section (4.5mm, ×0.75 width, ×0.55 waist), steep sweeps (44°/50°), enlarged plate (×1.28/×1.24), thin plate (×0.70), and a large 13.0° battery wedge.
result6.588 — valid but well off best-so-far (6.257) and behind most pivots this generation, worst in storm (5.06) and cold headwind (4.24). The steep sweep angles and oversized plate likely added drag/mass the wedge's CG benefit didn't offset. Worth testing: keep the lean arm profile (close to the champion's) but dial the wedge back to 4–5° and shrink the plate toward the champion's dimensions to isolate whether the wedge alone helps.

metricvalue
energy score6.618 Wh/km
scenario mean4.077 Wh/km
worst scenario5.082 Wh/km
frame mass118.8 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 19 via pivot
hypothesisParent a8cec93bcca6 (6.546) ran a short plate with a thick section and tight waist; bet was that lengthening the plate while relaxing the waist and thinning the plate could trade mass for structural margin.
methodPlate lengthened (×0.96→×1.16), arm waist relaxed (×0.55→×0.75), plate thickness reduced (×1.24→×1.06), deck gap tightened (40.7→36.4mm).
result6.618 — worse than the parent (6.546) and well off best-so-far. The longer plate and looser waist likely added more mass/drag than the thinner section saved. Worth testing: the plate-length increase in isolation, holding waist and deck gap at the parent's values, to see if length alone is the culprit.

worst scenario5.280 Wh/km
frame mass133.1 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 19 via pivot
hypothesisSame parent f6397fd6043c (6.487); bet was that beefing up arm thickness and committing fully to pure cf_plate (dropping the material blend) would buy stiffness headroom worth its weight.
methodArm thickness increased substantially (4.0→5.6mm), arm width widened (×0.75→×0.81), plate width trimmed (×1.23→×1.19), material shifted fully to pure cf_plate.
result6.874 — clearly worse than the parent (6.487) and best-so-far. The heavier, thicker arms cost more in every scenario than the material purity or narrower plate saved, most visible in storm (5.28) and crosswind (4.22). Worth testing: the material-purity shift alone, without the arm thickness increase, to isolate whether mass or material choice drove the regression.

energy score7.577 Wh/km
scenario mean4.664 Wh/km
worst scenario5.825 Wh/km
frame mass178.6 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 19 via immigrant
hypothesisA second random immigrant, probing a heavy, over-built corner of the design space to test whether robustness through mass could still be competitive.
methodFresh random genome: long arms (×1.27) with a very thick section (7.2mm), moderate waist (×0.71), aggressive sweeps (49.5°/44.2°), large plate (×1.29/×1.21), wide deck gap (35.9mm), battery wedge 5.3°, cf_plate.
result7.577 — valid but far worse than best-so-far (6.257) and near the bottom of the generation, worst in storm (5.82) and crosswind (4.64). The thick arms and oversized plate add mass that the efficiency metric punishes across every scenario, confirming over-built robustness isn't free in Wh/km terms. Worth testing: keep the aggressive sweep angles but pair them with much leaner, shorter arms closer to the champion's profile.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

statusinvalid — plate web too thin (plate_mid 1.11 mm, stock 1.47 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass92.1 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 19 via pivot
hypothesisParent 54a73703249e is the reigning best-so-far (6.257); bet was that shortening the plate and pushing sweep asymmetry further (more rear, less front) could shave mass and drag off the champion.
methodPlate shortened (×0.97→×0.85), rear sweep opened (36.2°→41.1°), front sweep pulled in (33.5°→30.0°), arm width widened slightly (×0.77→×0.86).
resultInvalid — the shortened plate failed the web-thickness check (1.11mm vs 1.47mm stock minimum) without a compensating thickness increase. This shows the champion's plate length is already close to the minimum safe margin at its current thickness. Worth testing: the same sweep changes with plate length left near the champion's value, or shortened only alongside a proportional thickness increase.

hypothesisParent d8d770d5f10a was itself unevaluated/invalid; the bet was that shortening the plate while thickening it and adding a small battery wedge might fix whatever made the parent non-viable.
methodPlate shortened (×1.00→×0.85), plate thickness increased (×1.00→×1.14), front sweep opened slightly (31.4°→33.4°), small battery wedge added (0→0.9°), material asa.
resultInvalid — plate web still too thin (1.12mm vs 1.47mm stock); the length cut outpaced the thickness gain, the same failure mode as fd0496c33092. Lesson: shrinking plate length is unsafe unless thickness scales up more aggressively than it did here. Worth testing: skip the length cut for this lineage and test the wedge/sweep changes on the parent's original plate length.

energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass86.8 g
materialpet_cf
borngeneration 19 via pivot
hypothesisParent c47e1f6da0e0 was unevaluated; bet was that shifting the material blend toward pet_cf and thickening the plate could unlock a lighter, still-stiff configuration.
methodMaterial blend shifted toward pet_cf (0.02→0.40), plate thickness increased (×0.70→×1.05), rear sweep tightened (38.2°→35.2°), arm thickness trimmed (4.5→4.0mm).
resultInvalid — plate_top too thin (1.30mm vs 1.75mm stock). The pet_cf shift raises the required minimum web thickness well above what a cf_plate design would need, so the same relative thickening fell short. Lesson: material choice changes the safety margin needed, not just the stiffness/weight tradeoff. Worth testing: keep the pet_cf shift but push plate thickness closer to ×1.3–1.4 to clear the material-specific minimum.

borngeneration 19 via pivot
hypothesisSame parent c47e1f6da0e0; bet was that a large plate-thickness jump plus a forward battery wedge would give enough structural margin while improving CG balance.
methodPlate thickness increased substantially (×0.70→×1.25), battery wedge added (0→3.9°), plate width nudged (×0.95→×0.98), arm length nudged (×1.31→×1.33).
resultInvalid — plate_top still too thin (1.39mm vs 1.75mm stock) despite the big thickness increase, even with cf_plate material. This suggests the battery wedge itself may be locally carving out web material at the plate top independent of the thickness gene. Worth testing: the same thickness increase without the battery wedge, to see if removing the wedge alone clears the constraint.

statusinvalid — arm root tongues collide on the main plate
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass95.9 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 19 via crossover
hypothesisParents were the reigning best-so-far 54a73703249e (6.257) and an unevaluated f66bf5675cbb; bet was that blending in wider arms and a lower rear sweep from the second parent could nudge the champion further.
methodArm width widened (×0.77→×0.85), plate thickness increased slightly (×0.70→×0.77), rear sweep reduced (36.2°→34.0°), front sweep raised marginally (33.5°→34.2°).
resultInvalid — arm root tongues collide on the main plate, a geometric interference distinct from this generation's web-thickness failures. The wider arm combined with the closer front/rear sweep angles left no clearance at the root. Worth testing: keep the small sweep adjustments but revert arm width to the champion's value to check whether sweep alone is viable without the collision.

statusinvalid — arm tip deflection (pet_cf)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass133.5 g
materialpet_cf
borngeneration 19 via immigrant
hypothesisRandom immigrant meant to inject diversity into a population converging around the 6.25–6.5 band; explores a larger, higher-sweep design point on pet_cf material.
methodFresh random genome: moderate arm scaling (length ×1.20, width ×1.03, waist ×1.16, thickness 4.7mm), aggressive sweeps (49.6°/41.1°), enlarged thick plate (×1.27/×1.13, ×1.22 thickness), battery wedge 4.0°, pet_cf material.
resultInvalid — arm tip deflection specific to pet_cf, meaning this material is too compliant for the chosen arm length/thickness. Confirms pet_cf needs shorter or thicker arms than cf_plate to pass deflection checks. Worth testing: rerun this same geometry with cf_plate, or with meaningfully thicker arms, to see if the underlying design point has merit once the material mismatch is fixed.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — arm resonance with rotor 1P
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass234.5 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 19 via designer
hypothesisClaude-designer candidate testing a deliberately over-built, high-stiffness hypothesis — thick, wide arms and a thick plate — to see if maximal structural margin could open room for bolder sweep/deck-gap choices elsewhere.
methodHand-designed genome: arm length ×1.10, width ×1.35 (much wider), waist ×1.25 (looser), thickness 8.0mm (heaviest arm section this generation), plate thickness ×1.50, deck gap 38.0mm, moderate sweeps (34°/37°).
resultInvalid — arm resonance with rotor 1P, a dynamic constraint rather than a static one. The thick, wide, stiff arm shifted the natural frequency into the rotor's excitation band. Lesson for the designer: maximizing section stiffness isn't safe by itself — arm geometry must also dodge the rotor's 1P frequency. Worth testing: keep the arm width/waist but trim thickness or extend length to detune the arm away from 1P.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

energy score6.310 Wh/km
scenario mean3.888 Wh/km
worst scenario4.844 Wh/km
frame mass93.6 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 18 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing a near-best performer (6.296) with the reigning champion (6.257), the bet is that combining their genes could produce a new best.
methodWidened the plate slightly, tightened the deck gap by about 1.7 mm, trimmed front sweep marginally, and kept arm thickness unchanged.
result6.31 -- lands between the two parents but does not beat the champion's 6.257, so no new winner. Storm remains the worst scenario at 4.84 Wh/km, suggesting the plate widening or deck-gap tightening cost a small margin versus the champion baseline. Next idea: test the deck-gap tightening alone against the champion to isolate its effect.

frame mass105.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 18 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing a weaker high-sweep parent (6.475) with the champion (6.257), the bet is that the champion's genes would pull the aggressive-sweep design toward better efficiency.
methodTrimmed rear sweep by about 3 degrees, nudged front sweep up slightly, and left plate dimensions essentially unchanged from the high-sweep parent.
result6.487 -- essentially matches the weaker parent, not the champion, showing the extreme 44/47-degree sweep angles dominate the outcome regardless of crossover with a low-sweep champion. Confirms high sweep is a losing direction this generation. Next idea: stop breeding from high-sweep (>40 degree) lineages and focus offspring on the champion's ~30-33 degree sweep range.

frame mass111.8 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 18 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing two mid-tier parents (6.504 and 6.384), the bet is that combining their differing strengths -- plus an untested battery tilt -- could beat either alone.
methodBlended genes from both parents, lengthened the arms further, added a 2.3-degree battery wedge, and made small deck-gap and rear-sweep tweaks.
result6.546 -- worse than both parents, not a new winner. The crossover failed to combine advantages additively; storm remained the weak scenario at 5.02 Wh/km. Next idea: test the battery wedge tilt in isolation on the stronger parent to see if it's what dragged performance down.

materialcf_plate
borngeneration 18 via designer
hypothesisA hand-designed candidate deliberately exploring a divergent concept from the low-sweep champion lineage: compact waisted arms, steep sweep, a large plate footprint, and a notable battery tilt.
methodShorter, narrower, more waisted arms than the champion, steep 43/52-degree front/rear sweep, an oversized plate (25% longer, 25% wider), and an 8-degree battery wedge.
result6.646 -- well behind the 6.257 best-so-far and behind most valid crossovers, reinforcing (alongside f6397fd6043c) that steep sweep angles hurt efficiency across scenarios, especially storm at 5.10 Wh/km. Next idea: retry this compact-plate concept but with sweep angles pulled back into the champion's 30-33 degree range.

energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass137.5 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 18 via pivot
hypothesisThe parent lineage never got evaluated, so this pivot is a blind bet: stiffen the arm dramatically while shrinking the plate footprint to see if a beefier, tighter frame pays off.
methodNearly doubled arm thickness to 7.0 mm, narrowed the plate width slightly, thickened the plate multiplier, and pulled the deck gap in by about 5.5 mm.
resultInvalid: the main plate web collapsed to just 0.21 mm against the 1.90 mm stock minimum. Despite the higher plate-thickness multiplier, shrinking plate width while ballooning arm thickness ate all the web material at the arm root. Worth testing next: repeat the thicker-arm bet but hold plate width at parity so the web isn't starved.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 18 via pivot
hypothesisStarting from a near-best parent (6.274), this pivot bets that a much more compact frame -- shorter arms, tighter deck gap -- would cut drag further.
methodShrank deck gap from 26 mm to 20 mm, shortened arms, and widened both the plate and the arms slightly.
resultInvalid: 20 mm deck gap can't physically fit the FC/ESC stack. A hard packaging floor, not an aero tradeoff. Next idea: retry the arm-shortening/widening bet but keep deck gap at or above ~26 mm.

borngeneration 18 via pivot
hypothesisPivoting directly off the reigning best-so-far frame, this candidate bets that a longer plate and wider deck gap would improve stack clearance and stability without giving up the champion's efficiency.
methodLengthened the plate by about 27%, widened the deck gap by roughly 9.5 mm, reduced front sweep to 30 degrees, and nudged arm thickness up slightly.
resultInvalid: arm root tongues collide on the main plate. Growing the plate while cutting front sweep to 30 degrees pushed the tongue geometry into overlap. Next idea: test the plate-length increase alone, holding front sweep at the champion's ~33.5 degrees.

hypothesisWith the parent trailing the best-so-far by over a tenth of a point, this pivot bets that flattening front sweep and widening the arms would recover some of that gap.
methodCut front sweep to 30 degrees, lengthened the plate slightly, widened the arms, and thickened the plate marginally.
resultInvalid: same arm-root tongue collision as the sibling pivot from the champion lineage. This is the second candidate this generation to fail exactly at 30-degree front sweep, suggesting that angle is a real geometric floor for this plate/arm combination. Next idea: test front sweep reduction alone (no plate-length change) to isolate whether sweep or plate growth is the real culprit.

hypothesisAlso branching from the current champion, this candidate bets that heavy structural reinforcement -- thicker plate and arms -- plus a small battery tilt for CG tuning could squeeze out more robustness.

frame mass132.2 g
materialpetg
borngeneration 18 via immigrant
hypothesisA fresh random immigrant, meant to inject diversity and probe a far corner of the design space -- steep sweep angles, a large battery tilt, and PETG instead of carbon fiber -- in case the population is plateauing around the champion's design.
methodFully random genome: long/waisted/thick arms, steep 49/48-degree front/rear sweep, oversized plate length, large 13.3-degree battery wedge, and PETG material.
resultInvalid: plate web too thin at 0.51 mm against the 1.90 mm minimum. The aggressive plate dimensions combined with the softer PETG material left no structural margin. Next idea: constrain immigrant sampling so plate web thickness scales appropriately when PETG or other softer materials are drawn.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — arm tip deflection (asa)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass100.8 g
materialasa
borngeneration 18 via designer
hypothesisA hand-designed candidate testing whether swapping to ASA material, with baseline plate dimensions and champion-adjacent sweep angles, could unlock a lighter or more robust structure.
methodSwitched material to ASA, thickened the arm to 6.0 mm, widened and increased arm waist moderately, and kept plate dimensions at baseline (1.0x) with sweep close to the champion's values.
resultInvalid: arm tip deflection failure. ASA's lower stiffness relative to the carbon-fiber plate couldn't support the arm length/thickness combination under load even with the thicker 6 mm arm. Next idea: retry ASA with either a shorter arm length or a further-increased arm thickness (7 mm+) to pass the deflection check.

energy score6.296 Wh/km
scenario mean3.879 Wh/km
worst scenario4.834 Wh/km
frame mass92.7 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 17 via pivot
hypothesisThe parent was a mid-pack performer; the bet was that slimming the arms a touch further and steepening the rear sweep would cut drag without pushing any dimension past its structural margin.
methodArm width narrowed slightly further, rear sweep steepened by about 2 degrees, plate length nudged up a hair, arm thickness left unchanged.
resultFitness improved to 6.296 from the parent's 6.322, but stayed short of the 6.2571847613660045 best-so-far — not a new winner. Storm remains the worst scenario at 4.83, suggesting the frame is still paying a mass or stiffness penalty in the roughest condition. Worth testing next: hold this arm width and sweep combo but attack storm robustness directly, e.g. via a small arm-thickness bump instead of further slimming.

borngeneration 17 via crossover
hypothesisA crossover bet that blending a decent performer's wider arm waist into the current champion's geometry might combine the best of both.
methodArm waist widened toward the non-champion parent's value, material shifted toward a more mixed blend, deck gap nudged slightly.
resultFitness came in at 6.384, worse than both parents (6.337 and 6.257) and off the best-so-far — the wider waist blend added drag or mass without a matching payoff, a negative interaction rather than synergy. Not a new winner. Next: test the arm-waist widening in isolation off the champion alone to see if it's the waist change itself or the crossover combination that hurts.

borngeneration 17 via designer
hypothesisA designer candidate that kept the champion's arm dimensions but deliberately pushed much wider sweep angles and a larger plate footprint, betting the extra sweep would pay off despite the added plate mass.
methodArm length, width, waist and thickness matched the champion; front sweep and rear sweep both steepened substantially, and plate length and width both enlarged well past stock.
resultValid at 6.475, but well off the 6.2571847613660045 best-so-far, with storm scenario (4.97) again the heaviest penalty — the larger plate footprint likely added more mass/drag than the wider sweep angles recovered. Next: keep the champion's compact plate and test the wide sweep angles alone.

frame mass110.5 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 17 via designer
hypothesisA second designer candidate that isolated plate thickness as the single variable, keeping everything else matched to the champion, to test whether extra stiffness pays for itself.
methodArm dimensions, sweep angles, plate length/width and deck gap all matched the champion; plate thickness multiplier nearly doubled.
resultValid at 6.504, but worse than the 6.257 champion despite matching its geometry almost exactly — the added plate mass from the thickness increase wasn't offset by any robustness gain. This confirms the champion's leaner plate thickness is closer to optimal than heavier alternatives. Next: if testing stiffness again, compensate elsewhere in the mass budget (e.g. thinner arms) rather than a blanket plate-thickness increase.

borngeneration 17 via mutation
hypothesisSame weak-performing parent as several pivots this generation; the bet was a small nudge toward the cf_plate mainline geometry to see if it closes the gap to the leaders.
methodArm waist widened slightly, plate length brought to full stock, arm length trimmed a touch, material shifted fully to cf_plate.
resultFitness came in at 6.647, essentially flat with the parent's 6.63 and well off the 6.2571847613660045 best-so-far. Storm scenario (5.11) is again the worst offender, suggesting this whole parent lineage carries a structural drag or mass penalty that small mutations can't fix. Next: abandon this lineage in favor of pivots off the stronger champion family.

materialpla_plus
borngeneration 17 via designer
hypothesisA third designer candidate testing a more conservative build philosophy — thicker arms, wider waist, shorter length, PLA-plus material — as a 'sturdier but heavier' contrast to the lean cf_plate champion.
methodArm length shortened, arm width and waist both widened, arm thickness increased substantially, material switched to pla_plus, sweep/plate/deck-gap left matched to the champion.
resultValid but the weakest of the three designer candidates at 6.769, far from the 6.2571847613660045 best-so-far, with storm scenario (5.2) again the worst offender. The heavier PLA-plus, thick-arm approach clearly costs more energy than it buys in robustness. Next: abandon the thick-arm/PLA direction for this frame — the lean cf_plate family is dominating every scenario tested this generation.

frame mass90.9 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 17 via pivot
hypothesisThe parent needed to shed plate mass and improve weathercocking behavior in crosswinds; the bet was a shorter plate paired with a much sharper rear sweep.
methodPlate length cut substantially, rear sweep increased by nearly 6 degrees, with small nudges to arm waist and width.
resultInvalid — the plate web thinned to 1.11 mm against a 1.47 mm stock minimum, so the plate-length cut was too aggressive for this lineage's thickness setting. This shows plate length and plate thickness are tightly coupled here and can't be moved independently by this much. Next: retry the same rear-sweep steepening but keep plate length within a few percent of the parent's value.

frame mass176.8 g
materialpla_plus
borngeneration 17 via pivot
hypothesisThe parent was a heavy, thick-armed underperformer; the bet was that slimming the arms and shortening the plate could recover most of that lost efficiency.
methodArm width reduced substantially, arm waist widened, plate length shortened, rear sweep flattened slightly.
resultInvalid — plate web dropped to 1.02 mm against a 1.90 mm stock requirement. Because the parent already carried thick arms and a heavier plate-thickness multiplier, cutting plate length on top of that combination blew through the safety margin faster than expected. Lesson: candidates descended from this heavier lineage can't tolerate the same plate-length cuts the lean cf_plate mainline can. Next: keep plate thickness fixed and vary only sweep angles when working from this lineage.

statusinvalid — arm root tongues collide on the main plate
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass115.9 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 17 via pivot
hypothesisThe parent was lagging the leaders; the bet was that a shallower front sweep and longer arms would improve airflow while trimming the waist pinch.
methodFront sweep flattened slightly, arm waist trimmed, arm length stretched, battery wedge tilted a small amount.
resultInvalid — the arm root tongues collide on the main plate, a mounting-geometry failure from stretching the arms while flattening the sweep. This reveals arm length and front sweep are coupled at the mount interface: lengthening arms needs either a steeper sweep or more tongue clearance to stay clear. Next: retest the longer arm length while holding front sweep at or above its parent value.

borngeneration 17 via pivot
hypothesisThe parent never flew at all, so this was a repair attempt — opening the deck gap and tilting the battery wedge to fix whatever had killed it, while keeping the narrow-arm family.
methodBattery wedge angle increased sharply, deck gap widened, arm width increased slightly, plate length brought back up near full stock.
resultStill invalid, but for a different reason than the parent — plate web thinned to 1.45 mm against a 1.90 mm requirement. The repair fixed one constraint but exposed another: this lineage's low plate-thickness multiplier can't support near-stock plate length. Next: raise plate thickness before touching plate length or width again in this lineage.

methodDeck gap widened, plate width trimmed slightly, material shifted fully to cf_plate, arm width increased a touch.

borngeneration 17 via pivot
hypothesisDescended from the reigning best-so-far, this pivot bet on toughening the arms and tightening the frame — thicker arms, shorter deck gap, flatter front sweep, longer arms — to push past the plateau.
methodArm thickness nearly doubled, deck gap shrunk considerably, front sweep flattened, arm length stretched further.
resultInvalid — the deck gap shrank too far for the FC/ESC stack to physically fit. A costly lesson coming from the champion lineage: deck gap has a hard practical floor that can't be traded away even for stiffness gains. Next: keep deck gap near or above the champion's value and test the thicker-arm idea in isolation.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

statusinvalid — arm tongue bolts miss the main plate
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass91.5 g
materialpetg
borngeneration 17 via immigrant
hypothesisA pure random immigrant injected to keep diversity in a population that has plateaued around the current best-so-far; no parent performance to react to.
methodEntirely fresh genome — wider arms, much steeper front and rear sweep angles, wide deck gap, tilted battery wedge, thick plate, petg material.
resultInvalid — the arm tongue bolts miss the main plate, a basic mounting failure typical of unconstrained sampling at extreme sweep angles. This confirms the population's tight clustering around 30-something-degree sweep angles isn't just historical drift but a real geometric ceiling. Next: bias immigrant sampling to keep sweep angles under roughly 45 degrees so bolt alignment holds more often.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 17 via mutation
hypothesisDescended from the current best-so-far, this mutation bet on a small refinement — steepening front sweep and trimming plate length to shave a bit more drag off an already-strong design.
methodFront sweep increased, plate length shortened slightly.
resultInvalid — plate web thinned to 1.34 mm against a 1.75 mm requirement. Even a modest length cut on the champion's already-lean plate crosses the structural floor, showing the champion's plate geometry has little slack left. Next: try the front-sweep steepening alone, without touching plate length.

borngeneration 16 via pivot
hypothesisSame champion parent as candidate c61da0591e9a; this pivot tests a gentler version of the deck-gap-widening idea paired with a wider arm to see if a moderate step pays off where the aggressive one didn't.
methodDeck gap widened to 33.7mm from 26.1mm (a smaller jump than the sibling pivot), arm width increased to 0.81x from 0.77x, plate width trimmed slightly, front sweep pulled in a touch.
resultFitness landed at 6.322 — worse than the 6.257 champion but the best result among this generation's valid candidates. It confirms deck-gap widening is directionally costly even at a smaller step, and the extra arm width didn't offset it. Worth trying the champion's original deck gap with just the wider arm in isolation.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 16 via pivot
hypothesisParent 54a73703249e sits at the current best-so-far; this pivot bets that opening up the deck gap and adding a slight battery wedge could redistribute weight for a further gain without touching the arm/plate footprint much.
methodDeck gap widened from 26.1mm to 40.1mm, battery tilted to 0.9° from flat, arm length trimmed marginally, rear sweep pulled in slightly to 35.6°.
resultFitness rose to 6.337, worse than the 6.257 parent and no threat to best-so-far. Storm (4.86) drove most of the penalty, suggesting the wider deck gap added frontal area/weight without any aero payoff. Worth testing a smaller deck-gap increase (e.g. +5mm) in isolation to see if any of this move helps.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 16 via pivot
hypothesisParent 7d6bb6555ba3 (6.36) was already off the frontier; this pivot swaps sweep bias between front and rear to test whether reversing which end sweeps more helps.
methodRear sweep reduced to 34.0° from 38.0°, front sweep increased to 37.3° from 36.2°, arm width and deck gap nudged marginally.
resultFitness came in at 6.367, essentially flat vs the 6.36 parent and well off the 6.257 best-so-far. Swapping sweep bias didn't move the needle, suggesting this parent's sweep angles aren't the limiting factor. Next worth trying a bigger structural change off this lineage rather than more sweep tweaks.

borngeneration 16 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing two mid-pack parents (567629068d98 at 6.397, 1704aea48de9 at 6.274) bets that blending their sweep and material genes finds a combination better than either alone.
methodMaterial blended toward cf_plate, front sweep pulled back to 47.4° from 49.2°, arm width widened to 0.88x, battery wedge increased slightly to 1.4°.
resultFitness landed at 6.418, worse than both parents and well off the 6.257 best-so-far. The blend didn't inherit either side's better traits — a front sweep near 47° looks too aggressive for this arm/plate combination. Worth trying a crossover that keeps sweep closer to the stronger parent's value instead of averaging.

borngeneration 16 via pivot
hypothesisParent fb429755f2c9 (6.309) had headroom; this pivot bets that a strong battery wedge plus a wider, thicker plate could rebalance the airframe for a gain.
methodBattery wedge tilted sharply to 4.7° from flat, plate width widened to 1.08x, plate thickness increased to 0.86x from 0.70x, arm length extended to 1.35x.
resultFitness worsened sharply to 6.475 from the 6.309 parent, with every scenario getting worse, especially storm (4.97). The aggressive battery tilt combined with a much heavier plate likely added drag and mass with no offsetting benefit. Next worth isolating the battery wedge alone at a smaller angle to see if it's the wedge or the plate thickening that hurt more.

borngeneration 16 via designer
hypothesisA designer-authored probe testing whether deliberately thickening the arm waist well beyond the champion lineage's slender profile buys enough stiffness margin to justify its added weight.
methodHand-set to a champion-like footprint (arm length 1.30x, sweep near 32°/37°, deck gap 26mm) but with arm waist fattened to 1.10x, roughly double the champion's typical value, and thickness at 4.5mm.
resultFitness came in at 6.63, notably worse than the 6.257 best-so-far and worse than most pivots this generation. The fat waist added mass across every scenario without a compensating aero or structural win, suggesting the champion's slender waist is already close to optimal for this material/thickness combo. Next worth testing an intermediate waist (~0.7-0.8x) to find where the mass penalty starts.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

materialcf_plate
borngeneration 16 via designer
hypothesisA designer probe testing whether a much larger plate footprint, for potential payload/battery growth, combined with aggressive rear sweep could still be competitive on efficiency.
methodHand-set with plate length and width both scaled up substantially (1.22x/1.25x) versus the champion's near-stock plates, rear sweep pushed to 46°, moderate arm thickness at 4.5mm.
resultFitness came in at 6.641, worse than the 6.257 best-so-far and similar in magnitude to a072202cd3c9's shortfall. The oversized plates added drag/weight across every scenario without a compensating benefit, reinforcing that the champion's near-stock plate footprint sits closer to the efficient frontier. Next worth testing a smaller plate upsize (~1.05-1.10x) to find where footprint growth stops being a net loss.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 16 via immigrant
hypothesisA fresh random immigrant, meant to test whether an entirely different region of design space — heavier arms, wider plates, softer material — could out-compete the refined champion lineage.
methodArms scaled up across the board (length, width, waist all above 1x, thickness 8.3mm), plates enlarged and thickened to 1.35x, deck gap widened to 36.9mm, material switched to pla_plus.
resultFitness came in at 7.779, far worse than the 6.257 best-so-far and the worst valid result this generation. The oversized arms/plates plus a weaker, denser material piled on mass across every scenario, especially storm (5.99). This confirms the champion lineage's slimmer, cf_plate-based approach is well ahead of this design region — no need to explore further immigrants this heavy.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — plate web too thin (plate_main 1.45 mm, stock 1.90 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass88.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 16 via pivot
hypothesisParent ba61fc4b63fb (6.286) was solid but not leading; this pivot bets that narrowing the plate and tightening the deck gap trims mass for a free efficiency gain.
methodPlate width cut to 0.85x from 1.00x, deck gap tightened to 22.8mm from 26.0mm, rear sweep opened slightly, plate length trimmed marginally.
resultInvalid — the plate web thinned to 1.45mm against a 1.90mm stock minimum. Shrinking plate width while also tightening deck gap squeezed the load path below the structural floor. Lesson: plate width and deck gap can't both shrink together without also increasing plate thickness to compensate.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5
genome

metricvalue
statusinvalid — plates too thin for pa12_cf (1.4 < 1.6 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass70.4 g
materialpa12_cf
borngeneration 16 via pivot
hypothesisParent 1704aea48de9 (6.274) was near the frontier; this pivot bets that switching toward the lighter pa12_cf material could shave weight even with a slightly wider plate.
methodMaterial shifted toward pa12_cf, plate width increased to 1.09x, battery wedge added at 1.4°, plate length trimmed slightly.
resultInvalid — resulting plates came out at 1.4mm, below the 1.6mm minimum pa12_cf requires. The plate-thickness multiplier that was fine for cf_plate isn't enough once material switches to a weaker plastic. Lesson: material swaps to pa12_cf need a compensating plate-thickness bump, not just a width change.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
statusinvalid — arm tongue bolts miss the main plate
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass104.6 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 16 via pivot
hypothesisSame parent as b3aaaeaeec58; this pivot goes further, betting that a much fatter arm waist buys enough stiffness margin to allow a more aggressive rear-sweep shape.
methodArm waist thickened to 0.78x from 0.55x, rear sweep swept back hard to 45.2° from 38.0°, front sweep pulled forward to 31.4°, plate length trimmed slightly.
resultInvalid — the arm tongue's bolt holes ended up missing the main plate entirely. Stacking a big waist change with a large sweep swing moved the arm-to-plate mounting geometry out of alignment. Lesson: sweep and waist changes need to be tested independently before combining, since together they can break the bolt pattern.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

materialpa12_cf
borngeneration 16 via mutation
hypothesisSame champion parent as the two deck-gap pivots; this mutation randomly probes a wider, more sharply swept plate with a beefier material near the champion's structural envelope.
methodPlate width increased to 1.07x, rear sweep opened to 40.2° from 36.2°, battery wedge added at 1.9°, arm thickness bumped to 4.3mm, with material shifting toward pa12_cf.
resultInvalid — same failure mode as 3359f647701d: resulting plates measured 1.4mm against pa12_cf's 1.6mm minimum. A random mutation landed in the same material/thickness trap independently, reinforcing that pa12_cf pivots need a thickness floor the current gene ranges aren't respecting. Worth flagging pa12_cf moves to auto-bump plate thickness so this stops recurring.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — deck gap too small for FC/ESC stack
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass89.5 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 16 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing 210243eee823 (6.284) with bb51c78c03f1 (6.525) bets that taking the better parent's tighter dimensions while pulling sweep back could combine their strengths.
methodFront sweep pulled back to 45.8° from 48.7°, deck gap tightened to 21.0mm from 23.2mm, arm width and length trimmed marginally.
resultInvalid — the tightened deck gap left no room for the FC/ESC stack. Crossover recombination pulled the gap below the avionics clearance floor even though neither parent's change looked extreme individually. Lesson: deck gap needs a hard lower bound enforced during crossover, not just mutation/pivot.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — arm tip deflection (petg)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass122.6 g
materialpetg
borngeneration 16 via designer
hypothesisA designer probe testing whether petg — cheaper and more forgiving than cf_plate — could work if arms are beefed up enough to compensate for its lower stiffness.
methodHand-set with thick 6.5mm arms, 0.80x waist, and full-thickness (1.00x) plates, built entirely in petg instead of cf_plate.
resultInvalid — arm tip deflection exceeded limits even with the thickened geometry. This confirms petg's modulus is too low for this arm envelope regardless of how much material is added; matching cf_plate's stiffness would require disproportionate thickness that likely costs more in weight than switching material saves. Lesson: petg is probably not viable for this frame's arm design without a fundamentally different arm profile — worth deprioritizing further petg attempts.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

frame mass90.6 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 15 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing two solid ~6.3 performers on the bet that blending their front-sweep and arm-length traits could land closer to the frontier.
methodNarrowed the arm width, shortened arm length slightly, and kept front sweep and deck gap close to the parents' values.
result6.284 — better than both parents (6.324 and 6.373) and very close to the 6.257 best-so-far, though it doesn't overtake it. This reinforces that a slimmer arm width (~0.77) is a consistently winning trait this generation. Worth combining this candidate's front sweep with the champion's arm waist/length to try to beat 6.257 outright.

metricvalue
energy score6.286 Wh/km
scenario mean3.874 Wh/km
worst scenario4.824 Wh/km
frame mass92.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 11 via crossover
hypothesisThe reigning champion and the runner-up were both cf_plate designs sitting right at the current plateau; the bet was that blending their sweep, waist, and plate-thickness settings could shave a little more mass/drag without leaving the material family that was already working.
methodIncreased front-arm sweep by about 1.7 degrees, thinned the plate stock a bit further (from 0.75x to 0.70x of stock), let the arm waist taper a touch less aggressively, and nudged the material blend slightly while staying inside the cf_plate family.
resultFitness came in at 6.286, edging past both parents (6.291 and 6.308) to become a NEW WINNER at the moment it was evaluated. The thinner plates likely trimmed mass with no structural penalty, delivering small gains across every scenario. Worth testing next: push the plate-thickness multiplier even lower to find where the cf_plate margin actually breaks.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 15 via crossover
hypothesisTwo mid-6.3 performers were crossed on the bet that a sharper front sweep could trim aero losses further.
methodIncreased front sweep to 52° from 48.6°, narrowed the arm slightly, and left plate width unchanged.
result6.309 — an improvement over both parents (6.324 and 6.379) but still behind the 6.257 best-so-far. Gains were spread evenly across scenarios, suggesting front sweep near 50° is a mild, broad-based aero win. Worth pushing front sweep further, toward 52-55°, combined with the champion's slim arm waist.

borngeneration 15 via crossover
hypothesisThis crosses the prior best-so-far parent (6.274) with itself, essentially a local mutation probe to see if nearby arm-width and rear-sweep tweaks can improve on it directly.
methodWidened the arm slightly and straightened the rear sweep a touch, both modest changes from the parent.
result6.311 — slightly worse than the parent it came from (6.274) and behind the new best-so-far of 6.257. The small arm-width increase likely added marginal mass without a stiffness payoff. Worth trying the opposite direction — narrowing arm width below the parent's value — since that pattern won elsewhere this generation.

hypothesisTwo closely-matched strong parents (6.373 and 6.371) were crossed on the bet that recombining their traits could squeeze out an incremental gain.
methodShortened the plate about 5%, pulled the material blend fully back to pure carbon-fiber plate, and nudged the battery wedge up slightly.
result6.36 — better than both parents, but the generation's best-so-far had already moved to 6.257 (set by 54a73703249e), so this falls short. Storm remains the costliest scenario (4.88), suggesting these two parents were already near a local plateau. Worth crossing this line with the new champion's slimmer arm waist next.

frame mass99.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 15 via pivot
hypothesisThe fitter parent (6.324) was already near the front of the pack, so this pivot bets that lengthening the plate slightly while trimming arm length can free up a bit more efficiency.
methodLengthened the main plate about 7%, thickened the arm slightly to 4.3 mm, shortened arm length a touch, and added a small 0.7° battery wedge.
resultCame in at 6.397 — worse than its stronger parent (6.324) and short of the pre-generation best (6.274). Storm was the biggest drag (4.91 Wh/km), suggesting the thicker arm added mass without buying enough stiffness back. Worth retrying the plate-length change alone, without the arm thickness bump.

borngeneration 15 via pivot
hypothesisDrawing on a much weaker parent's wider-arm traits, this pivot bets that a beefier arm waist and width could add stiffness margin for the storm scenario without costing too much elsewhere.
methodWidened the arm width and waist noticeably, eased the front sweep back to 46°, and stretched arm length slightly.
resultLanded at 6.525, worse than its stronger parent (6.324) and well off the pre-generation best. Storm again the weak point (5.01), pointing to the wider arm just adding drag and mass. Lesson: this generation's winners consistently keep arm waist near 0.55 — worth reverting to that instead of widening.

energy score6.574 Wh/km
scenario mean4.051 Wh/km
worst scenario5.045 Wh/km
frame mass118.3 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 15 via designer
hypothesisA hand-designed seed from the Claude-designer role, exploring a fresh baseline independent of the evolutionary lineage — testing a slim arm paired with a thick plate and generous deck gap as a hand-picked structural hypothesis.
methodHand-set a slim, narrow-waisted arm at 4.0 mm thickness, but combined it with a large 40 mm deck gap and a heavily thickened plate (x1.50).
result6.574 — well behind the 6.257 best-so-far and behind the top evolved lineage. The oversized deck gap and thick plate likely added mass the evolved lines have learned to avoid, since winners cluster around 22-26 mm deck gap and ~x0.70 plate thickness. Worth rerunning this same arm seed with a tighter deck gap and thinner plate to align with what evolution has already found.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score7.773 Wh/km
scenario mean4.781 Wh/km
worst scenario5.985 Wh/km
frame mass199.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 15 via designer
hypothesisA hand-designed test of whether a much beefier, wider and thicker arm could buy enough structural margin to pay off across the adverse-weather battery.
methodHand-set a heavily widened arm (x1.40) with a thick waist (x1.30) and 7.0 mm thickness, on the same large 40 mm deck gap.
result7.773 — the worst result in this batch, far behind the 6.257 best-so-far and behind nearly every evolved candidate. The heavy arm drove up cost in every scenario, especially storm (5.98), confirming that the evolutionary trend toward slim arms (waist ~0.55, width ~0.75-0.85) is correct. This heavy-arm design line looks like a dead end worth abandoning.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
statusinvalid — deck gap too small for FC/ESC stack
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass125.7 g
materialpet_cf
borngeneration 15 via pivot
hypothesisBoth parents were well off the pace (6.99 and 7.60), so this pivot bets on a more aggressive redesign: a straighter rear arm and a much tighter deck gap to shed frontal area and mass rather than making an incremental tweak.
methodStraightened the rear sweep, shifted the material blend toward a stiffer composite, thickened the plate about 17%, and shrank the deck gap from 28 mm down to 21.5 mm.
resultInvalid — the deck gap collapsed below what the FC/ESC stack needs to physically fit. This confirms deck gap has a hard floor somewhere above 21.5 mm regardless of how the plate is thickened. Worth testing the same sweep/material bet again but holding deck gap at 23 mm or more.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5
