statusinvalid — plate web too thin (plate_main 0.84 mm, stock 1.90 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass83.0 g
materialpetg
borngeneration 15 via pivot
hypothesisOne parent was already invalid and the other weak (7.79), so this pivot gambles on a material swap toward a lighter plastic plus a narrower, longer plate to cut weight.
methodShifted the material blend heavily toward petg, narrowed the plate width about 6%, lengthened the arm, and thinned the plate slightly.
resultInvalid — the main plate web thinned to 0.84 mm against a 1.90 mm structural stock minimum. The narrower plate combined with the material and thickness change pushed the web past its floor. Next: keep plate thickness at or above the parent's x0.90 whenever pairing a lighter material with a narrower plate.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

statusinvalid — plate web too thin (plate_mid 1.12 mm, stock 1.47 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass94.5 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 15 via pivot
hypothesisWith a strong parent at 6.379, this pivot bets that tilting the battery and opening the deck gap could improve cooling/CG balance enough to offset the extra structure needed.
methodAdded a 10.5° battery wedge, nearly doubled the deck gap increase to 33.4 mm, and shortened the plate about 15% to compensate.
resultInvalid — the mid plate web thinned to 1.12 mm against a 1.47 mm stock minimum. Shortening the plate to make room for the bigger deck gap took too much material from the mid section. Lesson: large deck-gap increases need plate thickness compensation, not plate-length reduction. Worth retrying the wedge/gap idea while holding plate length near the parent's value.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

statusinvalid — arm root tongues collide on the main plate
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass92.2 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 15 via pivot
hypothesisStrong parent at 6.371, so this pivot bets that a wider arm paired with a straighter front sweep could add stiffness and cut aero losses simultaneously.
methodWidened the arm, reduced front sweep to 30°, nudged battery wedge up to 15°, and trimmed arm thickness slightly.
resultInvalid — the arm root tongues collide on the main plate. The wider arm combined with the reduced front sweep pushed the root geometry into interference. Teaches that arm width and front sweep are coupled near the mount; next time keep front sweep at 32° or above whenever pushing arm width past about 0.85.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

statusinvalid — arm tip deflection (petg)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass126.6 g
materialpetg
borngeneration 15 via designer
hypothesisA hand-designed test of whether a cheaper, softer petg material could still hold up structurally if paired with a moderately thickened, wider arm.
methodHand-set a moderately widened arm at 6.5 mm thickness with a 42 mm deck gap, switched material to petg, and thinned the plate to x0.85.
resultInvalid — arm tip deflection exceeded limits under petg. The thicker 6.5 mm arm wasn't enough to compensate for petg's lower stiffness at this length and waist. Teaches that petg alone can't substitute for a stiffer composite at these arm proportions; worth retrying this exact geometry with cf_plate material instead.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

energy score6.324 Wh/km
scenario mean3.899 Wh/km
worst scenario4.851 Wh/km
frame mass92.7 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 14 via pivot
hypothesisWith the reigning best-so-far as one parent, this pivot explores whether a much sharper front sweep and tighter deck gap can trade a little arm mass for better aerodynamic balance across the weather scenarios.
methodSwept the front arms much further forward, pulled the rear sweep back slightly, narrowed the arms, and tightened the deck gap.
resultValid at 6.324 Wh/km — clearly better than its weaker parent (7.127) but slightly behind the 6.274 best-so-far parent, so no new record. The aggressive front-sweep swing likely added drag/handling penalty in crosswind and storm that offset any calm-air gain. Next: try a smaller, more incremental front-sweep nudge from the 6.274 baseline rather than a ~17° jump.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

energy score6.371 Wh/km
scenario mean3.928 Wh/km
worst scenario4.887 Wh/km
frame mass94.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 14 via designer
hypothesisA designer fresh build testing whether an aggressive battery-wedge tilt (shifting CG) could unlock efficiency the evolved population hasn't explored, on otherwise lean evolved-like proportions.
methodFresh design with lean arm and plate proportions close to top evolved frames, but with a much steeper battery wedge tilt than any current-generation candidate.
resultValid at 6.371 Wh/km — respectable and close to the evolved pack's mid-6.3s cluster, though still behind the 6.274 best-so-far. The steep battery tilt didn't hurt as much as expected, suggesting CG shift is a relatively low-cost gene the population hasn't explored much. Next: cross this battery-wedge idea onto the actual best-so-far frame to see if it stacks additively.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.373 Wh/km
scenario mean3.928 Wh/km
worst scenario4.890 Wh/km
frame mass98.2 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 14 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing two closely-matched competitive frames tests whether nudging their front sweep angle can push performance past both.
methodThe only real change is a steeper front sweep angle; the rest of the genome stays close to the shared parent values.
resultValid at 6.373 Wh/km, essentially matching but not beating either parent (6.37/6.35) — a near-neutral single-gene change. Suggests front sweep alone, in this narrow range, isn't the lever separating top performers. Next: hold front sweep fixed at the better parent's value and vary a different single gene, e.g. plate thickness, to find a more sensitive lever.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

frame mass98.4 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 14 via crossover
hypothesisA self-crossover of a solid mid-pack frame explores nearby mutations in plate proportions and arm waist to see if small perturbations can beat the parent outright.
methodWidened and lengthened the plate slightly, loosened the arm waist a touch, and thickened the arm marginally.
resultValid at 6.379 Wh/km, slightly worse than the 6.34 parent — the small plate/waist perturbations added marginal weight without a matching gain. This parent seems to sit at a local optimum where small moves in any direction cost efficiency. Next: try a self-cross that shrinks, rather than grows, plate width to test the opposite direction.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

worst scenario4.909 Wh/km
frame mass99.6 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 14 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing a competitive frame with the reigning best-so-far bets that blending their plate proportions and material tweak could combine the best-so-far's efficiency with the other parent's structural margin.
methodShifted the material parameter slightly within the carbon-plate family, trimmed the plate length a bit, and loosened the arm waist slightly.
resultValid at 6.395 Wh/km, worse than both parents (6.38 and 6.274) — the blend landed structurally in between but lost efficiency in every scenario relative to the best-so-far. Simple gene-averaging isn't finding a better point here. Next: try crossing with the plate length locked to the best-so-far's value and vary only the material parameter.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.468 Wh/km
scenario mean3.985 Wh/km
worst scenario4.967 Wh/km
frame mass107.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 14 via pivot
hypothesisStarting from the current best-so-far, this pivot tests whether a stiffer, longer arm and thicker plate can extend its edge further into storm conditions.
methodWidened the arms noticeably, lengthened them slightly, thickened the plate, and nudged the material parameter within the carbon-plate family.
resultValid at 6.468 Wh/km, clearly worse than the 6.274 best-so-far parent — the extra arm and plate mass outweighed any storm-scenario robustness gain. Reinforces that the best-so-far is already near the weight/stiffness sweet spot and further beefing up loses more than it gains. Next: try thickening only the plate, not the arm width, to isolate which change costs the most.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 14 via pivot
hypothesisBets that beefing up arm width and thickness on a mid-pack frame would improve storm/crosswind stiffness enough to offset the added mass.
methodWidened the arms back toward stock, thickened them slightly, eased the front sweep, and opened the deck gap marginally.
resultValid at 6.478 Wh/km, worse than both the 6.333 parent and the 6.274 best-so-far — the added arm bulk cost more across every scenario than the stiffness bought back. Confirms this frame lineage is weight-limited, not stiffness-limited. Next: revert the width/thickness increase and test the sweep/deck-gap change alone.

metricvalue
energy score6.991 Wh/km
scenario mean4.304 Wh/km
worst scenario5.374 Wh/km
frame mass143.1 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 14 via designer
hypothesisA Claude-designer fresh build testing a bulkier, higher-stiffness durability-first frame, wagering that beefier arms and plates pay off in storm robustness even at a mass penalty.
methodFresh design with notably longer/thicker arms, a wide/long plate, thick plate walls, and a pronounced rear sweep — heavier and stiffer across the board than typical evolved frames.
resultValid but weak at 6.991 Wh/km, well behind the 6.274 best-so-far and every other candidate this generation — the mass penalty from oversizing everything at once overwhelmed any robustness gain, with storm the worst score of the batch. Confirms the evolved population's lean frames already sit closer to the efficient edge than a designer's build-it-strong heuristic. Next: have the designer start from an evolved parent's proportions and dial up only one dimension, e.g. plate thickness, instead of a from-scratch bulky build.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
statusinvalid — plate web too thin (plate_mid 1.11 mm, stock 1.47 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass95.1 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 14 via pivot
hypothesisff46e60afeaa was a solid mid-pack frame; this pivot bets that shaving more mass from the plate (shorter, thinner, narrower arms) can push efficiency further before structural limits bite.
methodShortened the main plate further, narrowed the arms slightly, thinned the plate more, and eased the front sweep angle a touch.
resultInvalid — the plate web dropped to 1.11mm against a 1.47mm stock minimum, so it failed the structural check before it could fly. Stacking three separate weight-cuts (shorter plate, thinner plate, narrower arm) in the same direction at once was too aggressive. Next: try only one of these cuts at a time to find where the wall-thickness floor actually sits.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 14 via pivot
hypothesisTries to recover a strong parent's efficiency by opening the deck gap for better airflow while trimming plate size to shed weight.
methodWidened the deck gap substantially, shrank the plate width and length, and pulled the arm length in slightly.
resultInvalid — the main plate web thinned to 1.29mm against a 1.90mm stock minimum, so shrinking both plate dimensions at once broke the structural floor, the same failure mode as other plate-shrink pivots this generation. Next: keep plate dimensions near parent levels and test the deck-gap widening in isolation.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — arm root tongues collide on the main plate
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass116.1 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 14 via pivot
hypothesisBets that a much larger deck gap plus thicker arms could improve stability in gusty/storm conditions on a frame whose parent already sat competitively.
methodNearly doubled the deck gap, tightened the arm waist, lengthened the plate, and thickened the arms substantially.
resultInvalid — the arm root tongues now collide on the main plate, a geometric interference from combining a much thicker arm with a tighter waist. Shows the arm-thickness and waist genes aren't independent — pushing one against print-space limits requires easing the other. Next: retest the deck-gap/plate-length change alone, keeping arm thickness and waist at parent values.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

hypothesisBets that a wider plate and steeper battery wedge (shifting CG) could improve handling enough to justify a much thicker arm waist for durability.
methodMore than doubled the arm waist thickness, widened the plate, tripled the battery tilt angle, and tightened the deck gap substantially.
resultInvalid — the deck gap shrank too far for the FC/ESC stack to fit. Squeezing the deck gap while also growing the waist and plate left no room for electronics clearance. Next: keep the deck gap near parent levels while testing the battery-wedge and waist changes separately.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — plates too thin for pla_plus (1.5 < 1.6 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass77.7 g
materialpla_plus
borngeneration 14 via immigrant
hypothesisA random immigrant testing PLA+ as a lighter/cheaper alternative material family, probing whether the population's carbon-plate dominance is a local optimum rather than a global one.
methodFresh random genome — smaller arms, wide/long plate, wide deck gap, built in PLA+ instead of carbon plate.
resultInvalid — plates measured 1.5mm against PLA+'s 1.6mm minimum wall requirement, since PLA+ is weaker than carbon plate and needs thicker walls for the same geometry. Useful negative data point: plate-thickness settings here were tuned for cf_plate's stock minimums, not PLA+'s stricter ones. Next: rerun similar immigrants but scale plate thickness up specifically for PLA+'s minimum before evaluating fitness.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

statusinvalid — arm tip deflection (pet_cf)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass88.2 g
materialpet_cf
borngeneration 14 via designer
hypothesisA designer fresh build testing PET-CF as a tougher, more impact-resistant alternative to carbon plate, on an otherwise lean, evolved-like arm geometry.
methodFresh design with long, narrow, tightly-waisted arms similar to top evolved frames, but built from PET-CF instead of carbon plate.
resultInvalid — arm tip deflection exceeded the limit for PET-CF, since its stiffness is lower than carbon plate and this arm geometry was sized for cf_plate's rigidity, not PET-CF's flex. Shows material swaps can't reuse cf_plate arm proportions directly. Next: retest PET-CF with a shorter or wider arm profile sized for its lower modulus.

frame mass93.8 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 13 via crossover
hypothesisWith both parents clustered near best-so-far, the bet was that blending a wider front sweep with a modestly tighter deck gap could nudge past the plateau.
methodFront sweep increased ~3.8°, deck gap trimmed by 1.2 mm, plate width held at parent value.
resultValid at 6.318, the best result of this generation's candidates and very close to best-so-far (6.274) without quite beating it; storm remains the heaviest scenario (4.85) but the frame is otherwise well balanced. Worth testing: trim deck gap a bit further toward 23-24 mm while holding front sweep fixed to see if it closes the remaining gap.

hypothesisCrossing the two close parents, the bet was that borrowing the leaner arm/plate thickness from one while adding a touch more front sweep would blend their strengths.
methodArms thinned back to 4.0 mm, plate thinned slightly, front sweep increased ~1.8°, material blend lightened slightly.
resultValid at 6.333, beating both parents individually but still short of best-so-far (6.274); the leaner structure helped but the extra front sweep may have cost some of that gain. Worth testing: the same thinner arm/plate combo without the front sweep bump.

hypothesisBuilt from the current best-so-far, this candidate bet that a wider front sweep and a stiffer, thicker-waisted arm combined with a trimmed deck gap could push past the plateau.
methodFront sweep opened by ~8°, plate widened 6%, arm waist thickened, deck gap trimmed by ~3 mm.
resultValid but slightly worse than its best-so-far parent (6.34 vs 6.274), dragged down mainly by storm (4.87); the added frontal area from the wider sweep and plate likely outweighed the deck-gap savings. borngeneration 13 via pivot
hypothesisThe parent was solid but not leading, so the bet was a heavier material blend plus a small battery wedge could rebalance CG and edge out a gain.
methodFront sweep pulled back ~1.3°, material blend shifted heavier, small 0.7° battery wedge added, arm waist nudged.
resultValid at 6.35, essentially flat versus the 6.311 parent and short of best-so-far; the heavier material blend likely added mass without an aero payoff. Worth testing: keep the battery wedge but revert the material blend back toward the lighter setting to isolate its contribution.

metricvalue
energy score6.370 Wh/km
scenario mean3.925 Wh/km
worst scenario4.891 Wh/km
frame mass98.1 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 13 via pivot
hypothesisFrom the same best-so-far parent, the bet here was that slightly longer, thicker arms would buy structural margin cheaply while a modest deck-gap trim clawed back some efficiency.
methodArm length extended ~3%, arm thickness increased 0.3 mm, deck gap cut by 1.3 mm, rear sweep eased back slightly.
resultValid at 6.37, worse than both the best-so-far parent and its sibling ad77afd2152e; the extra arm length/thickness added mass that the small deck-gap saving couldn't offset, with storm again the weak point (4.89). Worth testing: the arm-thickening alone, without the length increase, to see if it's a net loss on its own.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.380 Wh/km
scenario mean3.931 Wh/km
worst scenario4.898 Wh/km
frame mass99.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 13 via pivot
hypothesisThis candidate tested the opposite direction from the deck-gap-tightening attempts: betting that a wider gap plus the lightest material and thicker arm waist would still net a win through mass savings.
methodDeck gap widened by 5 mm, arm waist thickened, material switched to the lightest blend, plate shortened slightly.
resultValid but the weakest successful pivot this generation at 6.38; widening the deck gap clearly added drag/mass the lighter material couldn't compensate for, confirming the gap should move down, not up, from 26 mm. Worth testing: keep the pure-light material choice but pair it with a deck-gap trim instead of an expansion.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 13 via crossover
hypothesisThe bet was that a longer plate combined with more rear sweep and battery wedge would improve stability enough to offset the added structure.
methodPlate lengthened 8%, rear sweep increased 3°, battery wedge roughly doubled, deck gap trimmed slightly.
resultValid but worst of this generation's crossovers at 6.512, worse than both parents; storm (5.0) shows the added mass and wedge hurt worst-case performance most. Worth testing: the rear sweep/wedge combo without the plate lengthening to isolate which change is the real culprit.

energy score6.757 Wh/km
scenario mean4.162 Wh/km
worst scenario5.189 Wh/km
frame mass128.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 13 via designer
hypothesisThe bet was that a bulkier, more swept airframe with a larger plate footprint might trade some efficiency for wind stability and still come out ahead.
methodHand-designed genome: longer/thicker arms, wider front and rear sweep angles, notably larger plate length and width, wider deck gap.
resultValid at 6.757, the best of this generation's three designer seeds but still far off best-so-far; the enlarged plate and wide sweep angles added mass and frontal area beyond what any stability gain offset, with storm the weakest scenario (5.19). Worth testing: shrink the plate footprint back toward stock while keeping the wider sweep angles, to see if sweep alone contributes value.

frame mass132.2 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 13 via designer
hypothesisA fresh designer seed exploring a stockier, untapered arm baseline with a generous deck gap and steep battery wedge, testing whether a conservative structural design could compete outside the pivot/crossover lineage.
methodHand-designed genome: full-length untapered arms at 5.5 mm thickness, 42 mm deck gap, 14° battery wedge, thick plate.
resultValid at 6.84, well behind best-so-far and the weakest of this generation's three designer seeds; the oversized deck gap and steep wedge likely added drag and mass across every scenario, including storm (5.25). Worth testing: a designer seed with deck gap near 26 mm and battery wedge near 0-2° to isolate whether the arm geometry alone can compete.

metricvalue
energy score7.025 Wh/km
scenario mean4.326 Wh/km
worst scenario5.399 Wh/km
frame mass148.7 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 13 via designer
hypothesisThis designer seed bet that over-building the arms — thick and barely tapered — would provide structural margin without an aero penalty.
methodHand-designed genome: longer, thicker (6.0 mm) arms with minimal waist taper, near-stock deck gap, zero battery wedge, thinner plate.
resultValid but the worst candidate this generation at 7.025, far off best-so-far, with every scenario elevated (storm 5.4). This teaches that arm thickness beyond ~4-4.5 mm with minimal waist taper is a clear loss under this fitness metric. Worth testing: a designer seed with thinner, more tapered arms closer to what the pivot lineage has converged on (roughly 4.0 mm, 0.55-0.65 waist).
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
statusinvalid — deck gap too small for FC/ESC stack
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass105.6 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 13 via pivot
hypothesisThe near-best parent suggested there was still headroom in stiffening the plate and tightening the electronics deck, so this candidate bet on a thicker plate plus a leaner deck stack to shave mass and drag.
methodPlate thickness increased substantially, deck gap cut by 6 mm, a 2° battery wedge added, and the material blend lightened.
resultInvalid: the tightened deck gap left no room for the FC/ESC stack. This confirms 26 mm is close to the real floor and that thickening the plate eats into the same clearance budget. Worth testing: trim deck gap alone by 1-2 mm without touching plate thickness to find the true minimum.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 13 via pivot
hypothesisWith the stronger parent still trailing best-so-far, the bet was that a tighter deck stack combined with more battery wedge could recover some efficiency.
methodDeck gap cut by ~3.7 mm, battery wedge nearly doubled, plate widened slightly and thinned slightly.
resultInvalid for the same reason as its sibling: FC/ESC stack collision. Two independent failures around 20-23 mm strongly suggest the stack clearance floor sits close to 24-25 mm. Worth testing: deck gap at 24-25 mm alone, holding wedge and plate width at parent values, to bracket the floor precisely.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — arm tongue bolts miss the main plate
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass93.7 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 13 via pivot
hypothesisStarting from the strong parent, the bet was that a much larger rear sweep would improve crosswind/yaw balance enough to justify the structural risk.
methodRear sweep increased by 11°, plate and arms widened slightly, material blend shifted.
resultInvalid: the large rear sweep moved the arm tongues off the plate's bolt pattern. This shows sweep changes are bounded by the fixed bolt layout, likely capping rear sweep well below 47° for this arm length. Worth testing: rear sweep increases in smaller ~5° steps to find the real ceiling before bolts miss.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — plate web too thin (plate_main 0.85 mm, stock 1.90 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass93.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 13 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing two equally-scored parents, the bet was that narrowing the plate while opening the deck gap slightly could cut mass without compromising structure.
methodPlate narrowed 6%, deck gap opened 2.2 mm, arm waist thinned slightly.
resultInvalid: the narrower plate's web thickness fell below the structural minimum (0.85 mm vs 1.90 mm stock). This teaches that plate width and thickness are coupled — narrowing width without compensating thickness risks failing the web-thickness floor. Worth testing: narrow the plate width but pair it with a plate-thickness increase to keep the web above 1.90 mm.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.308 Wh/km
scenario mean3.887 Wh/km
worst scenario4.841 Wh/km
frame mass93.9 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 12 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing the current best-so-far (1704aea48de9, 6.274) with the near-best ba61fc4b63fb (6.286) aimed to combine the champion's efficiency with a thicker plate for extra structural margin.
methodThickened the plate (x0.70→x0.78), tightened the arm waist slightly (x0.59→x0.55), and marginally lengthened the arms (x1.30→x1.31).
result6.308, close to but not surpassing either parent - not a new best-so-far. The plate thickening added mass that offset the tighter waist's savings, confirming the champion's thinner x0.70 plate is closer to optimal. Worth testing next: repeat this cross with plate thickness left untouched at x0.70 to isolate the waist/length effect alone.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.311 Wh/km
scenario mean3.888 Wh/km
worst scenario4.845 Wh/km
frame mass94.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 12 via designer
hypothesisDesigner bet on pushing arm length further than the elite lineage's ~x1.30 norm (to x1.35) while keeping battery wedge at 0° and the thin-plate recipe intact, testing whether the arm-length trend keeps paying off.
methodExtended arm length to x1.35, kept arm width/waist/thickness at the proven thin values, enlarged the plate slightly (x1.00/x1.03), and held battery wedge at 0°.
result6.311, competitive but still short of the 6.274 best-so-far - not a new winner, and close to several crossover results in the same 6.30-6.35 band, suggesting x1.35 arm length sits near a plateau rather than an improvement. Worth testing next: keep arm length at x1.35 but shrink plate length/width back toward x0.97 to shed the extra plate mass added here.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 12 via crossover
hypothesisba61fc4b63fb sits close to best-so-far, so this self-pairing bet that thickening the arm waist while trimming arm length slightly could add robustness without losing efficiency.
methodThickened the arm waist from x0.59 to x0.70 and shortened arm length slightly from x1.30 to x1.27.
result6.311, a step back from the 6.286 parent - the added waist material outweighed the modest length savings. Confirms the parent's thinner waist was closer to optimal. Worth testing next: trim arm waist further below x0.59 while holding length at x1.30 to see if the trend keeps improving.

energy score6.333 Wh/km
scenario mean3.902 Wh/km
worst scenario4.861 Wh/km
frame mass95.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 12 via designer
hypothesisDesigner bet on testing whether a substantially larger deck gap (36 mm vs the elite 26 mm) trades battery/deck clearance for an aerodynamic or structural benefit, while otherwise matching the elite arm recipe.
methodKept the elite thin-arm proportions (x1.30 length, x0.76 width, x0.55 waist) but widened the deck gap from the usual 26.0 mm to 36.0 mm, with the same enlarged plate as its sibling candidate.
result6.333, worse than its tighter-deck-gap sibling (ddf19b536a16 at 6.311) and short of the 6.274 best-so-far - confirms deck gap widening is a mass/drag penalty, consistent with candidate 1b84d46a56fc's finding. Worth testing next: stop exploring wider deck gaps and instead probe values below 26.0 mm to find the tight-clearance boundary.

metricvalue
energy score6.346 Wh/km
scenario mean3.910 Wh/km
worst scenario4.872 Wh/km
frame mass95.8 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 12 via crossover
hypothesisParent ba61fc4b63fb sat near best-so-far at 6.286; crossing it with the heavier e0575966e577 (6.406) bet that blending their arm-width and deck-gap traits could combine strengths rather than dilute them.
methodWidened arm width from x0.75 to x0.86 and opened the deck gap from 26.0 mm to 28.4 mm, leaving plate length essentially unchanged.
resultLanded at 6.346, worse than either parent and well off the 6.274 best-so-far. The wider arm and larger deck gap added mass/drag across every scenario (storm rose to 4.87) without any offsetting gain. Worth testing next: pull the deck gap back toward 26 mm while keeping the parent's other genes, to isolate whether width or gap was the culprit.

energy score6.355 Wh/km
scenario mean3.918 Wh/km
worst scenario4.875 Wh/km
frame mass91.4 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 12 via designer
hypothesisDesigner bet that a much steeper battery wedge (12°) combined with an elevated rear sweep, layered onto the elite thin-arm/thin-plate recipe, could unlock a gain the mutation/crossover operators hadn't found.
methodKept the proven thin-arm proportions (x1.30 length, x0.76 width, x0.55 waist, x0.70 plate thickness) but raised rear sweep to 39.3° and pushed battery wedge to a steep 12.0°.
result6.355, worse than the 6.274 best-so-far and worse than near-identical crossovers using 0° wedge - the steep tilt looks like a net drag/mass penalty, echoing the same pattern seen in candidate 3007a946fb33. Storm rose to 4.88. Worth testing next: try battery wedge in smaller 2-4° increments rather than jumping straight to 12°, to find any beneficial range before ruling it out.

metricvalue
energy score6.458 Wh/km
scenario mean3.979 Wh/km
worst scenario4.959 Wh/km
frame mass104.8 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 12 via crossover
hypothesisParent e0575966e577 carried a modest battery wedge that hadn't yet paid off; this cross bet that pairing further arm lengthening with a slightly reduced wedge angle might recover efficiency.
methodLengthened arms (x1.30→x1.35), widened them slightly (x0.75→x0.79), increased rear sweep (36.3°→37.4°), and nudged battery wedge down (1.9°→1.7°).
result6.458, worse than both parents and the 6.274 best-so-far - the extra arm length and width added mass without any gain, and storm rose to 4.96. Battery wedge continues to underperform across this generation's crosses. Worth testing next: drop battery wedge to 0° entirely on an otherwise strong parent to check if tilt is a net negative overall.

materialcf_plate
borngeneration 12 via crossover
hypothesisWith both parents mid-pack (6.366, 6.291), this cross gambled that adding a battery wedge tilt on top of wider, longer arms might unlock an aerodynamic gain not yet seen in the elite lineage.
methodWidened arms substantially (x0.86→x1.01), lengthened them (x1.27→x1.35), and introduced a 1.5° battery wedge tilt.
result6.497, clearly worse than both parents and the 6.274 best-so-far. The combined mass from bulkier arms plus wedge tilt hurt every scenario, especially storm (4.99). Worth testing next: isolate the battery wedge alone on a proven thin-arm parent to see if tilt helps independent of arm bulk.

frame mass128.8 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 12 via crossover
hypothesisBoth parents (6.533, 6.415) were already well off the elite pace; this cross explored a bulkier, wide-deck-gap region of the space, betting that extended arms and a steeper battery wedge might unlock gains.
methodSubstantially widened the deck gap (34.5 mm→41.3 mm), lengthened the arms (x1.19→x1.33), steepened the battery wedge (2.0°→5.4°), and widened the plate (x1.07→x1.13).
result6.771, the weakest valid candidate this generation and far off the 6.274 best-so-far - stacking a wider deck gap, longer arms, steeper wedge, and wider plate compounded mass/drag penalties across every scenario. This lineage is diverging further from the elite region. Worth testing next: retire this parent pair in favor of crosses seeded from the top-4 elite lineage.

metricvalue
energy score7.423 Wh/km
scenario mean4.573 Wh/km
worst scenario5.700 Wh/km
frame mass176.2 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 12 via immigrant
hypothesisAn immigrant injects fresh diversity to counter convergence around the 6.27-6.4 elite band, exploring a bulkier, thicker-armed region of the design space unrelated to the current lineage.
methodFresh random genome: shorter but much wider/thicker arms (x0.94 length, x1.21 width, 6.6 mm thickness), a steep 44.2° front sweep, an enlarged plate (x1.06/x1.06), a wide 35.9 mm deck gap, a 3.2° battery wedge, and a thick x1.30 plate.
result7.423, the weakest candidate this generation and far off the 6.274 best-so-far - the over-built structure added mass and drag in every scenario, especially storm (5.70). This confirms the elite lineage's thin-arm, narrow-deck-gap direction is well-founded. Worth testing next: bias future immigrants toward lighter-weight starting genomes rather than uniform random draws.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — arm root tongues collide on the main plate
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass94.1 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 12 via crossover
hypothesisBoth parents were competitive (6.308 and 6.286), so this cross bet that combining longer arms with a reduced rear sweep and a thinner plate could shave off more mass.
methodReduced rear sweep from 36.0° to 34.0°, lengthened the arms slightly (x1.26→x1.30), and thinned the plate (x0.74→x0.70).
resultInvalid: the arm root tongues collided on the main plate. Pushing arm length up while tightening rear sweep closed the clearance margin at the root simultaneously. Worth testing next: lengthen the arms alone while holding rear sweep near 36° to preserve root clearance.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass94.1 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 12 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing with the reigning best-so-far (1704aea48de9, 6.274) aimed to inherit its efficiency while testing whether shorter arms and a tighter deck gap could shed more mass and drag.
methodShortened arms considerably (x1.26→x1.15), thickened the plate (x0.74→x0.86), and tightened the deck gap (26.2 mm→23.7 mm).
resultInvalid: the rotor came too close to the deck/battery. Shrinking arm length and deck gap together closed out the rotor clearance margin. Worth testing next: shorten the arms alone while keeping deck gap near 26 mm to find the clearance boundary safely.

statusinvalid — plate web too thin (plate_main 0.99 mm, stock 1.90 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass90.4 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 12 via crossover
hypothesisBoth mid-pack parents (6.291, 6.366) suggested the general trend that thinner, narrower plates help, so this cross bet on pushing both plate width and thickness down together for more mass savings.
methodNarrowed plate width (x1.00→x0.95) and thinned plate thickness (x0.75→x0.70) simultaneously.
resultInvalid: the plate web dropped to 0.99 mm, under half the 1.90 mm stock minimum. Width and thickness reductions compound rather than being independently safe. Worth testing next: thin the plate alone while holding plate width at the parent's value to map the safe thickness floor.

borngeneration 12 via crossover
hypothesisBet on testing whether a switch to pa12_cf material paired with a thicker arm wall could permit a lighter overall structure, exploring beyond the cf_plate material that has dominated so far.
methodSwitched material toward pa12_cf, thickened the arm wall (4.0 mm→5.1 mm), and narrowed the arm width (x0.86→x0.78).
resultInvalid: plate thickness (inherited from cf_plate-tuned parents) fell to 1.4 mm, below pa12_cf's 1.6 mm minimum wall requirement. Material swaps need their own plate-thickness re-tuning, since pa12_cf needs thicker walls than cf_plate for equivalent strength. Worth testing next: if retrying pa12_cf, explicitly raise the plate thickness gene above 1.6 mm before combining with other changes.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 10 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing the near-champion 8d9fd4a01c9a (6.291) with mid-pack 12db578f6d9a (6.387) tests whether blending in a wider arm and thinner plate from the second parent can nudge the champion lineage even leaner.
methodWidened arm width from ×0.76 to ×0.86, shortened arm length slightly from ×1.30 to ×1.26, and trimmed plate width and thickness marginally.
resultFitness came in at 6.308, just shy of the 6.291 best-so-far and matching sibling a0a25432bc7e almost exactly. The wider arm didn't help enough to beat the champion's narrower profile, suggesting arm width near ×0.76 is closer to the local optimum than ×0.86. Next: try isolating just the arm-length trim without the width increase to see which change is actually helping.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 11 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing the runner-up with a mid-tier design, betting that loosening the arm waist and shifting the material blend could combine the runner-up's efficient sweep with the other parent's structural margin.
methodLoosened the arm waist taper, nudged the material blend toward the boundary that elsewhere flips to pa12_cf (though it stayed cf_plate here), widened the plate slightly, and thinned the plate stock a touch.
resultFitness landed at 6.366, between its two parents (better than 6.427, worse than 6.308) and off the pace of the 6.274 leader. It picked up some of the runner-up's efficiency, but the looser arm waist likely added enough drag/mass to cancel most of the gain. Worth testing next: repeat the cross with arm waist held at the runner-up's tighter setting to isolate whether that half of the blend is the useful part.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 11 via crossover
hypothesisAlso crossing the champion with the runner-up, but this time betting on stiffness and stability -- adding a battery wedge tilt and thicker arms in hopes of buying robustness in the harder scenarios like storm and crosswind.
methodTilted the battery mount about 2 degrees, thickened the arms from 4.0mm to 4.6mm, bumped front sweep slightly, and trimmed arm width fractionally.
resultFitness landed at 6.406, worse than both parents (6.291 and 6.308) and well behind the new best of 6.274. Every scenario score rose versus the champion, showing the thicker arms and wedge tilt added mass/drag without buying enough robustness to pay for it. Worth testing next: try the battery wedge alone at under 1 degree, without the arm-thickness increase, to isolate whether tilt has any standalone benefit.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

frame mass107.4 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 11 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing two mid-pack designs that shared elevated sweep and plate proportions, betting that combining their deck-gap and arm-thickness traits could close some of the gap to the leaders.
methodTightened the deck gap slightly (about 31.6mm to 29.5mm), thickened the arms to 4.3mm, trimmed front sweep by about 1 degree, and kept plate length the same as both parents.
resultFitness came in at 6.415, worse than both parents (6.372 and 6.351) and far off the 6.274 leader. The cross didn't inherit either parent's strengths and only added arm mass, with every scenario score landing above the leader pack. Worth testing next: stop breeding from this deck-gap/arm-thickness lineage and redirect crossovers toward the champion pool instead.

materialcf_plate
borngeneration 11 via crossover
hypothesisEffectively a self-pairing of an already-weak design, betting that stretching plate length while trimming arm width could recover some of its fitness deficit.
methodStretched plate length by roughly 9%, trimmed arm width fractionally, and left everything else -- including the sizable battery wedge tilt and already-thick plates -- unchanged from the parent.
resultFitness was 6.533, worse even than the already-weak parent (6.494) and far off the 6.274 best-so-far. Stretching plate length alone added mass without touching the parent's real problems (heavy wedge, thick plates). Worth testing next: prune this lineage from future breeding and replace it with fresh immigrants rather than trying to patch it gene by gene.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — plates too thin for pa12_cf (1.4 < 1.6 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass72.2 g
materialpa12_cf
borngeneration 11 via mutation
hypothesisSame champion parent; the bet was to shift the design out of the cf_plate family into pa12_cf, chasing whatever mass/cost tradeoff that plastic offers, while loosening the arm waist and widening the plate slightly.
methodLoosened the arm waist taper, widened the plate a few percent, shortened arm length slightly, and pushed the material blend far enough to cross into the pa12_cf family instead of carbon-fiber plate.
resultInvalid: the plate was too thin for pa12_cf's manufacturability floor (1.4mm vs the 1.6mm minimum required). The mutation changed material family but kept a plate-thickness setting tuned for cf_plate's looser gauge requirement. Lesson: any material swap needs a compensating thickness bump baked in, or it inherits a gauge that's illegal for the new plastic. Worth testing next: repeat this exact cross but with plate thickness bumped up 15-20% to respect pa12_cf's floor.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

statusinvalid — plate web too thin (plate_main 0.37 mm, stock 1.90 mm)

materialasa
borngeneration 11 via immigrant
hypothesisA random immigrant probing a very different design region -- steep sweep angles, heavier arms, and asa plastic -- to check whether the search has overlooked a viable design outside the cf_plate cluster the leaders live in.
methodA fresh random design with shorter arms, a wider/thicker cross-section, very aggressive front/rear sweep (47/61 degrees), a steep battery wedge, thick plates, and asa plastic instead of carbon fiber.
resultInvalid: despite a large plate-thickness multiplier, the computed web at the main plate came out far below asa's minimum gauge (0.37mm vs 1.90mm required). The extreme sweep and length/width combination erodes plate material faster than the thickness setting can compensate. Lesson: at high sweep angles, the thickness multiplier doesn't scale linearly with actual print-safe web thickness -- this region is geometrically infeasible. Worth testing next: cap sweep angle for asa/petg immigrants to a range pre-validated against the plate-web model.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

statusinvalid — deck gap too small for FC/ESC stack
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass99.2 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 11 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing two mid-tier parents, betting that shrinking the deck gap and lengthening the arms could cut frontal area and mass for a meaningful efficiency win.
methodShrank the deck gap sharply (about 26.9mm to 20.0mm), widened the arms to match the wider parent, trimmed rear sweep about 2 degrees, and stretched arm length slightly beyond either parent.
resultInvalid: a 20mm deck gap doesn't leave enough vertical clearance for the FC/ESC stack, a hard manufacturability floor. Lesson: deck gap has a firm lower bound in the low-to-mid 20s (mm), and crossovers keep landing there when both parents already sit close to the edge. Worth testing next: give deck gap a protected floor in the sampler, above the electronics stack height, so evaluations aren't wasted here.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass93.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 11 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing the runner-up with a near-identical sibling of the same fitness, betting that shrinking plate length while holding arm width could trim mass from an already-strong design.
methodShrank plate length by about 5% while nudging arm width up fractionally, leaving sweep, plate thickness, and material as in the runner-up.
resultInvalid: shrinking plate length while arm width stayed high pinched the plate-top web below the cf_plate minimum (1.00mm vs 1.75mm required). Lesson: plate length and arm width interact at the plate-top web, so trimming one without relaxing the other can break even a strong parent design. Worth testing next: pair any plate-length reduction with a small plate-width or thickness increase to preserve top-web margin.

frame mass119.9 g
materialasa
borngeneration 11 via immigrant
hypothesisAnother random immigrant probing the heavy-arm, high-sweep, asa-plastic region, betting that a bulkier structural profile with an inflated arm waist might unlock a storm-tolerant design outside the current cluster.
methodA fresh random design with near-parent arm length, an arm waist wider than the arm itself, thick 6.7mm arms, steep front/rear sweep (43/52 degrees), large plates, a big battery wedge, and asa plastic.
resultInvalid: the same failure mode as the other asa immigrant -- the plate-thickness multiplier didn't survive the geometry, leaving the main plate web at 1.20mm against asa's 1.90mm minimum. Lesson: this second asa/high-sweep failure confirms it's a systematic dead zone, where steep sweep plus an enlarged arm waist starves plate web thickness regardless of the thickness gene. Worth testing next: exclude arm-waist-over-1.0x combined with sweep-over-40 degrees from the immigrant sampler until the plate model is checked analytically against that region.

statusinvalid — arm tongue bolts miss the main plate
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass97.4 g
materialpetg
borngeneration 11 via immigrant
hypothesisA random immigrant testing a short, wide-arm, high-sweep petg design, betting a stockier arm profile might trade some efficiency for print robustness.
methodA fresh random design with arms shortened about 22%, widened arms, steep front sweep (nearly 50 degrees), moderate plate size, and petg plastic.
resultInvalid: the short arm length combined with the steep sweep moved the arm's tongue/bolt pattern completely off the edge of the main plate -- a geometric interference, not a strength failure. Lesson: arm length and sweep jointly decide whether mounting bolts land on the plate, and this pairing crossed that line. Worth testing next: add a fast bolt-pattern feasibility pre-filter before running full sims on short-arm, high-sweep immigrants.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
statusinvalid — deck gap too small for FC/ESC stack
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass110.1 g
materialpetg
borngeneration 11 via immigrant
hypothesisAnother random immigrant, betting on a compact, steep-sweep petg design with a strong battery wedge tilt to probe aggressive aerodynamic tilting as a new strategy.
methodA fresh random design with near-parent arm length, high symmetric front/rear sweep (52/52 degrees), large plates, a tight 20.1mm deck gap, a steep 14-degree battery wedge, and petg plastic.
resultInvalid: the same deck-gap floor violation as 7c7bb013dfe2 -- 20.1mm doesn't clear the FC/ESC stack. Lesson: the deck-gap floor is being hit independently by both crossover and random immigrants this generation, which argues for a harder constraint in the sampler rather than catching it only at evaluation. Worth testing next: raise the immigrant sampler's minimum deck gap directly into the mid-20s (mm).

metricvalue
statusinvalid — plate web too thin (plate_top 1.25 mm, stock 1.75 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass105.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 11 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing two mid-tier parents, betting that narrowing plate width while pushing plate thickness even higher could shore up structural margin for a robustness gain.
methodNarrowed plate width back toward stock (about 1.05x to 1.00x), increased the already-thick plate stock further (1.41x to 1.46x), and left the deck gap essentially unchanged.
resultInvalid: even with plate thickness pushed higher, the plate-top web still came in under the cf_plate minimum (1.25mm vs 1.75mm required), because the sweep angles inherited from the parents (about 34/38 degrees) were already carving that spot thin. Lesson: increasing the thickness multiplier doesn't rescue plate-top margin when sweep angle is the real driver of the thin spot. Worth testing next: once sweep exceeds roughly 33 degrees, check plate-top web thickness explicitly rather than relying on the blanket thickness multiplier.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

materialcf_plate
borngeneration 10 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing the near-champion 8d9fd4a01c9a (6.291) with a weaker but differently-swept design (5bf4af89c395, 6.497) probes whether borrowing its front sweep angle can help without dragging down performance.
methodIncreased front sweep from about 32° to 35.5°, tightened the deck gap from 26.0 to 25.0 mm, and nudged plate width down slightly, keeping the cf_plate material.
resultLanded at 6.308, essentially matching sibling 423a5b75b462 and staying just above the 6.291 best. Not a new winner, but confirms the champion's core arm/plate proportions dominate over the borrowed sweep angle. Next idea: hold the champion's sweep exactly and vary only deck gap in finer steps to see if 25-26 mm is truly optimal.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

materialcf_plate
borngeneration 10 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing near-champion 8d9fd4a01c9a (6.291) with mid-pack 88460ed9a2e2 (6.454) tests whether a thicker arm waist can allow a thinner plate without losing structural margin.
methodThickened the arm waist from ×0.55 to ×0.67, opened the deck gap from 26.0 to 28.3 mm, and trimmed plate width and thickness slightly.
resultFitness landed at 6.351, close to sibling 44ef779fcdde but still behind the 6.291 best. The thicker waist added mass that the thinner plate and wider deck gap couldn't fully offset. Next: try the thicker waist paired with the champion's original deck gap (26.0 mm) rather than opening it further, to isolate whether the gap change is hurting more than helping.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 10 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing near-champion 8d9fd4a01c9a (6.291) with weaker 7c1296374769 (6.579) tests whether a wider arm from the weaker parent can be tolerated if paired with a thinner plate to keep mass in check.
methodWidened arm width from ×0.76 to ×0.93, opened the deck gap slightly from 26.0 to 26.9 mm, and thinned the plate marginally from ×0.75 to ×0.73.
resultFitness landed at 6.352, between the two parents but still behind the 6.291 best. The wider arm cost more than the thinner plate saved, consistent with other siblings this generation showing the champion's narrower arm width is hard to beat. Next: hold arm width fixed at the champion's value and vary only deck gap to isolate its individual effect.

frame mass103.8 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 10 via mutation
hypothesisParent 12db578f6d9a (6.387) was a mid-pack cf_plate design; this mutation bets that pushing the front arms further forward while trimming plate thickness and opening the deck gap slightly can shave mass without losing stiffness where it hurts.
methodIncreased front sweep from about 34° to 40°, thinned the plate slightly (×1.41 to ×1.33 stock thickness), and widened the deck gap from 30.3 to 31.6 mm.
resultFitness improved modestly to 6.372 from the parent's 6.387, but still trails the standing best of 6.291. The extra sweep likely cut a touch of drag/mass without tripping any structural limit, but the thinner plate wasn't enough to close the gap to the champion. Worth trying: keep this sweep angle but pair it with the champion's leaner arm-length/width combo instead of the thicker plate lineage.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 10 via crossover
hypothesisA near-duplicate crossover of 12db578f6d9a (6.387) with 056184de8923 (6.412), essentially testing whether recombination without real gene movement reproduces the parent's performance as a sanity baseline.
methodGenes came out nearly identical to parent 12db578f6d9a, with no meaningful change to sweep, plate, or material.
resultFitness landed at 6.387, matching the parent exactly, confirming the crossover contributed no new information here. It's a wasted evaluation but a useful control point showing the pipeline reproduces known results faithfully. Next: bias crossover selection away from near-identical parents to avoid burning evals on redundant candidates.

borngeneration 10 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing 2ab37da5aded (6.429) with mid-pack 12db578f6d9a (6.387) bets that reducing front sweep back toward 30° while keeping a slightly thicker plate can recover some of the performance lost in both parents.
methodReduced front sweep from about 32° to 30° and nudged plate thickness up marginally from ×1.10 to ×1.11, with arm length essentially unchanged.
resultFitness landed at 6.427, close to parent 2ab37da5aded's 6.429 and still well off the 6.291 best. The sweep reduction alone wasn't enough to unlock a real gain, implying this parent pair's arm/plate proportions are the limiting factor rather than sweep angle. Next: pair this lower sweep angle with the champion's arm-width and plate-length values instead of this parent lineage's.

borngeneration 10 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing two mid-pack parents (88460ed9a2e2 6.454, 01f642b698ee 6.523) bets that widening the deck gap and growing the plate slightly can find a better balance point between them.
methodOpened the deck gap from 31.2 to 34.5 mm, grew plate width and length slightly, and widened the arm marginally.
resultFitness landed at 6.494, roughly splitting the difference between its parents but nowhere near the 6.291 best-so-far. Growing both plate dimensions together while opening the deck gap looks like it just adds mass without any offsetting gain. Next: this parent pair seems to have plateaued; better to cross either parent directly with the champion lineage instead.

borngeneration 10 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing 5bf4af89c395 (6.497) with near-champion 8d9fd4a01c9a (6.291) bets that borrowing the champion's compact plate proportions can lift the weaker parent's performance closer to the front of the pack.
methodGrew plate length slightly from ×1.16 to ×1.19, thickened the arm waist marginally, and tightened the deck gap from 33.3 to 32.9 mm.
resultFitness came in at 6.513, actually worse than both parents (6.497 and 6.291), so the recombination lost ground rather than gaining it. The larger plate combined with the wider arm waist likely added mass without a compensating benefit, and storm again shows the largest hit at 5.00. Next: this cross should be abandoned in favor of pairing 8d9fd4a01c9a with parents that share more of its plate geometry.

metricvalue
energy score6.616 Wh/km
scenario mean4.075 Wh/km
worst scenario5.082 Wh/km
frame mass119.8 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 10 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing two weaker parents (01f642b698ee 6.523, 88460ed9a2e2 6.454) bets that combining their wider arms and larger plates might find a niche the fitter lineage hasn't explored, even though both starting points already trail the best-so-far.
methodWidened arms from ×0.78 to ×0.91, lengthened arms from ×1.05 to ×1.12, grew the plate slightly, and introduced a small 0.2° battery wedge tilt.
resultFitness worsened to 6.616, below both parents and far off the 6.291 best. Bulking up arm width and length together likely added mass faster than any aerodynamic or stiffness benefit could offset, especially visible in the storm scenario spiking to 5.08. Next: this parent pair looks like a dead end for further crossover; better to retire this lineage and seed new mutations from the champion instead.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
statusinvalid — plate web too thin (plate_top 1.02 mm, stock 1.75 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass91.5 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 10 via mutation
hypothesisStarting from the near-champion 8d9fd4a01c9a (6.291), this mutation bets that shrinking the plate footprint further and opening the deck gap can trim more mass off an already-strong design.
methodShortened plate length from ×0.97 to ×0.89 of stock and increased the deck gap from 26.0 to 27.7 mm, with a negligible plate-width bump.
resultInvalid: the plate web thinned to 1.02 mm against a 1.75 mm stock minimum. Shrinking plate length on this lineage eats directly into web thickness margin, so the champion's plate is already close to the structural floor. Lesson for next time: any further plate-length shrink on this branch needs a compensating thickness increase, not just a smaller footprint.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
statusinvalid — arm tip deflection (pa12_cf)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass77.5 g
materialpa12_cf
borngeneration 10 via crossover
hypothesisCombining two mid-pack cf_plate parents (12db578f6d9a 6.387, 056184de8923 6.412), this crossover bets that switching toward a lighter pa12_cf material blend alongside a shorter, thicker-waisted arm can unlock a mass win neither parent achieved alone.
methodShifted material composition further toward pa12_cf, shortened arm length from ×0.96 to ×0.90, shrank plate length from ×0.92 to ×0.88, and thickened the arm waist from ×0.55 to ×0.61.
resultInvalid: arm tip deflection failure under pa12_cf. The material swap reduces stiffness enough that even with a thicker waist the arm flexes too much at the tip. This echoes a recurring pattern this generation: pa12_cf crossovers keep failing deflection unless arm thickness itself (not just waist) is beefed up. Next: try pa12_cf only paired with a thicker arm cross-section, not just a fatter waist.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

frame mass82.6 g
materialpa12_cf
borngeneration 10 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing mid-pack 12db578f6d9a (6.387) with weaker 5bf4af89c395 (6.497) bets that a pa12_cf material shift combined with reduced front sweep and increased rear sweep could unlock a lighter, still-stiff-enough frame.
methodReduced front sweep from about 34° to 31°, increased rear sweep from 38° to 40.5°, shifted material composition toward pa12_cf, and shrank plate length slightly.
resultInvalid: arm tip deflection failure under pa12_cf, the same failure mode seen in sibling 0e5d846ccfa1. This confirms pa12_cf crossovers in this generation consistently lack the stiffness margin these arm geometries need. Next: stop testing pa12_cf on these arm-length/width combos until arm thickness itself is increased to compensate for the material's lower stiffness.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — rotor disks overlap (tip clearance)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass107.6 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 10 via crossover
hypothesisA self-cross of champion-adjacent 12db578f6d9a (6.387) tests whether reducing rear sweep and widening the plate can tighten the frame's footprint for a small efficiency gain.
methodReduced rear sweep from about 38° to 35° and widened the plate from ×1.05 to ×1.09, with a small shift back toward cf_plate material.
resultInvalid: rotor disks overlap, tip clearance violated. Pulling rear sweep down that far closes the angle between rear rotors faster than the wider plate can compensate for, putting adjacent rotor disks into collision. Lesson: rear sweep below roughly 36-37° on this arm-length lineage isn't viable without also lengthening the arms. Next: try the same plate widening but hold rear sweep near 38° or increase arm length to preserve clearance.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

energy score6.412 Wh/km
scenario mean3.953 Wh/km
worst scenario4.919 Wh/km
frame mass108.1 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 6 via crossover
hypothesisWith d60afe7b910e having just shown thinner arms pay off, and 50e6dee87d3a (6.466) still the most efficient base geometry, the bet was to fuse 71f9b10676b2's (6.595) thin-arm trend with 50e6dee87d3a's proven sweep/gap tuning.
methodTrimmed arm length slightly (×1.13→×1.01), thinned the arms further (4.6mm→4.0mm), and left plate length and front sweep essentially unchanged.
result6.412 Wh/km — another NEW WINNER, beating the just-set 6.461 mark from d60afe7b910e. Shorter, thinner arms combined with 50e6dee87d3a's sweep geometry improved every scenario, most notably storm (4.92) and calm_warm (3.39). Worth testing: hold arm thickness at 4.0mm and sweep arm length down further to find where the strength margin starts to break.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.454 Wh/km
scenario mean3.978 Wh/km
worst scenario4.951 Wh/km
frame mass106.7 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 7 via crossover
hypothesisBoth parents were solid mid-6.4s performers; this cross tests whether slimming the arm width further and adding a slight battery tilt can trim drag beyond what either parent achieved alone.
methodNarrowed arm width to 75% (from 86%), tilted the battery wedge up to 1.9° from flat, opened the deck gap marginally to 31.2mm, and pulled rear sweep in slightly to 34°.
result6.454 Wh/km — the best result of this generation, beating both parents (6.466, 6.487), but still short of the 6.412 best-so-far. The narrower arm cut drag evenly across every scenario, including the gen's best storm figure (4.95), suggesting slim arm width plus a small battery tilt is a productive direction. Next: push arm width narrower still (below 0.75x) while holding everything else fixed, to see if drag keeps falling before hitting a structural limit.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.478 Wh/km
scenario mean3.993 Wh/km
worst scenario4.971 Wh/km
frame mass113.1 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 8 via crossover
hypothesisThis was the mirror cross of the same two parents (champion 6.412 and weak 6.582), betting that keeping the champion as primary parent while only lightly borrowing the weak parent's deck gap and plate thickness would preserve most of the champion's efficiency.
methodDeck gap widened marginally to 32.8 mm, plate thickness increased sharply by 44% versus the champion, and arm width/length nudged up a few percent.
resultScored 6.478 -- worse than the 6.412 champion but notably better than the 6.582 weak parent, showing the champion's genetic contribution dominated this cross even with a much heavier plate riding along. The large plate-thickness increase didn't pay for itself, though it also didn't break anything; a tighter version with plate thickness held near the champion's own value (rather than +44%) is the natural next probe.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.483 Wh/km
scenario mean3.996 Wh/km
worst scenario4.974 Wh/km
frame mass108.6 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 8 via mutation
hypothesisComing off a solid 6.461 parent, this mutation probed whether opening up the deck gap substantially would relieve some structural margin and let material fraction rise for free stiffness.
methodDeck gap widened sharply from 30.1 mm to 38.3 mm, material fraction raised from 0.04 to 0.17, plate width trimmed slightly, and rear sweep shallowed to 34.0 degrees.
resultCame in at 6.483, slightly worse than the 6.461 parent and short of the 6.412 best-so-far. The big deck-gap opening likely added unneeded frontal height/mass without an offsetting benefit, since none of the per-scenario numbers improved meaningfully over the parent. Worth testing a much smaller deck-gap increase (32-33 mm) with the same material bump to see if a gentler version pays off.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.491 Wh/km
scenario mean4.001 Wh/km
worst scenario4.981 Wh/km
frame mass109.5 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 8 via crossover
hypothesisSelf-crossing the 6.454 parent with itself was a low-risk probe into whether a slightly wider arm waist and shallower front sweep, isolated from any other change, could nudge the same design forward.
methodArm waist loosened from 0.55x to 0.65x and front sweep shallowed by about 1.5 degrees; every other gene stayed at the parent's values.
resultScored 6.491, a step backward from the 6.454 parent, meaning the waist/sweep tweak alone was a net negative rather than a refinement. This isolates the effect cleanly: the parent's original tighter waist was doing useful work, so future front-sweep experiments should keep arm waist locked at 0.55x.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.495 Wh/km
scenario mean4.003 Wh/km
worst scenario4.984 Wh/km
frame mass111.3 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 8 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing a mid-pack flyer with a weaker one (6.654) was a gamble that the weaker parent's wider plate geometry could be salvaged by borrowing the stronger parent's thinner arms and lower material fraction elsewhere.
methodPlate width expanded 9% beyond the fitter parent, material fraction jumped from 0.01 to 0.15, plate length trimmed slightly, and arm thickness thinned back to 4.0 mm.
resultScored 6.495, splitting the difference between its parents but not beating either outright and nowhere near the 6.412 champion. The wider, denser plate seems to have canceled out the thinner-arm savings. A cleaner test would isolate the arm-thickness change alone against the fitter parent, without also widening the plate.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.503 Wh/km
scenario mean4.007 Wh/km
worst scenario4.992 Wh/km
frame mass111.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 8 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing two closely-matched parents (6.484, 6.483) tested whether adding a slight battery wedge tilt plus a wider deck gap could improve stability in gusty/crosswind scenarios enough to justify the added structure.
methodDeck gap widened from 30.1 to 33.3 mm, battery wedge tilted to 1.0 degrees from near-flat, material fraction raised slightly, and rear sweep steepened marginally.
resultScored 6.503, worse than both parents and off the 6.412 best-so-far, with crosswind essentially flat (4.01) rather than improved -- so the battery wedge tilt bought nothing in the scenario it targeted. Given two parents this close still regressed, the deck-gap widen and wedge tilt together look like pure overhead; next worth testing the wedge tilt alone against an unmodified deck gap to isolate its effect.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.504 Wh/km
scenario mean4.008 Wh/km
worst scenario4.991 Wh/km
frame mass112.2 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 8 via crossover
hypothesisWith both parents clustered near 6.48-6.45, the bet was that a slightly longer, denser main plate (more material fraction) would add stiffness worth its weight, while deck gap and arm width stayed essentially untouched.
methodPlate length grown about 3% beyond the fitter parent, material fraction raised from 0.12 to 0.15, and deck gap nudged up half a millimeter; arm width left unchanged.
resultLanded at 6.504, worse than both parents and well off the 6.412 best-so-far, so no new winner. The extra plate material and length likely just added mass/drag without a matching aerodynamic payoff -- storm scenario (4.99) stayed the dominant cost, unmoved. Next worth trying: keep the material bump but pair it with a length cut back toward the fitter parent's baseline to isolate whether length or material fraction is the drag.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.510 Wh/km
scenario mean4.012 Wh/km
worst scenario4.996 Wh/km
frame mass113.8 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 8 via crossover
hypothesisBoth parents were competitive (6.491, 6.461), so this cross tried thickening the arms and widening them slightly while also boosting the rear sweep, betting that more arm robustness would pay off in the storm and crosswind scenarios.
methodArm width raised toward parity (0.99x), arm length grown 3%, material fraction raised slightly, and plate thickness held near the parents' level with rear sweep pushed toward 43 degrees.
resultFinished at 6.51, worse than both parents and the worst crosswind/storm combo of this batch (4.0 / 5.0). The added arm bulk seems to have cost more in drag than it returned in robustness. A more promising next step is isolating just the rear-sweep change without the arm-width increase, since the previous generation's champion already uses a similar sweep.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

frame mass113.4 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 8 via crossover
hypothesisCombining a mid-pack flyer (6.491) with the champion (6.412) tested whether loosening the arm waist and thickening the plate could add robustness to the champion's already-efficient base without giving up much drag.
methodPlate width trimmed to near parity with the champion, arm waist loosened from 0.55x to 0.67x, plate thickness increased 6% beyond the non-champion parent, and arm width nudged up slightly.
resultCame in at 6.528, worse than both parents and well off the champion's 6.412. The looser arm waist plus thicker plate added drag across every scenario rather than the intended robustness gain, echoing candidate 73a3de639945's lesson that this champion lineage prefers the tighter 0.55x waist. Next worth testing the plate-thickness increase alone, with arm waist left at the champion's original 0.55x.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

frame mass120.8 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 8 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing a weak parent (6.582) with the champion (6.412) bet that borrowing the champion's tighter plate proportions could rescue the weaker design's oversized plate footprint.
methodPlate length grown a further 8% beyond the weaker parent's already-large size, plate thickness increased 9%, and rear sweep steepened to 39.2 degrees while plate width trimmed slightly.
resultScored 6.63, worse than both parents and the weakest result of this generation -- every scenario cost rose, led by storm at 5.09. The extra plate length and thickness clearly compounded rather than offset the weak parent's mass penalty; this lineage (9d30a0e485e1's oversized plate) looks like a dead end unless plate length is cut, not grown, in future crosses.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

statusinvalid — rotor too close to deck/battery
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass117.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 8 via crossover
hypothesisBoth parents sat just above 6.45, so this cross bet that stiffening the deck plate and tightening the whole airframe (shorter deck gap, shallower sweeps) would shave a bit more drag without touching the still-strong arm geometry.
methodPlate thickness increased about 23%, deck gap tightened from 30.1 mm to 24.2 mm, and both front and rear sweep angles pulled in by 1-2 degrees relative to the parents.
resultInvalid: the tightened deck gap brought the rotor disk into collision with the deck/battery stack. This is the second time this generation a deck-gap cut below ~26 mm has killed a candidate, so the safe floor for deck gap looks like it sits closer to 28-30 mm; worth testing a milder deck-gap trim (27-28 mm) paired with the thicker plate to see if the thermal/rigidity gain survives without the clearance violation.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
statusinvalid — plate web too thin (plate_main 1.23 mm, stock 1.90 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass148.3 g
materialpetg
borngeneration 8 via crossover
hypothesisOne parent was itself invalid and the other only mid-pack (6.49), so this was an exploratory cross testing whether switching to PETG with thinner, waisted arms could unlock a lighter frame class distinct from the carbon-fiber lineage that has dominated so far.
methodArm thickness reduced from 8.9 mm to 7.5 mm, front sweep shallowed from 41.2 to 35.7 degrees, arm waist loosened slightly, and the whole frame switched material from carbon-fiber plate to PETG.
resultInvalid: the main plate's web came out at only 1.23 mm against a structural minimum of 1.90 mm -- PETG's lower stiffness needed a thicker plate to compensate, and this genome didn't carry one. This confirms the material switch can't be paired blindly with carbon-fiber-tuned plate thickness genes; next worth trying PETG again but with plate thickness scaled up by roughly 50% to hit the wall-thickness floor.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 8 via crossover
hypothesisSame two solid parents as candidate 7d41c5bf7fcb (6.491, 6.461) were recombined a second way, this time betting that shrinking the deck gap and softening both sweep angles together would trim drag.
methodPlate width trimmed back toward the weaker parent, deck gap cut from 30.1 mm to 25.7 mm, and rear sweep shallowed from 43.2 to 40.6 degrees while front sweep increased slightly.
resultInvalid: rotor too close to deck/battery, the same failure mode as f47a31a3f356 earlier in this generation. Two independent crosses now confirm ~25-26 mm deck gap is inside the collision zone for this arm-length class; the safe experiment is to keep the sweep changes but leave deck gap at or above the 30 mm baseline.

statusinvalid — arm root tongues collide on the main plate
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass105.1 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 8 via crossover
hypothesisPairing a solid performer (6.461) with the generation's best-so-far champion (6.412) was a direct attempt to fold the champion's front-sweep and material choices into a slightly longer-armed frame.
methodFront sweep shallowed to 30.0 degrees, arm length grown 14% versus the champion's shorter arms, plate width trimmed slightly, and material fraction dropped to the champion's near-zero level.
resultInvalid: arm root tongues collide on the main plate. The longer arms combined with the champion's shallow front sweep left insufficient clearance at the arm-to-plate joint, a geometric interaction neither parent alone exposed. This suggests arm length and front sweep are coupled genes here; next worth testing the champion's exact sweep with only a modest 5-6% arm-length increase to find where the collision threshold actually sits.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

statusinvalid — rotor disks overlap (tip clearance)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass109.4 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 8 via crossover
hypothesisSelf-crossing the champion (6.412) with itself probed whether shallowing rear sweep and widening the plate slightly, isolated from any other change, could squeeze out a further gain from the best design so far.
methodRear sweep pulled in from 37.6 to 34.0 degrees, plate width widened 3%, and material fraction lowered slightly relative to the champion's baseline.
resultInvalid: rotor disks overlap (tip clearance). This is the third clearance failure this generation and pinpoints a specific coupling: the champion's rear sweep of ~37-38 degrees is apparently close to the minimum needed for rotor tip clearance at this arm length, so shallowing it to 34 degrees pushes disks into contact. Next worth testing rear sweep at an intermediate 36 degrees to map exactly where the clearance boundary sits.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

frame mass107.5 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 6 via crossover
hypothesisf9862e99b160 (6.528) and 71f9b10676b2 (6.595) were both mid-pack, but each carried one promising trait; the bet was that combining thinner arm stock with a lighter plate could finally undercut the plateaued 6.466 leader.
methodThinned the arm tubing (4.3mm→4.0mm), reduced plate thickness (×1.10→×0.96), grew plate length slightly (×1.07→×1.13), and nudged the material blend fraction slightly (still carbon-fiber plate).
result6.461 Wh/km — a NEW WINNER, edging past the prior best-so-far of 6.466 and both parents. The combination of thinner arms and a lighter plate cut weight everywhere, with the biggest gain in storm (4.96), well below either parent's storm number. Worth testing: push arm thickness slightly below 4.0mm to see if structural margin still holds under storm loads.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

scenario mean3.995 Wh/km
worst scenario4.975 Wh/km
frame mass110.8 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 7 via crossover
hypothesisUsing the same parent pair as the sibling that failed on deck-gap clearance, this cross instead widens the deck gap moderately rather than tightening it, testing whether extra clearance margin combined with more composite material improves robustness without costing efficiency.
methodOpened deck gap slightly to 31.3mm (from 29.0mm), raised the material blend fraction to 0.12 (from 0.07), narrowed arm width marginally to 87%, and nudged front sweep up to 32.0°.
result6.483 Wh/km — a small, valid improvement over both parents (6.487, 6.528), still behind the 6.412 best-so-far but a clean incremental gain. Moderate deck-gap widening bought clearance margin without a real efficiency penalty, unlike its sibling's aggressive tightening which failed outright. Next: edge the material blend fraction a bit higher still, since this small bump seemed to help, before touching other genes.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

energy score6.484 Wh/km

worst scenario4.978 Wh/km
frame mass109.7 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 7 via crossover
hypothesisBoth parents sit near the top of the current elite cluster; this cross tests whether widening the arm slightly and stretching the plate further, while flattening front sweep, can push past them.
methodWidened arm to 94% (from 87%), flattened front sweep to 31.4° (from 33.2°), stretched plate length to 116% (from 113%), and shifted material fully to cf_plate.
result6.484 Wh/km — a small regression against both parents (6.461, 6.466) rather than a breakthrough, and still off the 6.412 best-so-far. The wider arm and longer plate likely added marginal mass the flatter sweep didn't offset. Teaches that this elite cluster's arm width and plate length are already close to a local optimum. Next: flatten front sweep alone against the unmodified parent genome to isolate whether sweep angle by itself is beneficial.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.490 Wh/km
