worst scenario4.981 Wh/km
frame mass111.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 7 via crossover
hypothesisWith both parents already near-identical high performers, this cross makes a minimal single-trait tweak — a whisper of battery tilt — to test whether fine-grained wedge adjustment offers a free efficiency gain on an otherwise-converged genome.
methodKept every gene at the shared parent baseline and introduced only a barely-perceptible 0.2° battery wedge, up from perfectly flat.
result6.49 Wh/km — essentially flat versus both parents (6.487, 6.466), landing in between with no meaningful change, and still short of the 6.412 best-so-far. Confirms the battery wedge is near-irrelevant at these small magnitudes on this frame — the genome is converging and micro-mutations here aren't moving the needle. Next: instead of micro-tweaking wedge angle, try a larger step on a less-explored gene in this cluster, like material blend or plate thickness.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

worst scenario4.982 Wh/km
frame mass112.7 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 7 via crossover
hypothesisBoth parents sat in the mid-6.5s with long arms and moderate rear sweep; this cross bets that pulling the arm back toward stock length while opening rear sweep and widening the plate can cut mass and drag together.
methodShortened arm length to near-stock (from the longer parent's 119%), opened rear sweep to 43° (from 36°), and widened the plate to 115% while trimming plate length back near stock.
result6.491 Wh/km — beats both parents (6.528, 6.539) but remains short of the 6.412 best-so-far, so no new winner. The shorter arm paired with more rear sweep likely cut mass without the drag penalty a longer arm carried. Worth testing: keep this shorter arm length but dial rear sweep back toward 36-38° to see if a less extreme angle captures more gain without any storm-scenario cost.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

materialcf_plate
borngeneration 7 via crossover
hypothesisBoth parents were stuck in the mid-6.5s with slightly oversized plates; this cross probes whether stretching plate length further while narrowing plate width redistributes mass toward better aero balance.
methodLengthened the plate to 112% (from 107%) while trimming width back to stock (100%, from 103%), and nudged arm width marginally wider to 106%.
result6.537 Wh/km — essentially a wash between the two parents (6.528, 6.539) and nowhere near the 6.412 best-so-far. Stretching plate length while narrowing width appears to cancel out, netting no real gain. Teaches that plate aspect-ratio tweaks alone, without touching arm geometry, won't move this lineage off its plateau. Next: try shrinking overall plate length back toward stock instead of stretching it, since none of the frontrunners carry plates this long.

frame mass117.3 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 7 via crossover
hypothesisBoth parents were near the elite cluster of long, thin-armed frames; this cross tests whether nudging both sweep angles wider and thickening the arm slightly buys extra stiffness margin without hurting drag.
methodBumped front sweep to 36.3° and rear sweep to 37.8° (both wider than either parent), thickened the arm to 4.6mm, and shifted the material blend fully to cf_plate.
result6.582 Wh/km — worse than both parents (6.461, 6.487) and well off the 6.412 best-so-far, with every scenario degrading (storm rose to 5.05). This shows the elite cluster is already near a local optimum for arm thickness and sweep — widening both at once overshoots it. Next: vary only one of front/rear sweep at a time from the parent baseline to isolate which change actually hurts.

frame mass118.7 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 7 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing the current best-so-far frame with a weaker wide-plate lineage aims to test whether injecting some of the weaker parent's traits — shorter plate, wider deck gap — still preserves the champion's efficiency edge.
methodShrank plate length to 98% (from the champion parent's 107%), widened the deck gap notably to 33.5mm (from 29.4mm), relaxed the arm waist taper to 77% (from 70%), and shifted the material blend slightly.
result6.654 Wh/km — worse than both parents, including the 6.412 best-so-far it was crossed from, confirming the champion's tight 29.4mm deck gap and pinched arm waist are load-bearing traits, not incidental choices. Every scenario degraded (storm rose to 5.11). Next: recombine with the champion but vary only one trait at a time, starting with deck gap alone, to find which single change is most damaging.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

materialcf_plate
borngeneration 7 via crossover
hypothesisA second attempt at crossing the champion with the weaker wide-arm parent, this time testing whether thickening the arm and widening the plate can offset the deck-gap widening that hurt the previous sibling cross.
methodWidened the plate to 113%, thickened the arm to 4.8mm (from the champion's 4.0mm), widened the deck gap to 32.3mm (again away from the champion's 29.4mm), and flattened front sweep slightly to 30.1°.
result6.802 Wh/km — the worst result of the generation, degrading further than its sibling cross and far worse than both parents, with every scenario worsening in lockstep (storm hit 5.22). This confirms that moving deck gap away from the champion's tight 29.4mm value is consistently costly, and thickening the arm on top compounds the loss. Next: stop perturbing deck gap in this lineage — hold it fixed near 29-30mm and vary only plate or arm dimensions.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — arm resonance with rotor 1P
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass166.4 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 7 via immigrant
hypothesisWith the population plateaued in the mid-6.4-6.5 range on thin, long cf_plate arms, this immigrant probes the opposite corner: a stubbier, much thicker-armed frame with wide sweep angles and a steep battery wedge, betting that extra arm mass and plate area might unlock a different efficiency regime.
methodFresh random genome: arm shortened to 87% length but thickened to 7.7mm with a pinched waist, front/rear sweep both pushed out to 45-46°, plate enlarged to 108-110%, deck gap opened to 41.9mm, and a steep 10.8° battery wedge, cast in carbon-fiber plate.
resultKilled before flight — the short, thick-arm combination sits directly on a 1P rotor resonance frequency. This shows that thickening arms while shortening them shifts the natural frequency straight into the rotor's forcing band, a hazard the current thin-arm elites avoid by construction. Next: try a similarly thick 7-8mm arm at a longer length or different waist taper to detune from 1P before pairing it with wide sweep angles.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

statusinvalid — rotor disks overlap (tip clearance)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass123.4 g
materialpetg
borngeneration 7 via immigrant
hypothesisWith elite lineages clustered around 33° sweep and 4mm-thin arms, this immigrant tests whether a much thicker PETG arm at near-elite sweep angles trades some efficiency for durability margin.
methodNear-stock arm length with 34° sweep front and rear (matching the elite range), but arm width/waist trimmed while thickness roughly doubled to 7.6mm, printed in PETG instead of carbon fiber, with a 36.8mm deck gap.
resultKilled before flight — the thick 7.6mm arm at 34° sweep pushes the rotor disks close enough together to overlap, violating tip clearance. This shows sweep angle interacts with arm thickness and deck gap on prop clearance, not just arm length alone. Next: retry a similarly thick PETG arm with a wider deck gap or shallower sweep to restore clearance margin.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

statusinvalid — arm tongue bolts miss the main plate
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass144.2 g
materialpet_cf
borngeneration 7 via immigrant
hypothesisTo counter the plateau around thin 4mm cf_plate arms, this immigrant tests an aggressively swept, short, thick-armed PETG frame with a tight deck gap, probing a structurally opposite region of the design space.
methodFresh random genome: arm shortened to 88% but thickened to 7.8mm and widened to 116%, both sweep angles pushed past 50°, plate shrunk in length but widened, deck gap tightened to 23.7mm, and a steep 9.9° battery wedge, all in PETG.
resultKilled before flight — the extreme sweep angles combined with the shortened, tightened geometry move the arm's mounting tongue clear of the main plate's bolt pattern, a manufacturing failure rather than a performance one. Teaches that sweep angles above roughly 45° need a compensating plate width or deck-gap adjustment to keep the bolt pattern aligned. Next: retry a similarly swept design but scale plate width in proportion to sweep angle so the tongue-bolt alignment survives.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

frame mass115.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 7 via crossover
hypothesisBoth parents were solid mid-6.5s performers; this cross tests whether tightening the deck gap further, past the champion's roughly 29.4mm baseline, can shave more mass and drag while widening the arm slightly for stiffness.
methodTightened deck gap to 27.7mm (from 30.1mm), widened arm width to 96% (from 87%), trimmed plate length to 104%, and nearly matched rear sweep at 35.8°.
resultKilled before flight — tightening the deck gap to 27.7mm brings the rotor disk too close to the deck/battery stack, violating clearance. This sets a lower bound on deck gap, showing the champion's roughly 29.4mm setting is already near the safe minimum rather than an arbitrary choice. Next: test deck gap values between 28-29mm to pin down exactly where the clearance boundary sits.

frame mass170.5 g
materialpetg
borngeneration 7 via immigrant
hypothesisProbing yet another far corner of the design space, this immigrant tests a long, very wide, thick-armed frame with a tightly pinched waist and steep battery wedge, betting that extra arm mass and width could buy stiffness margin the plateaued elites lack.
methodFresh random genome: arm lengthened to 116% and widened dramatically to 133% with a tight waist pinch and heavy 8.9mm thickness, both sweep angles near 41-43°, plate stretched in length but narrowed in width and nominally thickened, with a steep 8.9° battery wedge, all in PETG.
resultKilled before flight — despite the plate thickness gene being scaled up, the resulting load-bearing web measured only 1.24mm versus the 1.90mm stock minimum, showing the narrowed plate width and pinched arm waist interact with the thickness gene in a way that leaves the web too thin. Teaches that plate thickness can't be evaluated in isolation from plate width and waist geometry. Next: retest a similarly heavy-arm design with plate width closer to stock to confirm the web-thinning was driven by the width squeeze rather than the thickness gene itself.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 5 via crossover
hypothesisA champion-tier parent (6.552) was crossed with an untested candidate, betting there was still headroom left in the champion lineage for marginal thinning after a good run this generation.
methodThinned the arm walls slightly, narrowed arm width a touch, trimmed rear sweep marginally, and shortened arm length slightly.
resultScored 6.466 -- a NEW WINNER, beating every prior best-so-far this generation including the 6.528 mark. All six scenarios improved by a small uniform margin, suggesting the combined trims cut mass/drag broadly without hitting a structural floor. Worth continuing to thin arm thickness incrementally from 4.1 mm while watching for the point it becomes structurally invalid.

borngeneration 6 via crossover
hypothesis89bafd954020 (6.547) was crossed with e69646c004a3, a candidate that had itself been invalid; the bet was that 89bafd954020's genes could still rescue a viable, competitive offspring from that pairing.
methodWidened the arm slightly (×0.87→×0.98), shortened the plate back toward parity (×1.07→×1.00), reduced plate thickness (×1.15→×1.05), and pulled front sweep back (31.7°→30.0°).
resultValid at 6.487, an improvement over parent 89bafd954020 but still short of the 6.412 best-so-far. The rescue worked in the sense of producing a viable, mid-pack frame, showing 89bafd954020's genes dominate this cross. Worth testing: cross 89bafd954020 with the new 056184de8923 winner now that a stronger partner exists.

borngeneration 6 via crossover
hypothesis50e6dee87d3a (6.466) and 89bafd954020 (6.547) were paired on the bet that a very small arm-width and deck-gap nudge from the weaker parent might still improve on the stronger parent alone.
methodWidened the arm marginally (×0.86→×0.89) and tightened the deck gap slightly (30.2mm→29.0mm), with arm thickness essentially unchanged.
result6.487, nearly identical to 0b455cc0f748 and worse than the 6.412 best-so-far. The tiny gene nudges barely moved fitness, suggesting this region of the search space is fairly flat around 50e6dee87d3a's configuration. Worth testing: a larger-magnitude mutation on arm width or deck gap from this same base, since micro-nudges aren't differentiating outcomes.

borngeneration 5 via crossover
hypothesisOne of the tied-best parents (6.547) was self-crossed, essentially a fine-tuning mutation, betting that a full switch to the lighter plate material plus small sweep and thickness tweaks could edge past the current best.
methodSwitched material fully to cf_plate, increased front sweep slightly, and thinned the plate a bit.
resultScored 6.528 -- a NEW WINNER, beating the prior best-so-far of 6.547. Every scenario improved by a small, uniform margin, suggesting the full material switch plus modest thinning cut mass/drag broadly rather than fixing one weather condition. Worth pushing plate thickness down and front sweep up further from this new baseline.

energy score6.539 Wh/km
scenario mean4.030 Wh/km
worst scenario5.020 Wh/km
frame mass114.8 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 6 via crossover
hypothesisf9862e99b160 (6.528) and 50e6dee87d3a (6.466, the pool's best) were close in fitness; the bet was that splitting the difference on arm geometry could land between them or edge past the leader.
methodWidened the arms slightly (×0.87→×1.04), shortened them a touch (×1.19→×1.11), and trimmed plate width (×1.06→×1.03).
resultCame in at 6.539, between the two parents but still short of the 6.466 best-so-far, so no new winner. Widening the arm while shortening it seems to have been a wash — extra frontal area offset the weight saved from the shorter arm. Worth testing: the shorter arm length paired with the original narrower arm width instead.

borngeneration 6 via crossover
hypothesis71f9b10676b2 (6.595) and 89bafd954020 (6.547) hadn't yet tried a narrower arm width together; the bet was that combining the parents' shared thick-plate profile with a slimmer arm might claw back some efficiency.
methodNarrowed arm width (×0.85→×0.75), widened arm waist (×0.55→×0.64), and shaved plate length slightly (×0.97→×0.96) while nudging plate width up.
result6.586, between the two parents but well short of the fresh 6.412 best-so-far. The waist widening likely added back drag that the narrower arm width was supposed to save. Worth testing: keep the narrow arm width but leave arm waist untouched, to isolate whether waist or width drove the regression.

borngeneration 6 via crossover
hypothesisc9cf7c11f59f (6.547) and 50e6dee87d3a (6.466) were combined on the bet that adding battery wedge to the leader's proven geometry might rebalance weight and cut a scenario or two's losses.
methodAdded a battery wedge (0°→4.8°), thickened the plate (×1.05→×1.24), and pulled rear sweep back slightly (35.5°→34.0°).
result6.631, worse than either parent and the 6.412 best-so-far. The battery wedge plus thicker plate added mass without a clear payoff, with crosswind (4.08) and storm (5.09) both elevated. Worth testing: battery wedge in isolation, without the plate-thickness increase, since wedge alone hasn't been tested cleanly on this base geometry.

metricvalue
energy score6.788 Wh/km
scenario mean4.183 Wh/km
worst scenario5.210 Wh/km
frame mass132.6 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 6 via crossover
hypothesisParents d3770c40a877 (6.692) and f9862e99b160 (6.528) both carried extra plate margin; the bet was that combining the sturdier parent's mass distribution with the lighter parent's arm geometry might trim drag while keeping stiffness.
methodIncreased plate thickness further (×1.16→×1.44), added a slight battery wedge (0°→2.9°), swept the rear arms further back (36.6°→41.3°), and tightened the deck gap (35.2mm→31.1mm).
resultLanded at 6.788 Wh/km, worse than both parents and far off the 6.466 best-so-far — no new winner. The extra plate mass and steeper rear sweep likely added drag/weight across all six scenarios without a compensating benefit, worst in storm (5.21). Worth testing: isolate the rear-sweep change alone, without the plate-thickness increase, to find which gene is the actual culprit.

borngeneration 6 via crossover
hypothesis412f5906d4ae (6.873) was a weaker parent but contributed thicker arms and wider plates; the bet was that pairing its structural mass with 89bafd954020's (6.547) better wedge/gap tuning could offset the weak parent's penalty.
methodWidened the deck gap (31.0mm→36.2mm), pulled back plate width slightly (×1.22→×1.15), increased battery wedge (1.5°→3.3°), and grew plate length (×1.15→×1.16).
result6.881, worse than both parents and the 6.466 best-so-far. The heavy-arm parent's penalty wasn't rescued by the gap/wedge tuning — storm (5.28) was hit hardest, suggesting the thicker arm stock simply adds too much mass. Worth testing: keep 89bafd954020's thinner arms and only borrow this cross's deck-gap/wedge values.

metricvalue
energy score6.943 Wh/km
scenario mean4.277 Wh/km
worst scenario5.332 Wh/km
frame mass142.4 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 6 via designer
hypothesisLearning from the two overly-heavy designer attempts already tried this generation, this bet moderated arm thickness while pushing sweep angles more aggressively, testing whether sweep geometry alone could unlock efficiency without full arm-thickness commitment.
methodBuilt from scratch with moderate arms (5.5mm), steep sweeps (front 38.0°, rear 43.0°), larger plates (×1.20 length, ×1.18 width), and no plate-thickness surplus (×1.00).
result6.943, better than the other two designer attempts this generation but still well behind the 6.412 best-so-far and every viable crossover. Moderating arm thickness helped, but the very steep 43° rear sweep likely still added drag, particularly visible in storm (5.33). Worth testing: the same plate/sweep profile but with arm thickness dropped to the ~4mm range crossovers have shown works best.

metricvalue
energy score7.262 Wh/km
scenario mean4.471 Wh/km
worst scenario5.581 Wh/km
frame mass166.9 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 6 via designer
hypothesisWith the population plateaued near 6.4-6.5, the designer proposed a deliberately heavier, stiffer frame betting that raw structural robustness might reduce storm-scenario losses enough to offset the weight penalty elsewhere.
methodBuilt from scratch with thick arms (6.0mm), enlarged plates (×1.15 length, ×1.13 width, ×1.20 thickness), and steep sweeps (front 36.5°, rear 41.0°).
result7.262, far worse than the 6.412 best-so-far and every crossover candidate this generation. The extra structural mass dominated every scenario, including storm (5.58), which was supposed to be its strength. Lesson: this problem is weight-limited, not strength-limited, at the fitness levels already reached. Worth testing: a designer proposal that keeps arms thin (~4mm) but explores an untried gene combination like an extreme sweep angle.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

frame mass173.8 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 6 via designer
hypothesisBuilding on the same stiffness bet as the prior designer entry, this went even further on arm thickness plus added battery wedge, hypothesizing the wedge could compensate for weight via better distribution in headwind/storm.
methodBuilt from scratch with thick arms (6.5mm), moderate sweeps (34°/38°), and a 3.0° battery wedge.
result7.377, the worst result of the generation, well below the 6.412 best-so-far. Thicker arms than even the previous designer attempt made things worse, confirming the weight-limited hypothesis from 7b8317ed444b. Worth testing: designer proposals should start from the current best-so-far's arm thickness (~4mm) rather than exploring the heavy end of the range.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — rotor too close to deck/battery
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass113.5 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 6 via crossover
hypothesis50e6dee87d3a (6.466) was self-paired to explore its own local neighborhood, betting that a steeper front sweep and tighter deck gap could squeeze out more efficiency from an already-strong design.
methodIncreased front sweep (31.5°→35.9°), shrank the deck gap substantially (30.2mm→25.7mm), and thickened the plate (×1.06→×1.21).
resultInvalid — rotor too close to deck/battery. Tightening the deck gap by 4.5mm crossed the rotor clearance constraint, so this design never flew. Lesson: deck gap has a hard floor near 26-28mm for this frame family and shouldn't be shrunk alongside a front-sweep increase. Worth testing: the front-sweep increase alone, without touching deck gap.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass108.4 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 6 via crossover
hypothesis50e6dee87d3a (6.466) and f9862e99b160 (6.528) were bred on the bet that a longer arm plus tighter deck gap could reduce drag while keeping the proven rear-sweep value.
methodShrank the deck gap sharply (30.2mm→22.2mm), lengthened the arm (×1.18→×1.21), and pulled rear sweep back (35.0°→34.0°).
resultInvalid — deck gap too small for the FC/ESC stack. An 8mm deck-gap cut was too aggressive, echoing the same failure mode as bd9d3434a36d. Lesson: deck gap below roughly 26-28mm collides with onboard electronics regardless of arm-length changes. Worth testing: the arm-length and rear-sweep changes alone, holding deck gap at or above 30mm.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

metricvalue
energy score6.547 Wh/km
scenario mean4.034 Wh/km
worst scenario5.025 Wh/km
frame mass116.4 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 4 via crossover
hypothesisCrossed strong performer 1e1a58d2ae26 (6.552) with solid a76a6566ac11 (6.705), betting that a small extra dose of plate thickness for stiffness margin, paired with slightly thinner and narrower-waisted arms, could trim mass without tripping the thin-plate constraint that was killing so many siblings this generation.
methodPlate thickness increased slightly from ×1.06 to ×1.15, arm thickness thinned marginally from 4.5 mm to 4.3 mm, arm width widened a touch, with rear sweep essentially unchanged.
result6.547 Wh/km is a NEW WINNER, edging out the prior best-so-far (held by c9cf7c11f59f, also ~6.547) and beating both parents outright. The win looks like fine-tuning rather than a leap: a slightly heavier plate paired with a slightly lighter, narrower arm nearly cancel in mass while easing local stress enough to dodge the plate-thinness trap that invalidated four other candidates this generation. Storm remains the weak point at 5.03 Wh/km, so worth testing next: push arm thickness down further from this exact base while holding plate thickness at ×1.15.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

frame mass112.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 3 via crossover
hypothesisThe current best-so-far, 1e1a58d2ae26 (6.552), still ran with zero battery tilt and a loose arm waist; crossing it with the weak 15c422327b08 line bets that borrowing a modest battery wedge and a thinner arm wall can trim drag and mass further.
methodAdded a modest battery wedge (0°→4.7°), loosened the arm waist taper (×0.55→×0.71), thinned the arm wall from 4.5 mm to 4.0 mm, and nudged front sweep out (31.5°→32.5°).
resultValid at 6.547 — a NEW WINNER, edging out both the prior best-so-far (6.552) and both parents. The combination of a small battery tilt and a thinner arm wall likely cut drag and mass while the loosened waist taper kept enough stiffness to avoid failure. Worth testing: sweep battery wedge in the 3-6° band around this design to pin down the true optimum.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 5 via crossover
hypothesisThe same pair of champions was crossed the other direction, betting that going fully to the lighter plate material plus a slightly longer plate and less rear sweep would trim drag beyond either parent.
methodSwitched material fully to cf_plate, lengthened the main plate, and reduced rear sweep slightly.
resultCame in at 6.568, close to but not beating either parent or the 6.547 best-so-far. The plate lengthening likely added just enough mass or drag to erase the benefit of the sweep reduction. Worth retrying the material switch and sweep reduction without the plate lengthening.

frame mass121.5 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 5 via crossover
hypothesisThe same champion lineage (6.552) was crossed with an untested candidate, betting that thicker plates combined with a shorter plate length could offset each other's mass and stiffness tradeoffs.
methodThickened the plate substantially, shortened plate length, shortened arm length slightly, and increased front sweep.
resultScored 6.595, worse than its parent and well off the new best of 6.466. The plate thickening added more mass/drag than the length reduction saved, showing this lineage is drag-limited by plate mass rather than helped by compensating length cuts. Worth trying further plate thinning from the new 6.466 champion instead of thickening.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

materialcf_plate
borngeneration 5 via crossover
hypothesisA champion-tier parent (6.552) was crossed with a mid-pack parent (6.654), betting that the champion's slender-arm design could absorb some of the mid-pack parent's wider, thicker plate traits.
methodWidened the plate, thickened it, opened the deck gap slightly, and trimmed rear sweep.
resultScored 6.635, landing between the two parents but worse than the top parent alone. The added plate width and thickness cost more than they gained, confirming this lineage prefers thin, narrow plates. Worth retrying with the champion's original plate dimensions but keeping just the reduced rear sweep.

borngeneration 5 via crossover
hypothesisThe top performer (6.547) was crossed again with the persistently weak 7.139 parent, betting its steeper battery wedge and narrower arms might still combine well with the champion's core.
methodIncreased battery wedge tilt, narrowed the arms, thickened the arm walls slightly, and lengthened the arms.
resultScored 6.637, worse than the champion parent and far from the generation's new best of 6.466. The weak parent's traits diluted the champion lineage's efficiency yet again, confirming that lineage underperforms in every cross this generation. Worth abandoning crosses with this weak parent and focusing recombination within the top-performing lineage instead.

borngeneration 5 via crossover
hypothesisThe top performer (6.547) was crossed with a lagging parent (6.8), betting the champion's core geometry could absorb the laggard's longer arms and wider deck gap without losing much efficiency.
methodLengthened the arms further, widened them slightly, opened the deck gap, and nudged front sweep up marginally.
resultScored 6.692, worse than the champion parent alone. Extending arm length and deck gap added enough mass/drag to erase the champion's edge, confirming its compact arm length was close to optimal already. Worth testing the laggard's front-sweep nudge alone on the champion's unmodified arm length.

borngeneration 5 via mutation
hypothesisA lagging lineage (6.8) was mutated on the bet that opening up the gap between the deck and props was the limiting factor holding back its efficiency.
methodIncreased the deck gap by about 2.6 mm, leaving everything else unchanged.
resultScored 6.811, essentially flat versus the 6.8 parent. This confirms deck gap isn't the bottleneck for this lineage -- the heavier arm waist and thicker plates are the more likely drag/mass drivers. Worth mutating arm waist or plate thickness on this lineage instead.

borngeneration 5 via crossover
hypothesisThe same lagging lineage (6.8) was self-crossed, betting that a wider plate combined with a shallower battery wedge angle would cut drag.
methodWidened the main plate further, reduced battery wedge tilt, loosened the arm waist slightly, and tightened the deck gap.
resultScored 6.873, worse than the 6.8 parent. The wider plate likely added frontal area and mass that outweighed any benefit from the shallower wedge angle. Teaches this lineage doesn't tolerate a wider plate; worth testing a narrower plate paired with just the lower battery wedge angle.

frame mass142.3 g

borngeneration 5 via crossover
hypothesisOne parent was tied for best-so-far while the other lagged badly at 7.139; the bet was that blending them would keep the strong parent's efficiency while nudging sweep and arm proportions further.
methodSlightly reduced rear sweep, tightened the arm waist, trimmed plate width a touch, and lengthened the arms, on a pa12_cf frame.
resultLanded at 7.126 -- almost exactly the weak parent's score and nowhere near the strong parent's 6.547, so no new record. The cross pulled toward the underperforming parent's material and geometry instead of inheriting the good parent's traits. Worth testing the same tweaks on a cf_plate frame instead of pa12_cf.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

frame mass147.4 g
materialpa12_cf
borngeneration 5 via crossover
hypothesisThe weak 7.139 parent was crossed with an untested candidate on the bet that thinner plates plus thicker, less-swept arms could shave weight without sacrificing stiffness.
methodThinned the plates, thickened the arms, reduced rear sweep, and tilted the battery wedge up a bit more.
resultInvalid: the arm root tongues collide on the main plate. The reduced rear sweep combined with the arm changes pushed the tongue geometry into interference with the plate. Teaches that sweep reductions on this lineage need a matching plate footprint change; next worth widening plate length whenever rear sweep drops this much.

borngeneration 5 via crossover
hypothesisA mid-pack performer (6.627) was crossed with an untested candidate, betting that loosening the arm waist and thinning the arms could trim mass further on top of an already aggressive rear-sweep design.
methodWidened the arm waist, thinned the arm walls slightly, increased battery wedge tilt, and lengthened the arms a bit.
resultInvalid: the arm tongue bolts miss the main plate. The extreme rear sweep (over 60 degrees) combined with the longer arms shifted the tongues past the plate's bolt pattern. Teaches this lineage's rear sweep is already near a geometric limit; next worth retrying the same waist/thickness change with rear sweep dialed back toward 45-50 degrees.

borngeneration 5 via crossover
hypothesisTwo nearly tied champions (6.547 and 6.552) were crossed on the bet that shorter, stiffer arms with thicker plates could squeeze out further gains from an already strong design.
methodShortened the arms substantially, thickened the plates a lot, increased battery wedge tilt, and shifted the material blend.
resultInvalid: rotor disks overlap on tip clearance. Shortening the arms past a threshold left too little room between propellers. Teaches arm length has a hard floor set by rotor diameter for this frame; next worth keeping arm length shrink modest while still testing the thicker-plate and battery-wedge combination.

borngeneration 5 via crossover
hypothesisThe weak 7.139 parent was crossed with an untested candidate, betting that aggressively widening the arms and thinning the plates would cut enough mass to overcome its poor baseline.
methodWidened the arms, loosened the arm waist, thinned the plates significantly, and adjusted plate length.
resultInvalid: plates fell below the minimum thickness allowed for pa12_cf material. The thinning combination landed right at the material's structural floor. Teaches pa12_cf has a strict minimum plate thickness that's easy to breach when stacking thinning mutations; worth rerunning the same geometry on cf_plate, which likely tolerates thinner plates.

materialcf_plate
borngeneration 2 via crossover
hypothesisParent 8e06d4306a70 had solid proportions but relatively thick arms; the bet was that a self-cross exploring a much thinner arm wall could shed mass-driven power draw without failing structurally.
methodSelf-crossover cut arm thickness sharply to 4.5 mm from 6.0 mm, while widening the plate about 6% and lengthening it about 7% to compensate.
result6.552 Wh/km — a clear NEW WINNER and the best of the generation, a solid drop from the 6.719 record set earlier. The thinner arms cut power draw in every scenario without tripping a structural invalid, so there's likely still headroom to shed weight; worth pushing arm thickness even lower next, watching for the plate-web-too-thin failure seen elsewhere this generation.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

materialpa12_cf
borngeneration 4 via crossover
hypothesisParent cb6b6c5540f9 (6.719) was solid but not exceptional; paired with a much weaker 09309f9a3ffc (7.79) mainly for diversity, the bet was that a sharply tighter arm waist plus a trimmed sweep/battery tilt could cut mass and drag beyond what either parent achieved alone.
methodArm waist tightened sharply (roughly ×0.81 to ×0.55) while arm width widened slightly, and both front sweep and battery wedge were trimmed a couple of degrees.
result6.627 Wh/km clearly beats both parents (6.719 and 7.79) but still trails the 6.547 best-so-far by about 0.08. The tighter waist likely cut mass without tripping the thin-plate limits that killed several other candidates this generation. Worth testing next: layer this waist-tightening onto the new champion's plate-thickness recipe to see if the gains stack.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 4 via crossover
hypothesisCrossed the incumbent champion c9cf7c11f59f (6.547) with mid-pack a76a6566ac11 (6.705), betting that easing the battery tilt and trimming plate width/arm length slightly would shed a bit more mass from the champion's frame.
methodBattery wedge reduced from 4.0° to 1.7°, plate width trimmed from ×1.12 to ×1.09, and arm length shortened from ×1.05 to ×1.02, with material shifting within cf_plate variants.
result6.654 Wh/km beats the weaker parent but is a clear step down from the champion (6.547), so the trims diluted rather than improved its recipe — reducing battery wedge in particular likely cost some aerodynamic benefit the champion relied on. Next: retry mixing with the champion but hold battery wedge fixed to isolate whether the wedge angle or the plate/arm trims caused the regression.

borngeneration 4 via crossover
hypothesisCrossed two mid-pack performers (a76a6566ac11 6.705, 30f7b0f6b0b7 6.755), betting that thicker plates plus slightly longer arms and reduced rear sweep would combine their separate strengths.
methodPlate thickness increased from ×0.95 to ×1.08, rear sweep reduced from 43.0° to 40.7°, and arm length lengthened from ×1.05 to ×1.08, with material shifting within the cf_plate family.
result6.8 Wh/km is worse than either parent, so the cross compounded their shared mediocrity rather than their strengths — the extra plate mass likely outweighed any drag benefit from the reduced sweep. Next: test the reduced rear sweep alone against a stronger base genome rather than pairing two mid-pack parents.

borngeneration 4 via crossover
hypothesisPaired weak 4ac6230067c7 (7.026) with strong 1e1a58d2ae26 (6.552), betting that trimming plate length, flattening the battery tilt, and thinning the arms would let a lighter pla_plus material compete despite its lower strength margin.
methodPlate length shortened slightly, battery wedge flattened from 2.0° to 0.0°, arm thickness thinned from 7.5 mm to 6.9 mm, plate width trimmed slightly, and material switched to pla_plus.
result6.855 Wh/km beats the weak parent (7.026) but falls well short of the strong parent (6.552) and the 6.547 best-so-far, roughly 0.2-0.3 Wh/km worse across every scenario. The material swap likely forced conservative compensating dimensions that ate the mass savings from the trims. Next: repeat this exact trim recipe in pa12_cf to isolate whether pla_plus itself is the bottleneck.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

materialpa12_cf
borngeneration 4 via crossover
hypothesisCrossed two below-average performers (edbeb4914449 6.977, a76a6566ac11 6.705), betting that substantially lengthening the arms while narrowing width and waist would trade mass for stiffness in a way neither parent had tried.
methodArm length increased sharply from ×1.05 to ×1.23, arm width narrowed slightly, and arm waist tightened slightly, with material held within pa12_cf.
result7.139 Wh/km is worse than both parents and well off the 6.547 best-so-far, roughly 0.2-0.3 Wh/km worse than a76a6566ac11 in every scenario. The long-arm bet backfired, likely adding both mass and drag without compensation. Next: this generation's data suggests arm length beyond roughly ×1.15 consistently hurts fitness — worth testing a length cap around there for future crossovers.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — plates too thin for pa12_cf (1.4 < 1.6 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass105.6 g
materialpa12_cf
borngeneration 4 via crossover
hypothesisBred from a previously non-viable parent (a634b4cbe897, fitness null) alongside solid performer d3bc126ea7b7 (6.687), betting that beefing up arm width/length and plate length while shrinking the deck gap would compound the viable parent's strengths.
methodArm width widened substantially (~×0.80 to ×1.09), plate length increased (~×0.94 to ×1.08), deck gap tightened to 26.3 mm from 30.3 mm, and arm length nudged slightly longer.
resultInvalid: pa12_cf plates came out too thin (1.4 mm vs the 1.6 mm minimum), because plate thickness stayed low while every other dimension grew rather than compensating. This confirms that breeding off a null-fitness parent carries real geometric risk when its thin-plate trait isn't explicitly corrected. Next: pair any low plate-thickness genome with a forced thickness floor before crossover.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 4 via crossover
hypothesisAgain drew on the null-fitness a634b4cbe897 alongside strong performer 1e1a58d2ae26 (6.552), betting that thickening the arms would let the plates stay thin without breaching the pa12_cf minimum.
methodArm thickness increased from 6.1 mm to 7.0 mm, arm width widened, and plate width/thickness were both nudged up slightly (thickness ×0.70 to ×0.75).
resultStill invalid: plate thickness only reached 1.5 mm, just under the 1.6 mm pa12_cf floor. The correction was too small to clear a hard constraint carried by this parent's genome. Next: for offspring inheriting from this parent, clamp plate thickness explicitly to ≥1.6 mm rather than letting crossover nudge it incrementally.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 4 via crossover
hypothesisCrossed mediocre 30f7b0f6b0b7 (6.755) with the incumbent champion c9cf7c11f59f (6.547), betting that shaving the champion's plate mass while sweeping the arms further forward would push fitness even lower.
methodPlate thickness cut sharply from full (×1.00) to ×0.75 and front sweep increased from 34.4° to 38.6°, with arm waist and thickness left almost unchanged.
resultInvalid: the aggressive plate cut dropped straight through the 1.6 mm pa12_cf floor (1.5 mm). This shows the champion's frame has little spare mass in its plates, so further weight savings need to come from arm taper or sweep, not direct plate thinning. Next: retry the sweep increase alone, leaving plate thickness untouched, to isolate its effect.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 4 via crossover
hypothesisSelf-crossed decent performer cb6b6c5540f9 (6.719) as a mutation probe, testing whether pushing sweep and arm length further while tightening the deck gap could squeeze more mass/drag savings out of an already-good frame.
methodFront sweep increased from 34.4° to 39.4°, deck gap tightened from 31.2 mm to 28.0 mm, arm thickness thinned from 6.9 mm to 6.3 mm, and arm length lengthened from ×1.09 to ×1.15.
resultInvalid: rotor too close to deck/battery. The tighter deck gap combined with longer, more-swept arms pushed a rotor into the restricted envelope. This is the second failure this generation tied to a tight deck gap, suggesting ~28 mm is unsafe once arm sweep/length are also pushed. Next: tighten deck gap alone, without increasing sweep or length, to find the real clearance floor.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

statusinvalid — arm tongue bolts miss the main plate
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass156.9 g
materialpet_cf
borngeneration 4 via immigrant
hypothesisA fresh random immigrant injected for diversity, exploring an extreme rear-sweep layout far outside the population's current cluster.
methodRandom genome with rear sweep swept back to 60.9° (roughly double the population norm), a wide 43.6 mm deck gap, moderate arm length and waist, built in pet_cf.
resultInvalid: arm tongue bolts miss the main plate. The extreme rear sweep moved the arm root tongues far enough that their bolt holes no longer land on the plate at all — a pure geometry mismatch rather than a strength/mass failure. This marks ~60° rear sweep as outside the buildable envelope for this plate layout. Next: cap rear sweep mutations below roughly 45-50° for future immigrants to keep them geometrically viable.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

materialasa
borngeneration 4 via immigrant
hypothesisA random immigrant testing the ASA material family, wagering its different stiffness profile might open a favorable mass/strength tradeoff not explored by the pa12_cf/cf_plate-dominated population.
methodFresh random genome with a loosely tapered arm waist (×1.25) and thick plates (×1.31 multiplier) built in ASA, with shorter, narrower arms than the population norm.
resultInvalid: plate web too thin (1.37 mm vs the 1.90 mm ASA requires). Despite the ×1.31 thickness multiplier, ASA's much higher minimum-gauge requirement than pa12_cf's 1.6 mm still wasn't met. Next: if testing ASA again, bias the plate-thickness gene higher specifically for that material to clear its stricter floor.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 4 via crossover
hypothesisSelf-crossed decent performer d3bc126ea7b7 (6.687) as a mutation probe, testing whether tightening the deck gap slightly further could squeeze out more mass savings.
methodDeck gap tightened from 30.2 mm to 28.0 mm, arm waist loosened marginally from ×0.55 to ×0.63, with arm width essentially unchanged.
resultInvalid: rotor too close to deck/battery, the same failure mode as f40d394738b2. Two independent lineages hitting this constraint at a deck gap around 28 mm strongly suggests that's close to the hard clearance floor for this rotor/deck layout regardless of arm geometry. Next: treat ~28 mm as a lower bound and avoid mutating deck gap below roughly 29-30 mm.

borngeneration 4 via crossover
hypothesisCrossed the same two strong parents that produced this generation's winner (1e1a58d2ae26, a76a6566ac11), this time betting that widening the deck gap for extra rotor clearance would allow thicker, wider arms without penalty.
methodDeck gap widened from 30.2 mm to 34.7 mm, arm thickness increased from 4.5 mm to 5.2 mm, arm width widened from ×0.84 to ×0.92, with plate width trimmed slightly.
resultInvalid: arm root tongues collide on the main plate. Widening the deck gap didn't help because the failure was at the arm root/plate junction, not rotor clearance — the thicker, wider arm roots simply ran out of room on the plate footprint. This is the opposite failure mode from the deck-gap-too-tight cases, showing arm root sizing has its own independent packing limit. Next: if thickening arm roots this much again, pair it with increased plate length/width rather than deck gap to give the tongues more room.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 3 via mutation
hypothesis8e06d4306a70 (6.828) was underperforming, likely dragged down by overly long arms; this mutation bets that reeling arm length back in while adding sweep and plate length can recover efficiency.
methodPulled arm length back in (×1.19→×1.06), increased front sweep (31.5°→34.5°), lengthened the plate further (×1.05→×1.10), and slimmed arm width (×0.84→×0.80).
resultValid at 6.687, about 2% better than its parent (6.828), but still short of the best-so-far (6.552). The shorter, more swept arms and longer plate likely trimmed both mass and drag versus the parent's overlong-arm design. Worth testing: apply this same arm-length pullback and sweep bump to a plate-width-optimized parent to see if it can beat 6.552.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 3 via designer
hypothesisGiven the leaders favor light, tapered arms, the designer bets an even more aggressive slim-and-sweep design with enlarged plates can push past the current best by minimizing arm mass while managing sweep-induced drag.
methodHand-designed a slim, swept frame: arms 5% longer but 20% narrower than stock with a slim 5.2 mm wall and tight waist taper, aggressive 37°/43° front/rear sweep, and enlarged plates, in cf_plate.
resultValid at 6.705, the best of the three designer entries and close to the mid-pack crossover results, though still short of the best-so-far. This validates the population's general direction toward slim, tapered arms even with steep sweep. Worth testing: keep this sweep and plate footprint but pull the arm wall down toward the winning line's ~4 mm to see if it closes the remaining gap.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 2 via crossover
hypothesisBoth parents were strong but plateaued near the old best; the bet was that leaning harder into a carbon-fiber-rich material blend while stretching arm and plate length slightly could cut both mass and flex losses at once.
methodCrossover shifted the material mix toward more carbon fiber, lengthened the arms about 9% and the plate about 7%, and thickened the arms slightly to 6.9 mm.
resultLanded at 6.719 Wh/km, beating both parents and becoming a NEW WINNER over the prior best-so-far of 6.807. The extra stiffness from more material and longer arms likely paid off across the gustier scenarios; worth testing an even richer material fraction or slightly longer arms to see if the trend keeps paying.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 3 via mutation
hypothesiscb6b6c5540f9 (6.719) had middling performance; this mutation bets that a longer plate and a slightly eased battery wedge can improve the mass/aerodynamic balance.
methodLengthened the plate substantially (×1.07→×1.20), eased the battery wedge back a touch (10.5°→9.3°), and nudged the material blend slightly lighter within pa12_cf.
resultValid at 6.755, worse than both its parent (6.719) and the best-so-far. The extra plate length likely added mass without an aerodynamic payoff. Worth testing: revert plate length toward the parent's ×1.07 and instead mutate arm geometry, which is where recent wins have come from.

borngeneration 3 via designer
hypothesisWith the population converging around slender, swept-arm designs scoring 6.55-6.8, the designer bets a contrasting stout, thick-walled frame in pa12_cf can test whether raw structural margin beats aerodynamic slimming.
methodHand-designed a stout frame: arms 5% longer and 5% wider than stock with a thick 7.5 mm wall, 33°/38° front/rear sweep, a 34 mm deck gap, 5° battery wedge, in pa12_cf.
resultValid at 6.977, well behind the leaders. The thick walls and wide deck gap add mass the mission profile doesn't reward, confirming the population's bias toward lighter arms is well-founded. Worth testing: keep this sweep/deck-gap layout but shave arm thickness toward 6 mm to isolate how much the extra wall mass is costing.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

materialpla_plus
borngeneration 3 via designer
hypothesisTo probe whether a cheaper, lighter print material can compensate for a heavier, thicker-walled frame, the designer pairs long thick arms with pla_plus, testing if reduced material density offsets structural bulk.
methodHand-designed a heavy-duty frame: arms 15% longer than stock with a thick 7.5 mm wall, 33.5°/39° front/rear sweep, and enlarged plates, in pla_plus.
resultValid at 7.026, the weakest of the three designer entries. pla_plus's lower stiffness likely forced compensating mass elsewhere (thick walls, big plates) that erased any material savings, rather than offsetting the bulk as hoped. Worth testing: rebuild the same geometry in cf_plate or pa12_cf to isolate whether material choice or arm geometry is the bigger drag on this design.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 3 via crossover
hypothesis15c422327b08 (7.622) was the weakest scored parent in the pool; self-crossing it explores whether widening arms and thickening plates in a different material can pull it toward the pack, though doubling down on a weak lineage is a long shot.
methodPulled rear sweep in (39.6°→34.0°), thickened plates further (×1.43→×1.60), widened arms (×1.10→×1.21), and shifted the material blend from pet_cf toward a lighter variant.
resultValid but worse than its own weak parent, at 7.79 versus 7.622. Piling more plate mass and arm width onto an already-weak design only added weight without an offsetting benefit, and the material swap didn't help either. This teaches that this lineage isn't worth further investment. Worth testing: retire this branch and instead cross a fresh design against one of the top-3 parents.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 3 via crossover
hypothesisThe cb6b6c5540f9 line (6.719) was solid but still heavier than the leaders; crossing it with an unscored sibling bets that shorter arms and a leveled battery mount can shave mass without giving up stiffness.
methodShortened arm length by about 19% versus the cb6b6c5540f9 parent, zeroed the battery wedge, pushed rear sweep out from 36.6° to 39.4°, and returned the arm waist to full width (untapered).
resultInvalid: the plate web came out at 0.97 mm against a 1.90 mm stock minimum. Untapering the arm waist while also trimming length pulled material away from the plate junction faster than expected. Worth testing: keep plate thickness bumped up whenever the arm waist is returned toward full width.

borngeneration 3 via crossover
hypothesiscb6b6c5540f9's arm/plate balance still trailed the leaders; blending it with an unscored sibling and pushing battery tilt further bets on finding a tighter arm-waist taper that stays stiff enough.
methodRaised battery wedge from 10.5° to 14.2°, pulled arm length back in (×1.09→×0.97), tightened the arm waist taper (×0.81→×0.72), and trimmed plate width slightly.
resultInvalid: plate web measured 1.44 mm versus the 1.90 mm minimum. Even a moderate waist tightening combined with wedge changes was enough to breach the floor. Worth testing: couple plate thickness upward automatically whenever waist taper drops below roughly ×0.75.

frame mass133.5 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 3 via crossover
hypothesis567a47539bb8 (6.807) was underperforming the leaders, likely from mass concentrated in its plates; this cross bets that trimming plate thickness and narrowing arms further can close the gap.
methodReduced plate thickness less aggressively than the other parent (×0.70→×0.85), narrowed arm width further (×0.86→×0.79), pulled front sweep in slightly (31.5°→30.0°), and shortened arm length a touch (×1.35→×1.31).
resultInvalid: the arm root tongues collided on the main plate. Narrower arms combined with a tighter front sweep crowded the four root attachment points. Worth testing: only narrow arm width when sweep stays wide (above ~32°) to preserve root clearance.

borngeneration 3 via crossover
hypothesis1e1a58d2ae26 is the current best-so-far (6.552); this cross makes only small tweaks to its winning recipe, betting that minor plate and battery refinements can eke out further gains without disturbing what already works.
methodTrimmed plate width slightly (×0.94→×0.93), thickened the arm wall marginally (6.2→6.3 mm), and left plate length and battery wedge essentially unchanged from the champion parent.
resultInvalid: plate web dropped to 0.73 mm against the 1.90 mm minimum. This shows the champion line is already sitting close to its structural floor, so even a small plate-width trim breaks it. Worth testing: leave plate width untouched on this lineage and vary only arm geometry instead.

borngeneration 3 via crossover
hypothesis15c422327b08 (7.622) is the weakest scored parent in the pool; crossing it in while thickening the arms bets that added arm robustness can rescue some of its traits, though shrinking the plates is a risk.
methodRaised battery wedge from 3.1° to 5.1°, shrank plate length and width (×1.00→×0.94, ×0.94→×0.89), and thickened the arm wall from 6.2 mm to 6.7 mm.
resultInvalid: arm root tongues collided on the main plate. Shrinking the plate footprint while thickening arms left no room for the root attachments even with the weak parent's genetics folded in. Worth testing: hold plate footprint fixed whenever arm thickness increases.

materialpa12_cf
borngeneration 3 via crossover
hypothesisThe leader 1e1a58d2ae26 (6.552) still used a relatively heavy arm wall; crossing it with 4743fc24d9d8 (6.79) and switching to lighter pa12_cf bets that a thinner-walled, lighter-material arm can cut mass further, at the risk of insufficient stiffness.
methodSwitched material from cf_plate to pa12_cf, thinned the arm wall to 4.0 mm, pulled rear sweep in (35.6°→34.0°), and widened the plate slightly (×1.06→×1.08).
resultInvalid: arm tip deflection failure in pa12_cf. A 4.0 mm wall was fine in stiffer cf_plate (as in the winning c9cf7c11f59f) but too flexible once the softer pa12_cf material was substituted in. Worth testing: raise the minimum arm wall thickness whenever material is set to pa12_cf, or restrict thin walls to cf_plate only.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 3 via crossover
hypothesis4743fc24d9d8 (6.79) and cb6b6c5540f9 (6.719) were both mid-pack; crossing them and shifting to pa12_cf bets that a lighter material with narrower arms can undercut both parents, at the risk of insufficient plate thickness for the softer material.
methodSwitched material toward pa12_cf, narrowed arm width (×0.88→×0.80), trimmed plate width slightly (×1.00→×0.97), and tightened the arm waist taper marginally.
resultInvalid: plates measured 1.4 mm against pa12_cf's 1.6 mm minimum. The ×0.70 plate thickness multiplier, inherited from a cf_plate-tuned parent, wasn't compensated for the material's stricter minimum. Worth testing: auto-floor the plate thickness multiplier near ×0.85+ whenever a cross switches material to pa12_cf.

borngeneration 2 via crossover
hypothesisWith 567a47539bb8 sitting at the old best-so-far, the bet was that trimming its arm length slightly and cross-pollinating with a slightly weaker parent could nudge the champion lineage even lower.
methodArm length was pulled back to about 1.28x from the champion's 1.35x, with width, waist, and plate thickness left nearly unchanged.
resultCame in at 6.79 Wh/km — better than the original 6.807 best-so-far but not a new record, since cb6b6c5540f9 had already claimed 6.719 earlier this generation. Trimming arm length under-shot the champion's sweet spot; worth re-testing this exact recipe against the newer, thinner-armed leader instead of the original champion.

borngeneration 1 via crossover
hypothesisThe reigning best frame 7792e42599f3 (6.886) was efficient but plate-heavy, while fb21f27a5a37 (7.288) ran leaner overall; the bet was that borrowing fb21's lighter proportions plus a lighter layup could shave mass without losing stiffness.
methodThinned the plate by 30%, shortened plate length by 6%, stretched the arms 35% longer, and switched to a lighter carbon-fiber weave.
resultA clear win at 6.807 Wh/km — a NEW WINNER, beating the prior best-so-far of 6.886 and both parents outright. The combined mass savings paid off across every scenario, notably storm (5.23), without costing stability. Worth testing next: push plate thickness further toward the structural floor to see if more savings remain before the web-thickness limit bites.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 1 via crossover
hypothesisSame pairing logic as sibling 567a47539bb8 — lend fb21f27a5a37's leaner arm proportions to champion 7792e42599f3 — but this variant kept the plate slightly thicker instead of shedding mass there.
methodShortened arms by 19% (versus 24% in the sibling), thickened the plate by 6% instead of thinning it, and trimmed the arm waist a bit further.
resultValid at 6.828 Wh/km, a modest gain over both parents but not the new best-so-far — sibling 567a47539bb8 already claimed that spot at 6.807 this same generation. The extra plate mass likely cost it the edge. Worth testing: retry this arm-length trim paired with the sibling's thinner plate to see if the two changes stack additively.

borngeneration 2 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing 155527aa42e6 and 7792e42599f3 bet that flattening the battery wedge closer to level and trimming plate length slightly could cut trim drag in headwind scenarios.
methodBattery wedge reduced to about 1.0° from 3.2°, plate length trimmed to about 98%, and arm waist tightened slightly to 91%.
result6.868 Wh/km — valid but worse than both parents and well off the generation's new best of 6.552. Flattening the wedge cost more in balance/trim than it saved in drag, most visible in cold_headwind and storm; worth testing an intermediate wedge angle rather than swinging all the way to level.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

frame mass174.9 g
materialpet_cf
borngeneration 2 via crossover
hypothesisParent 147c162c7115 was a weak performer, so this self-cross was a long-shot bet that a lighter alternative material and a steeper battery wedge could rescue it into contention.
methodSelf-crossover shifted the material blend toward pet_cf (45%, up from 0%) and steepened the battery wedge to 15° from 13.4°, leaving the rest of the geometry unchanged.
result7.622 Wh/km — an improvement over the 7.97 parent but still far off the field, let alone the new 6.552 best-so-far. The underlying arm/plate proportions were the real liability, not the material or wedge; this lineage probably isn't worth further crossing unless paired with a genuinely more competitive geometry parent.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

frame mass167.9 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 2 via immigrant
hypothesisA from-scratch immigrant probing a chunkier, pinched-waist arm with a much bigger deck gap, betting that a heavier-duty arm root could survive worse gusts even without inherited genes.
methodRandom genome: arms 6% shorter and 6% wider with the waist pinched to 65%, thickened to 8.3 mm, paired with a stiffer CF plate and a wide deck gap.
resultInvalid — the arm tongue bolts miss the main plate, a mismatch between arm-root width and the plate's bolt pattern. This defect recurs elsewhere this generation, suggesting arm width and plate width/length need to be sampled jointly for immigrants rather than independently.

borngeneration 2 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing 567a47539bb8 (near the old champion) with 7792e42599f3 bet that beefing up the arms with extra material margin could pay for itself in robustness.
methodArm thickness increased to 7.0 mm from 6.0 mm, plate width trimmed about 7%, material blend nudged up, and plate thickness increased slightly.
resultInvalid — the plate web ended up too thin (0.78 mm vs. the 1.90 mm stock minimum), a byproduct of narrowing plate width without compensating thickness enough. This exact failure recurs multiple times this generation; future crosses of these parents should hold plate width fixed whenever thickness is also reduced.

statusinvalid — rotor disks overlap (tip clearance)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass168.1 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 2 via crossover
hypothesisParent 155527aa42e6 had decent form; this self-cross bet on a much beefier arm and shorter overall arm length to shift mass inward and cut drag.
methodSelf-crossover thickened the arms to 8.6 mm from 6.2 mm, shortened arm length to about 83%, and widened the plate slightly with a tighter deck gap.
resultInvalid — shortening the arms while props stayed fixed caused the rotor disks to overlap, violating tip clearance. This marks a hard floor on arm-length reduction that's set by prop diameter; any future shrink needs a joint check against rotor clearance before committing to simulation.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 2 via crossover
hypothesisCrossing the weak 147c162c7115 with the stronger 9f8cb660ef38 bet that widening the arms while narrowing the plate could rescue the weaker parent's contribution.
methodArm width increased to about 1.30x from 1.10x, plate width and length both trimmed roughly 5-6%, and arm thickness reduced to 6.3 mm.
resultInvalid — the plate web came out too thin again (0.85 mm vs. 1.90 mm stock), the same failure mode as 525912aa347b. Narrowing the plate footprint while bumping the thickness multiplier isn't enough to protect the web when arm-root cutouts eat into it; plate width and thickness likely need a coupled constraint check before simulation.

borngeneration 2 via immigrant
hypothesisA fresh immigrant testing a compact deck layout with wide, pinched-waist arms and a lightweight ASA plate, betting a tighter, lighter frame could undercut the leaders on mass alone.
methodRandom genome: arms 7% longer and 30% wider with the waist pinched to 92%, thickened to 5.3 mm, with the deck gap shrunk to 21.6 mm and material switched to ASA.
resultInvalid — deck gap too small for the FC/ESC stack, a packaging constraint rather than an aerodynamic one. Deck gap needs a hard floor tied to electronics stack height regardless of how promising the aero genes look; future immigrants should sample it from a range respecting that floor.

hypothesisCrossing two mid-pack performers, 155527aa42e6 and 9f8cb660ef38, bet that a narrower plate and slightly steeper front sweep could trim drag without disturbing much else.
methodPlate width trimmed to about 94%, front sweep increased slightly to 32.1° from 31.5°, and deck gap opened marginally to 30.3 mm.
resultInvalid — plate web too thin (0.84 mm vs. 1.90 mm stock), the third occurrence of this exact failure this generation. Narrowing plate width appears to be the common thread across all three plate-web invalids; reductions below roughly 95% should be flagged unless plate thickness increases in tandem.

borngeneration 2 via crossover
hypothesisSelf-cross of 8e06d4306a70 bet that a shallower front sweep angle could shave frontal drag while keeping the rest of the proven geometry intact.
methodFront sweep reduced to 30.0° from 31.5° with arm length trimmed marginally to about 1.17x.
resultInvalid — arm root tongues collide on the main plate, a new failure mode distinct from the tongue-miss-bolt issue seen elsewhere. Even a small sweep change rotated the arm roots into collision on this already-tight plate footprint, showing this lineage sits close to a geometric boundary; future tweaks should widen the plate or deck gap before touching sweep angle again.

borngeneration 2 via immigrant
hypothesisA fresh immigrant testing a lightweight pet_cf build with a pinched, thin arm profile, betting that shaving material broadly across arms and plate could undercut the field on power draw.
methodRandom genome: arms roughly unchanged in length but narrowed to 81% width and 79% waist, thickened to 8.4 mm, with thinner plates (0.77x) in pet_cf material.
resultInvalid — plates too thin for pet_cf (1.5 mm vs. the 1.6 mm minimum for that material). This is a material-specific constraint: pet_cf needs a higher minimum plate thickness than cf_plate, so future immigrants sampling pet_cf need a higher thickness floor baked into the sampler.

metricvalue
energy score6.886 Wh/km
scenario mean4.243 Wh/km
worst scenario5.287 Wh/km
frame mass139.9 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 0 via designer
hypothesisReacting to the failure of scaling everything up, the designer's next bet is the opposite: keep the long arm for prop clearance but slim its width and waist while leaving plate, deck gap, and thickness at safe stock values to avoid any structural violation.
methodArm lengthened 25% like the previous designer try, but width cut to 85% and waist slimmed sharply to 60%, thickness left at stock 6.0mm; sweep, deck gap, and plate thickness all held at stock values, battery wedge flattened to 0°, plate length up a modest 5%, in carbon-fiber plate.
resultNEW WINNER at 6.886 Wh/km, edging out the previous best (6.936) in every single scenario, including storm (5.29 vs 5.32). The long arm evidently helps stability/efficiency while the slimmer width and waist cut mass without breaching any minimum-material constraint - the first sign that 'lean, not scaled' is the right direction. Next idea: push the waist or width slimming a bit further still, watching closely for the plate-web-too-thin failure mode seen in several seeds.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

frame mass142.1 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 1 via crossover
hypothesisbaac55fb3837 (6.936) needed a small refinement; paired with an unevaluated candidate to explore whether a narrower arm width and slight battery wedge tweak could trim a bit more drag.
methodNarrowed the arm width by 11%, tightened the arm waist by 7%, added a small battery wedge, and switched to a lighter carbon-fiber material.
resultValid at 6.901 Wh/km, a small improvement over its known parent baac55fb3837 (6.936) but well short of the generation's new best-so-far of 6.807. The arm-width trim helped modestly across every scenario without a standout win anywhere. Worth testing: apply the same arm-width and waist trim to the new champion lineage (567a47539bb8) rather than the older baac55fb3837 line.

metricvalue
energy score6.908 Wh/km
scenario mean4.257 Wh/km
worst scenario5.301 Wh/km
frame mass138.6 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 0 via designer
hypothesisBuilding on the winning slim-arm insight, the designer asks whether the width/waist slimming alone (without lengthening the arm) captures most of the benefit.
methodArm length left at stock, width and waist both trimmed to 80%, thickness at 6.5mm; sweep held at 34.0°/39.0°, plate and deck gap left at stock, battery wedge steepened to 10°, carbon-fiber plate kept.
resultValid at 6.908 Wh/km - better than the original stock baseline (6.936) in every scenario, but just short of the reigning best-so-far (6.886), so it does not take the lead. This confirms width/waist slimming is the dominant efficiency lever, though the current champion's added arm length still edges it out slightly, suggesting length and slimming compound rather than substitute. Next idea: combine this candidate's width/waist trim with the champion's longer arm to see if the two effects stack.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

frame mass182.3 g
materialpa12_cf
borngeneration 1 via crossover
hypothesisBoth parents were weak (89ffb906b148 at 8.479, 9be55419299a at 7.598) but each carried heavy-duty structural traits; the bet was that a lighter material swap could recover efficiency from their combined bulk.
methodThinned the plate slightly, trimmed plate length by 2%, and switched to a lighter pa12_cf material while keeping the arms fairly thick at 9mm.
resultValid at 7.727 Wh/km, a solid improvement over both parents but nowhere near the 6.807 best-so-far. The material swap clearly helped, but the still-thick arms and oversized plate width kept overall mass too high. Worth testing: keep the material swap but shrink arm thickness toward 6-7mm to see how much further this lineage can improve.

borngeneration 1 via crossover
hypothesisac6152897717 (7.761) was underperforming broadly; paired with an unevaluated candidate to test whether a wider plate and steeper battery wedge could offset its weaknesses.
methodWidened the plate by 11%, increased the battery wedge to 13.3 degrees, swept the rear arms back further, and shifted toward a heavier material blend.
resultValid at 7.765 Wh/km, essentially unchanged from its weak parent (7.761) and far off the 6.807 best-so-far. None of the changes moved the needle — the frame still carries too much structural mass from the wide plate and thick arms. Worth testing: abandon this heavy-plate direction and instead cross ac6152897717 with the new champion lineage for a bigger structural shake-up.

borngeneration 1 via crossover
hypothesisBoth parents (ac6152897717 at 7.761, fb21f27a5a37 at 7.288) were already underperformers; the bet was that combining ac6152897717's stouter arms with fb21's proportions might uncover a hidden sweet spot.
methodWidened the arm waist toward round, increased the battery wedge slightly, thickened the arms marginally, and widened the plate a touch.
resultValid but worse at 7.97 Wh/km, underperforming both parents and far off the best-so-far. Combining two already-weak designs compounded their weaknesses instead of cancelling them, especially visible in storm (6.13) and cold headwind (5.18). Worth testing: stop breeding from ac6152897717 unless paired with a much stronger second parent.

statusinvalid — arm tongue bolts miss the main plate
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass150.8 g
materialpet_cf
borngeneration 1 via crossover
hypothesisParent fb21f27a5a37 (7.288) ran a wide, draggy arm waist while baac55fb3837 (6.936) was leaner and more compact; the bet was that combining baac's tighter waist with fb21's rear sweep would cut drag further still.
methodSlimmed the arm waist well beyond either parent, swept the rear arms back further, trimmed plate length slightly, and opened the deck gap by a few millimeters.
resultInvalid — the arm tongue bolts no longer land on the main plate, a bolt-pattern miss caused by combining the aggressive waist thinning with the increased rear sweep. This is one of two candidates this generation to hit the exact same failure, pointing to a real design-space wall. Worth testing: cap rear sweep near 45° whenever arm waist drops below about 0.65x to keep the bolt pattern on the plate.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

materialcf_plate
borngeneration 1 via crossover
hypothesis7792e42599f3 (6.886) had margin in its deck clearance, while 59b9a52c15fa (7.127) ran a tighter deck stack; the bet was that pulling the deck gap down would shrink frontal area and drag.
methodShrank the deck gap by roughly 5.5mm, shortened the arms slightly, and nudged arm waist and material blend a touch thinner.
resultInvalid — the tighter deck gap pushed the rotor disk too close to the deck/battery stack. This is the generation's second clearance-driven failure, suggesting a deck gap below about 25mm isn't survivable at this arm length. Worth testing: pair any deck-gap reduction with a compensating arm-length increase to preserve rotor clearance.

borngeneration 1 via crossover
hypothesis9f8cb660ef38 (6.908) performed well on a lighter material blend, while ac6152897717 (7.761) had struggled; the bet was that a heavier composite plus wider battery wedge might buy stability margin in gusty conditions.
methodShifted the material blend much heavier, thinned the arm from 6.5mm to 5.2mm, widened the front sweep, and opened the arm waist toward round.
resultInvalid — the plate web came out too thin (1.25mm vs a 1.75mm stock minimum), a structural failure from thinning the arm while also shortening the plate. This shows arm-thickness and plate-length changes need a coupled constraint check. Worth testing: hold plate length near parity whenever arm thickness drops below 6mm.

borngeneration 1 via crossover
hypothesisA self-cross of 9f8cb660ef38 (6.908) probing its own neighborhood, testing whether trimming plate length and lightening the battery wedge could squeeze more efficiency from an already-strong design.
methodShortened the plate by 15%, reduced battery wedge from 10 to 5.8 degrees, lengthened the arms 16%, and switched to a lighter carbon-fiber weave.
resultInvalid — the plate web thinned to 1.11mm against a 1.47mm stock minimum, another structural miss driven by shortening the plate while lengthening the arms. Together with candidate 8647cb40064a's failure, plate-length reduction looks like the common thread behind this generation's web-thickness violations. Worth testing: hold plate length fixed and vary only arm length or material to isolate which change actually drives the efficiency gain.

frame mass112.0 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 1 via crossover
hypothesisA self-cross of baac55fb3837 (6.936) testing whether shortening the arms and opening the deck gap could cut frontal drag from an already solid mid-pack frame.
methodShortened the arms by 20%, thinned them from 6mm to 4.9mm, opened the deck gap by roughly 4.4mm, and tightened the arm waist slightly.
resultInvalid — the shorter, thinner arms caused the rotor disks to overlap in tip clearance. This is the fourth invalid candidate this generation and the second driven by arm shortening, suggesting arm length below about 0.85x needs a paired widening of plate width to keep rotor centers apart. Worth testing: shorten arms only alongside a wider plate to preserve rotor clearance.

borngeneration 1 via immigrant
hypothesisA random immigrant injected to keep genetic diversity up, exploring a fresh region of the design space unrelated to the current champion lineage.
methodSet arm proportions from scratch with a wider arm width and waist, a steeply swept rear arm, and a lighter pet_cf material.
resultInvalid — arm tongue bolts miss the main plate, the same bolt-alignment failure seen in candidate c7930fc5ffc6. Two independent lineages (a crossover and a fresh immigrant) hitting this exact constraint suggests wide waist plus aggressive rear sweep is a structural dead end regardless of ancestry. Worth testing: add an explicit bolt-pattern-vs-sweep check to the pre-flight validator so future immigrants don't burn slots on it.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — arm resonance with rotor 1P
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass162.3 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 1 via crossover
hypothesisPairing two of the generation's strongest parents — 9f8cb660ef38 (6.908) and reigning champion 7792e42599f3 (6.886) — betting that thicker arms would add stiffness headroom for a longer, more efficient plate.
methodThickened the arms from 6.5mm to 8.3mm, lengthened the plate by 11%, and shortened the arms by 6%.
resultInvalid — arm resonance with rotor 1P, a vibration failure from the thicker, shorter arm shifting its natural frequency into the rotor's first-harmonic band. This shows arm thickness and length are coupled through resonance, not just mass, even when otherwise structurally sound. Worth testing: check arm natural frequency against 1P before increasing thickness further on shortened arms.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

hypothesisA self-cross of 59b9a52c15fa (7.127) probing whether widening the plate and reducing front sweep could recover some of its lost efficiency.

metricvalue
energy score6.936 Wh/km
scenario mean4.274 Wh/km
worst scenario5.325 Wh/km
frame mass144.3 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 0 via seed
hypothesisAs the very first frame in the study, this candidate has no history to react to; its role is simply to fly the unmodified Source One V6 geometry and establish the reference point every later mutation will be judged against.
methodEvery gene left at its stock value: default arm length, width, waist, and 6.0mm thickness, stock 31.4°/36.0° sweep, stock plate footprint and 30mm deck gap, a mild 2.0° battery wedge, and the stock carbon-fiber plate material.
resultWith nothing to compare it to yet, it trivially becomes the first best-so-far at 6.936 Wh/km, with storm predictably the worst scenario at 5.32. It's a solid, unremarkable control build. Worth testing next: nudge single genes (thickness, width) off this baseline one at a time to map which levers actually move the needle before committing to compound mutations.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

materialpetg
borngeneration 0 via designer
hypothesisThe designer tries the opposite length bet from its winning sibling, wondering if a shorter but wider, thicker arm on a low-cost PETG frame could also trim energy use.
methodArm shortened to 90% of stock length but width up 20% and waist widened 10%, thickness raised to 8.0mm; sweep nudged to 34.0°/39.0°, deck gap widened slightly to 32mm, plate thickness up 10%, switched to PETG.
resultValid at 7.127 Wh/km, worse than both the stock baseline and the new best-so-far in every scenario. Shortening the arm didn't pay off once paired with a wider, thicker cross-section and a heavier PETG build - the mass increase dominates any drag savings from the shorter length. Next idea: retest a shortened arm but paired with the slim width/waist recipe from the current winner instead of thickening it.

borngeneration 0 via seed
hypothesisThis seed bets on a moderately scaled-up frame with a wider plate and heavier plate stock, hoping the extra structure pays for itself without breaking any constraints.
methodArms lengthened 23% and thickened to 6.5mm, width up 13% with waist trimmed to 80%; sweep raised to 34.8°/44.2°; plate stretched 7% longer and 23% wider, deck gap widened to 31.5mm, battery wedge nearly flat at 0.2°, plate thickness up 36%, in PET-CF.
resultIt came in valid at 7.288 Wh/km, worse than the stock baseline across every scenario (storm 5.60 vs 5.32). The wider, thicker build again added more mass/drag than it saved, landing well short of best-so-far. Next idea: try the same sweep and deck-gap changes but without the 23% plate-width and 36% thickness increases, to isolate whether sweep alone helps.

borngeneration 0 via designer
hypothesisAs a final check on the opposite extreme, the designer bets that maximizing width, waist, and thickness together for a very stiff ASA build might reduce vibration-driven losses enough to offset the added mass.
methodArm length up 10%, width up 30% (the widest tried), waist widened 20%, thickness raised to 9.0mm (the thickest); plate grew 10%/5% and thickened 40%, deck gap widened to 35mm, battery wedge flattened to 0°, switched to ASA.
resultValid at 7.598 Wh/km, the worst of the designer's candidates and well behind best-so-far in every scenario (storm 5.85). This closes out the generation confirming that mass dominates the energy budget - no over-built, thickened variant has beaten the lean, slim-waisted design. Next idea: focus the next generation's mutations tightly around the current champion's genome (long arm, slim width/waist, stock thickness) rather than exploring more mass-adding variants.
