scenario mean4.778 Wh/km
worst scenario5.966 Wh/km
frame mass196.5 g
materialcf_plate
borngeneration 0 via seed
hypothesisWith no prior generation to learn from, this seed bets that a longer, heavier-duty arm and a much stiffer plate will buy enough stability margin to be worth the extra mass.
methodArms stretched 26% longer and thickened to 6.8mm, width up 10% but the waist pinched 18% slimmer; both sweep angles pushed out to roughly 40°; the plate grew 5% in each dimension and was thickened 42%, sitting on a steeper 10.5° battery wedge.
resultIt landed at 7.761 Wh/km, worse than the stock baseline in every single scenario, worst in storm at 5.97. The added length and heavily reinforced plate clearly cost more in power draw than they returned in robustness, so it stays well behind best-so-far. Next idea: try the longer arm alone, without the 42% plate thickening, to see how much of the penalty came from the plate versus the arm.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

materialcf_plate
borngeneration 0 via designer
hypothesisAs the designer's opening move, it plays it safe on sweep and deck gap (keeping them at stock) while betting that scaling every structural dimension up uniformly would buy stability without tripping any constraint.
methodArm length, width, and waist all scaled up 10-25%, thickness raised to 8.5mm; sweep and deck gap left at stock; plate grew 10% in each dimension and thickened 40%, on a modest 5° battery wedge, in carbon-fiber plate.
resultValid but the worst-performing candidate in the generation at 8.479 Wh/km, trailing baseline in every scenario (storm 6.53). Playing it safe on sweep/deck-gap avoided invalidation, but uniformly overscaling every structural gene piled on mass that the fitness metric punishes directly. Next idea: the designer should treat 'scale everything up' as a dead end and instead try scaling dimensions in different directions (some up, some down) as the next candidate does.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — arm tongue bolts miss the main plate
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass95.0 g
materialpetg
borngeneration 0 via seed
hypothesisThis seed explores the aggressive end of the drag-reduction space, betting that a sharply swept-back rear arm and a slender arm waist could cut weight and frontal area.
methodWidth grew 18% while the waist was slimmed sharply to 56% of stock and thickness dropped to 4.5mm; rear sweep was pushed way out to 60.2°, plate thickness bumped 21%, and the frame switched to PETG.
resultInvalid: the arm tongue's mounting bolts miss the main plate entirely. The extreme rear sweep combined with the slim waist moved the arm root far enough that it no longer lines up with the fixed bolt pattern. Lesson: rear sweep and waist ratio need to be co-constrained so the tongue geometry stays registered to the plate. Worth testing next: cap rear sweep well below 60° whenever waist ratio drops much under 0.8.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

materialasa
borngeneration 0 via seed
hypothesisThis seed chases a heavily reinforced, oversized frame, betting that thicker arms and a tougher ASA plate would improve durability margins even at some weight cost.
methodArms lengthened 31% and thickened to 8.5mm, width up 23%; front sweep pushed to 40°; plate footprint shrank slightly (×0.92/×0.87) while deck gap tightened to 21.7mm and plate thickness rose 52%, in ASA.
resultInvalid: the 21.7mm deck gap leaves no room for the FC/ESC stack. Shrinking the plate footprint while thickening the plate itself squeezed the internal deck space below what the electronics need. Lesson: deck gap has a hard floor that must be checked independently of plate size/thickness trade-offs. Next idea: keep deck gap pinned above ~28mm whenever plate footprint is shrunk below stock.

materialasa
borngeneration 0 via seed
hypothesisThis seed tests a compact, high-sweep frame, betting that shorter/narrower arms and pronounced fore-and-aft sweep would cut mass and drag.
methodArms shortened to 78% of stock length and narrowed to 81% width, but waist widened 21%, with sweep pushed to 51.3° front and 45.0° rear; plate grew modestly (×1.04/×1.13), deck gap tightened to 21.5mm, and material switched to ASA.
resultInvalid: same failure mode as the previous candidate — deck gap of 21.5mm is too small for the FC/ESC stack. The combination of compact arms with an enlarged plate again ate into the internal deck clearance. This reinforces that deck gap needs its own explicit floor in the mutation rules. Next idea: treat deck gap as a protected gene that mutation operators can't push below a safe minimum regardless of other changes.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — plate web too thin (plate_main 0.66 mm, stock 1.90 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass107.6 g
materialpa12_cf
borngeneration 0 via seed
hypothesisThis seed bets that a shorter, wider arm paired with a lightweight PA12-CF material could shave weight while a thickened plate covers the structural risk.
methodArm length trimmed to 87%, width up 28%, waist widened 20%, thickness reduced to 5.2mm; sweep raised to 33.3°/44.8°; plate width trimmed 7%, plate thickness nominally raised 33%, material switched to PA12-CF.
resultInvalid: the resulting plate web is only 0.66mm, far under the 1.90mm stock minimum. Even though the thickness multiplier looks like an increase, the narrower plate footprint combined with the softer PA12-CF baseline left too little material once mounting cutouts were accounted for. Lesson: a positive plate-thickness multiplier alone doesn't guarantee a safe web — footprint shrinkage can undercut it. Next idea: check computed web thickness directly against the material's minimum rather than trusting the multiplier.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

borngeneration 0 via seed
hypothesisThis seed pushes toward an aggressively lightweight frame, betting that a longer sweep-heavy arm on a thinned ASA plate would still hold together.
methodWidth up 29%, waist widened 13%, thickness cut to 4.6mm; both sweep angles raised toward 48-50°; plate lengthened 17% but plate thickness actually cut to 81% of stock, in ASA.
resultInvalid: plate web comes out at just 0.59mm against the 1.90mm stock requirement. Cutting plate thickness below stock while also stretching the plate's length was always going to starve the web. Lesson: any plate-thickness multiplier under 1.0 is high-risk and should be flagged before simulation. Next idea: exclude sub-1.0 plate-thickness multipliers from future seeds unless paired with a footprint shrink, not a stretch.

materialpet_cf
borngeneration 0 via seed
hypothesisThis seed tests a near-stock plate thickness combined with a thick, stubby-waisted arm, betting the arm reinforcement alone would be enough margin.
methodArm length and width left close to stock, waist narrowed to 76%, thickness boosted to 8.9mm; sweep raised to 38.6°/41.9°; plate kept close to stock size with thickness nudged just 2% above stock, in PET-CF.
resultInvalid: plate web measures 1.06mm, still well under the 1.90mm minimum despite the thickness multiplier being barely above 1.0. This shows the margin needed is larger than a 2% bump can cover once the waist and sweep changes are factored in. Lesson: plate web sensitivity to waist/sweep combinations is stronger than expected, even when plate genes are almost untouched. Next idea: run a quick web-thickness estimate before committing a seed whenever waist ratio drops under ~0.8.

metricvalue
statusinvalid — plate web too thin (plate_main 1.48 mm, stock 1.90 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass101.1 g
materialasa
borngeneration 0 via seed
hypothesisThis seed explores a long, narrow-plate frame with high sweep, betting that shrinking plate width while keeping decent plate thickness would be safe.
methodArm lengthened 27% but width cut to 78%, waist widened 7%, thickness 5.4mm; sweep pushed to 42.9°/52.1°; plate width cut to 86% of stock while thickness rose 30%, in ASA.
resultInvalid: plate web still only 1.48mm versus the 1.90mm floor, despite a 30% thickness increase. The 14% narrower plate footprint ate more margin than the thickness gain provided. Lesson: narrowing plate width is a bigger risk to web integrity than plate-thickness multipliers can offset. Next idea: keep plate width multiplier close to or above 1.0 whenever plate thickness is also being pushed up, rather than trading one against the other.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5

statusinvalid — plate web too thin (plate_main 0.32 mm, stock 1.90 mm)
energy score∞ (never flew the scenarios)
frame mass137.5 g
materialpa12_cf
borngeneration 0 via seed
hypothesisThis seed bets on the most extreme sweep-and-waist combination yet, hoping a lighter-weight PA12-CF build with only mild plate thickening would still clear constraints.
methodArm length up 12%, width up 26%, waist widened 14%, thickness 6.6mm; sweep pushed hardest of all seeds to 44.2°/54.3°; deck gap widened to 43.4mm, plate thickness raised just 6%, in PA12-CF.
resultInvalid: plate web collapses to just 0.32mm, the thinnest of all the failures, against the 1.90mm requirement. The combination of high sweep, widened waist, and only a token 6% plate-thickness bump left almost no material margin. Lesson: extreme sweep angles compound with waist widening to demand much larger plate-thickness margins than a small percentage increase provides. Next idea: whenever sweep exceeds ~45°, require plate thickness multiplier well above 1.2 as a baseline safety margin.
notes by claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, claude-sonnet-5
